For instance, a necessary condition of stability of the structure of manganese dioxides is that vacant sites in $\square \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ (where $\square$ is a crystallographic empty site) should be linked as 3D tunnels or channels to accommodate $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$-ions motion.

The purpose of this chapter is to summarize the current status of materials with 3D-tunnel structure that have a cubic-close packed (ccp) array. These materials belong to the class of 3- and 4-V cathodes. Non-lithiated $\square M_{x} \mathrm{O}_{y}$ and lithiated $\mathrm{Li} M_{x} \mathrm{O}_{y}$ lattices are considered. The first group includes the different polymorphs of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}, \mathrm{WO}_{3}$, and its derivatives, while the second class includes $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$, $\mathrm{Li}_{0.33} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel, and its derivatives.

### 6.2 Manganese Dioxides

Manganese dioxide, $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, (MDO) is a widely used as material in primary electrochemical cells. It is the positive electrode of the zinc- $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ cell, invented in 1866 by the French engineer Georges-Lionel Leclanché [11]. Li-MnO ${ }_{2}$ battery was developed by Sanyo in 1975 [12] as low-power supplies for watches, calculators and memory backups. The poor cyclability of MDOs observed at that time [13] has been improved by preparing composite dimensional manganese oxides (CDMOs) for the development of flat-type secondary batteries. Table 6.2 lists the chemical formula of selected $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ compounds used in Li batteries.

### 6.2.1 $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$

Manganese oxides with tunnel and layered crystal structures constitute a large family of porous materials [14]. Manganese dioxide can exist in different crystal structures, including $\alpha-, \beta-, \gamma-, \varepsilon-, \eta-, \delta-$, and $\lambda-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, etc., where the basic structural unit [ $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ ] is linked in different manners [15-17]. Most of the frameworks are built by $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedral units shared by corners and/or edges, which define structural voids for cation insertion. According to different octahedron links, the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ structures can be divided into three categories: the chain-like tunnel structure such as $\alpha-, \beta$-, and $\gamma$-crystalline form; the sheet-like or layered structure such as $\delta$-type $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$; and the third category that is composed of three-dimensional structures such as $\lambda-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. The different crystalline structures of manganese dioxide exhibit different properties and life cycles [18, 19]. In addition to the crystal structure, the size and morphologies of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ particles also play a key role in determining the properties for practical applications. In this regard, many efforts have been made to prepare nanocrystalline $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ with different structures

Table 6.2 Designation and chemical formula of various $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ compounds
| Designation | Compound | Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Birnessite | $\mathrm{MnO}_{1.86} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\right]_{0.84}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\right]_{0.16} \mathrm{O}_{1.84} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| Na-birnessite | $\mathrm{Na}_{0.32} \mathrm{MnO}_{2} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\mathrm{Na}_{0.32}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\right]_{0.68}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\right]_{0.32} \mathrm{O}_{2} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| Li-birnessite | $\mathrm{Li}_{0.32} \mathrm{MnO}_{2} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\mathrm{Li}_{0.32}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\right]_{0.68}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\right]_{0.32} \mathrm{O}_{2} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| Co-birnessite | $\mathrm{Mn}_{0.85} \mathrm{Co}_{0.15} \mathrm{O}_{2} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ | $\left[\mathrm{Co}^{3+}\right]_{0.15}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}\right]_{0.72}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\right]_{0.13} \mathrm{O}_{1.80} \cdot 0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| Spinel | $\lambda-\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{4+} \mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ |
| NMD | Romanechite | $(\mathrm{R})_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{10} \cdot x \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| EMD | $\gamma-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{MnO}_{2} \cdot 0.16 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ |
| CDMO | $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$-based composite | $\gamma-\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$ |
| m-LMO | $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ | Monoclinic phase $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}\right] \mathrm{O}_{2}$ |
| LT-LMO | $\mathrm{Li}_{0.52} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ | Spinel-contained phase |
| HT-LMO | $\mathrm{Li}_{0.52} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ | Layered phase |


and shapes. Up to now, various nanostructures of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, such as nanoparticles, nanorods, belts, wires, tubes, fibers, urchins/orchids, mesoporous and branched structures, have been synthesized by different methods [18].

The excellent electrochemical properties of several $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ phases are attracting attention for positive electrodes in lithium batteries [20-23]. There is a wide variety of battery-grade MDOs, whose structures have been extensively investigated [24]. Among the MDOs, most attention has been focused on synthetic products prepared by either electrolytic (EMD) or chemical (CMD) method that belong to the nsutite ( $\gamma-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ ) group [25]. The electrochemical behavior of EMD compounds has been investigated by Ohzuku et al. [26]. Besides the $\gamma-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ form, particular attention has been paid to other stoichiometric compounds. As an example, the sodium-free birnessite, $\mathrm{MnO}_{1.85}-0.6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, displays a layered structure with larger interlayer distance and trigonal prismatic sites favorable for easy lithium insertion [27]. The manganese dioxides can be classified according the nature of the polymerization of $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ units and the number of $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedral chains between two basal layers to form tunnel ( $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}}$ ) openings. The $\mathrm{T}_{1, \mathrm{n}}$ group includes two chemically pure forms, the pyrolusite $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{1,1}\right)$ and the ramsdellite $R-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{1,2}\right)$. The $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{m}, \infty}$ group includes the layered phyllomanganates such as birnessite, buserite, and rancieite. Figure 6.2 shows the schematic structure of various $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ polymorph showing the variation in the chain and tunnel ( $m \times n$ ) structures [28].

The discharge curves of different forms of manganese dioxide such as singlephase $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}, R-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, and the stabilized phase $\alpha / \beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ are shown in Fig. 6.3. These data show that the stabilized two-phase $\alpha / \beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ sample delivers higher discharge capacity than the single-phase $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. Furthermore ramsdellite $R-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and pyrolusite $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ display the highest discharge capacities. These materials present flat discharge curves while hollandite structure shows an S-shaped discharge curve. On the initial discharge the stabilized $\alpha / \beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ material delivers a specific capacity $230 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. This electrode shows good recharge ability with capacity retention $150 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 20 cycles. The initial capacity loss of $33 \%$ suggests that $\approx 0.3 \mathrm{~mol}$ of inserted lithium ions is used [27].

### 6.2.2 $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$-Based Composites

Nohma et al. [29] have shown that, if $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ contained a small amount of lithium in its crystal structure beforehand, the electrochemical reversibility would be improved. Several composite dimensional manganese oxides (CDMOs) were prepared by reacting LiOH with $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. The XRD patterns of the product prepared from various $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$ atomic ratios are shown in Fig. 6.4.

From these results, the structural model of heat-treated $\mathrm{LiOH} \cdot \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ precursor at $375^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is considered to be a composite formed by the mixture of the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$ and $\gamma-\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ phases. $\gamma-\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ phases has one-dimensional channels while $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-164.jpg?height=1056&width=1180&top_left_y=203&top_left_x=171)
Fig. 6.2 Schematic view of the tunnel structure of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ polymorph (a) pyrolusite $\beta-\mathrm{MnO} 2\left(\mathrm{~T}_{1,1}\right)$ and (b) ramsdellite $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{1,2}\right)$, hollandite $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{2,2}\right)$ and (c) nsutite $\gamma-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ (intergrowth $\mathrm{T}_{1,1}+\mathrm{T}_{1,2}$ )

Fig. 6.3 Discharge curves of single-phase manganese dioxides: $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, $R-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, and the stabilized phase $\alpha / \beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-164.jpg?height=573&width=762&top_left_y=1499&top_left_x=582)

Fig. 6.4 X-ray diffraction patterns of CDMO at several $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$ atomic ratios. The $\mathrm{LiOH} \cdot \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ precursor was heated at $375^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-165.jpg?height=1146&width=703&top_left_y=205&top_left_x=646)

Fig. 6.5 Schematic view of the CDMO structure formed by the composite of $\gamma-\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$
is a layered compound, namely $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Li}_{1 / 3} \mathrm{Mn}_{2 / 3}\right] \mathrm{O}_{2}$ (Fig. 6.5). As a result, the CDMO prepared at $375^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with a $\mathrm{Li} /(\mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{Mn})=0.3$ yielded the largest discharge capacity $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for electrode cycled in the potential range $3.6-2.0 \mathrm{~V}$ at current density $1.1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$ [30].

### 6.2.3 $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ Nanorods

Nano-sized $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ samples with the shape of nanorod were prepared by hydrothermal reaction between ammonium persulfate and Mn (II) salts [31]. XRD measurements revealed the $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ phase for the sample prepared from manganese sulfate and the $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ structure for the sample prepared from manganese nitrate

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-166.jpg?height=516&width=1181&top_left_y=219&top_left_x=173)
Fig. 6.6 (a) XRD patterns of $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ nanorods prepared through redox reaction between $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}$ and $\mathrm{MnSO}_{4} \cdot 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, and through redox reaction between $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}$ and Mn $\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \cdot 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, respectively (b) TEM images of $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ nanorods. Values of diameter and length of nanorods are in nanometer

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-166.jpg?height=493&width=1180&top_left_y=982&top_left_x=171)
Fig. 6.7 Cyclic voltamograms of (a) $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and (b) $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. Data were recorded in lithium cells using a scan rate $0.05 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ in the voltage range $1.5-4.0 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$. Redox potentials are indicated in volt

(Fig. 6.6). TGA analysis has confirmed the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ structure for both samples showing three weight losses without thermal stabilization due to the absence of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ions in the tunnels. It seems that the difference in the nature and size of tunnels between $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{2,2}\right)$ and $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{1,1}\right)$ affect the specific capacity. Nanorods structures exhibit good electrochemical performance, evidenced by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 6.7). There is a structural evolution after the first cycle. This can be observed clearly from the reduction in the potential polarization after the first cycle and also the reduction in capacity fading. Shao-Horn et al. [32] have demonstrated that crystals with a small aspect ratio have a large electrochemically active surface because of the large exposed $\mathrm{T}_{2,2}$-type tunnel cross-sectional area per unit
volume available for lithium insertion. $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ nanorods yield a specific discharge capacity of 180 and $130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the first and 45th cycle respectively, while $\alpha-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ nanorods deliver $210 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at first cycle that decreases to $115 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the 45th cycle.

The electrochemical behavior of $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ may be attributed to the nature of nanorods of this sample, which alleviates the stress and provides flexibility for lithium insertion/extraction in/from $1 \times 1$ tunnels [33]. The capacity loss observed after the first cycle is attributed to the small amount of lithium ions inserted in the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ electrode that cannot be easily removed from the structure. The second reason for this degradation in the capacity upon cycling may be related to significant amount of $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ ions formed at the end of discharge. The $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ ions can be dissolved into the electrolyte causing significant increase in the capacity fading of lithium manganese oxides [34].

### 6.2.4 Birnessite

One class of MDO polymorphs crystallizes in a layered structure (monoclinic, $C / 2 m$ S.G.), namely birnessite ( $\delta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ ) or phylomanganates [27,35]. The birnessite-type manganese oxides are found in nature (soils, ore deposits, marine nodules, etc.) and produced synthetically (hydrothermal, sol-gel, etc.). The lamellar framework is built of edge-sharing $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra with water molecules and/or metal cations occupying the interlayer region as shown in Fig. 6.8 [36].

Depending on the synthesis route, a combination of $\mathrm{Mn}($ IV $) / \mathrm{Mn}($ III $)$ or $\mathrm{Mn}($ IV $) / \mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{II})$ is found in birnessite (BR). For example, the mean oxidation state of manganese in sol-gel materials and classical BR (prepared from Stähli's method) varies in the range $3.6<Z_{\mathrm{Mn}}<3.8$. In the case of sol-gel birnessite (SG-BR) and Co-doped sol-gel birnessite (SGCo-BR) [37], we have a mixture of $\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{IV})$ and Mn

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-167.jpg?height=362&width=875&top_left_y=1513&top_left_x=323)
Fig. 6.8 Layered structure of sodium birnessite. (a) The interlayer distance $d=7.1 \AA$ corresponds to two consecutive superposable $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedral sheets. The trigonal prismatic sites are occupied by sodium ions. (b) Layered structure of alkali-free birnessite. The interlayer distance $d=7.25 \AA$ corresponds to two consecutive non-superposable $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedral sheets. The interlayer space defines trigonal antiprismatic and trigonal pyramidal sites

(II) while a combination of $\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{IV})$ and $\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{III})$ is found for the classical birnessites. These phases have a hexagonal or monoclinic symmetry. One of them is like the layered $\mathrm{CdI}_{2}$-type structure and consists in single sheets of edge-sharing $\left[\mathrm{MnO}_{6}\right]$ octahedra and water molecules between layers and $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ or $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ located between the water layer and oxygen of the [ $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ ] slab in order to counterbalance the charge defects in $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ sheets. The sequence along the c axis is:

$$
\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{\mathrm{IV}}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{\mathrm{III}(+) \text { or II }(*)}-\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{\mathrm{III}(+) \text { or II }(*)}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Mn}^{\mathrm{IV}}-\mathrm{O},
$$

where (+) denotes classical BR and (*) SG-BR compounds. The orthogonal distance between two consecutive slabs of $\left[\mathrm{MnO}_{6}\right]$ is around $7 \AA$. In the case of SGCoBR, we have previously shown that $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ ions are incorporated into the $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ sheets as $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ is substituting for Mn ions [38] (see chemical formula in Table 6.1).

The maximum Li uptake in SG-BR $x(\mathrm{Li})=0.85$ is in good agreement with the concentration of $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ ions (the average valence of Mn ions is $Z_{\mathrm{Mn}}=3.68$ ), which are reduced to $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ during the intercalation reaction (discharge process) that occurs with a significant contraction $7.5 \%$ of the host lattice. Pereira-Ramos et al. [39] reported that the faradaic yield recovered for SG-BRr is twofold greater than that of BR . Such a result can be understood in terms of faster $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$-ion kinetics, as its diffusion coefficient was estimated to be $D_{\mathrm{Li}}{ }^{*}=10^{-10}-10^{-11} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. In the potential range $4.2-2.0 \mathrm{~V}$, the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{SG}-\mathrm{BR}$ cells exhibit good cycling behavior with a specific capacity of $150 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the 50th cycle.

### 6.3 Lithiated Manganese Dioxides

The phase diagram of the Li-Mn-O system in air between 350 and $1060^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ according to Paulsen and Dahn [40] is shown in Fig. 6.9. Lithium doped singlephase spinels $\mathrm{Li}_{1+z} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-z} \mathrm{O}_{4}(0 \leq z \leq 0.33)$ are stable between 400 and $880^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (with an average Mn valence between 3.5 and 4). Above the upper critical temperature line, $T c_{1}$, the spinel coexists with the monoclinic $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$, and below the lower critical temperature line, $T c_{2}$, with $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ or $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. Most of the manganese oxides are confronted with two major difficulties: (1) lattice distortions occurring during the charge-discharge processes due to the presence of Jahn-Teller (JT) distortion associated with the single electron in the $e_{\mathrm{g}}$ orbitals of the $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ cation [6] and (2) manganese dissolution from the cathode framework into the electrolyte particularly at higher temperature and in the high voltage charge state due to the disproportionation reactions of $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ into $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ induced by acids generated by reactions of fluorinated anions with water impurities and by oxidation of solvents [41].

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-169.jpg?height=846&width=930&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=295)
Fig. 6.9 Phase diagram of the Li-Mn-O system in air between 350 and $1060^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

### 6.3.1 $\mathrm{Li}_{0.33} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$

Lithiated $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ with $x=0.33$ obtained by heat treatment of a mixture of $\mathrm{LiOH}- \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{LiNO}_{3}-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ at low temperature, has been studied extensively as a $3-\mathrm{V}$ cathode material in liquid-electrolyte lithium batteries. This compound delivered a rechargeable capacity of $180 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles with a flat potential of 2.9 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$[42, 43]. $\mathrm{Li}_{0.33} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ crystallizes in the monoclinic structure ( $C 2 / m$ S.G.). According to the structural considerations of Levi et al. [44] Li ions are located in the octahedral sites of ( $1 \times 2$ ) channels that produce weak bonding for the lithium cations in the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ lattice. Figure 6.10 shows the first discharge-charge curves for the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{0.33+x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ cells in the voltage range $4.0-1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$at a current density $0.14 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ [45]. Positive electrodes synthesized by solid state reaction of CMD and Li salt were tested at 25 and $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ showing a faradaic yield $0.62 \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$ achieved upon discharge, which leads to a specific capacity of $194 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. For the first discharge-charge process, the voltage profile occurs with an S-shape that indicates the formation of a single-phase $\mathrm{Li}_{0.33+x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. Since the reduction process of $\mathrm{Li}_{0.33} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ is reversible, the construction of rechargeable cells is possible. Satisfactory charge-discharge efficiency and storage capability are other favorable features of this cell. Upon lithiation of $\mathrm{Li}_{0.33+x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, the electronic conductivity increases slightly from $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ for $x=0$ to ca. $5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ for $x=0.55$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-170.jpg?height=414&width=1164&top_left_y=205&top_left_x=185)
Fig. 6.10 First discharge-charge curves for the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{0.33+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ cells tested at 25 and $50{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the voltage range $4.0-1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$at a current density $0.14 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$

### 6.3.2 $\mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$

Lithium manganese oxide, $\mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, has been extensively investigated as a positive electrode material for secondary lithium batteries [46-49]. This compound can be prepared by a soft-chemical method using the corresponding sodium manganese oxide as the parent compound. $\mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ crystallizes in the orthorhombic structure (Pbam S.G.) that maintains the parent $\mathrm{Na}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$-type tunnel structure [47]. It consists of double and triple rutile-type chains of edge-sharing $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra and a single chain of edge-sharing $\mathrm{MnO}_{5}$ producing a framework containing large and small tunnels along the $c$-axis direction, with three lattice sies of Li ions located in the tunnels. The $\mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ lattice differs from that of the well-known rocksalt-related lithium manganese oxides such as spinel-type $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and layered $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$. The electrochemical measurements for the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ cell showed a novel high potential plateau region at around 4.3 V on both charge and discharge with an average discharge voltage of 3.57 V , and an initial discharge capacity of $166 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between 2.5 and 4.8 V . The tunnel structure can reversibly intercalate up to $0.55-0.6 \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$ at moderate current densities, corresponding to capacities of $160-180 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The $4-\mathrm{V}$ plateau was observed in all the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{Mn}_{1-y} \mathrm{Ti}_{y} \mathrm{O}_{2}(0<y<0.55)$ cells with different Ti contents [49, 50]. The maximum discharge capacity ( $179 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) was observed in the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{0.44} \mathrm{Mn}_{0.89} \mathrm{Ti}_{0.11} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ cell. $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{PEO} / \mathrm{Na}_{0.2} \mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ cells were cycled at $0.1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$, with excellent capacity retention [46].

### 6.3.3 $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$

The difficulties met with the $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel that are reported in the next section have also motivated the investigation of several non-spinel manganese oxides [16]. $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ is known to exist in several phases. Two of them are the high
temperature orthorhombic (hereafter referred to as $\mathrm{o}-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ ) form and the monoclinic (hereafter referred to as $\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ ) form; both of them involve distorted oxygen arrays from the ideal cubic close packing [51]. The third form is the tetragonal $\lambda$ - $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ phase. The o- $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ (Pmnm S.G.) material that has the advantages of being easy to prepare and air stable, exhibits a high gravimetric capacity ( $286 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) and high potential against lithium. Ohzuku et al. [52] have prepared a low temperature o- $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ form by reacting $\gamma-\mathrm{MnOOH}$ and LiOH at $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, while a very low temperature ( $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) was used by ion exchange [53]. Gummow et al. [51] investigated o- $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ obtained at moderate ( $\sim 600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) temperature from $\gamma-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and LiOH in argon with carbon as a reducing agent. Synthesis by hydrothermal route [54] and quenching method [55] have also been reported. Liu et al. [56] prepared o- $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ nanorods by hydrothermal conversion of MnOOH needles. At $C / 20$ discharge rate, two voltage plateaus appeared at 4.0 and 2.9 V , which can be ascribed to the phase transition during the cycling from o- $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ to spinel phase [51]. Cyclic voltammetry reveals the reactions occurring during the transformation of o- $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ to the spinel $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ phase [55]. The $\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ phase ( $C 2 / m$ S.G.) with the same layered structure as $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ was considered very difficult to obtain. The lithium content of the synthesized material can vary from $x=0$ to 1 in $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$, depending on the synthesis approach used [57, 58]. Capitaine et al. [58] prepared this phase by chimie douce reaction from $\alpha-\mathrm{NaMnO}_{2}$ precursor via $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Na}$ exchange. For the $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ structure, the monoclinic unit cell parameters are $a=5.439$ (3) $\AA, b=2.809$ (2) $\AA, c=5.395$ (4) $\AA$ and $\beta=115.9$ (4) $\AA$. Layered $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ suffers from structural instability during electrochemical cycling and as a result, exhibits significant capacity fade [16]. The transformation to spinel-like phases for the $\mathrm{Li}_{0.5} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ composition is due to a migration of the Mn ions from the Mn plane to the Li plane. Substantial efforts have been made to stabilize the layered structure by substituting Mn with cation dopants, such as Al and Co [59-61]. The tetragonal phase ( $I 4_{1} /$ amd S.G.) is obtained by electrochemical or chemical lithiation of the $\lambda$ - $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel. $\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ rechargeable AA-type batteries have been developed at Taridan (Israel). The insertion reaction $\mathrm{LiMn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{6}+2 \mathrm{Li} \leftrightarrows 3 \mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ was suggested that delivered an average voltage 2.8 V . Typical cycle life a fast charging ( $250 \mathrm{~mA}, C / 2-C / 3$ rate) demonstrated at least 150 cycles at about $100 \%$ DOD. The high cycle life obtained in these cells is attributed to the very smooth morphology of Li deposition in 1,3-dioxolane$\mathrm{LiAsF}_{6}$ solutions [62]. Unfortunately, both $o$ - and $m-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ with a close-packed oxygen array tend to transform irreversibly to spinel-like structures upon electrochemical cycling. This is consistent with the appearance of two plateaus at $\approx 4.0$ and $\approx 2.9 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$, as the parent space groups $P m n m$ and $C 2 / m$ are subgroups of the $F d \overline{3} m$ spinel. Figure 6.11 is a schematic representation of the structural transformation from $o$ - and $m-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ to spinel upon electrochemical cycling.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-172.jpg?height=541&width=929&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=298)
Fig. 6.11 Schematic representation of the structural transformation from $o-$ and $m-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel upon electrochemical cycling. $F d \overline{3} m$ is a klassengleiche ( $k$-) subgroup of $F m \overline{3} m$

### 6.3.4 $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Na}_{0.5-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$

$\mathrm{Na}_{0.5} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ (designated as $\mathrm{Na}_{0.44} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ in the literature) adopting a non-closepacked tunnel structure has drawn some attention for Li insertion host as it does not transform to spinel-like phases and it shows extraordinary structural stability up to temperatures as high as $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ [48, 63,64]. The structure consists of $\mathrm{MnO}_{5}$ square pyramids and $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra that are connected together by sharing edges and corners. However, only a small amount of lithium could be extracted from the ion-exchanged sample $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Na}_{0.5-x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ although additional lithium could be inserted into $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Na}_{0.5-x} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$. Therefore, it is not attractive for lithium-ion cells fabricated with carbon anodes. Nevertheless, it has been shown to be a promising candidate for lithium polymer batteries employing metallic lithium anode [63]. Additionally, amorphous manganese oxides deliver high capacity ( $300 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) with good cyclability [64]. However, the capacity occurs over a wide voltage range of 4.3 to 1.5 V with a continuously sloping discharge profile and not much lithium could be extracted from the initial material. Therefore these amorphous oxides are not attractive for lithium-ion cells fabricated with carbon anodes. However, they may become viable with the development of new lithium-containing anodes.

### 6.4 Lithium Manganese Spinels

### 6.4.1 $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (LMO)

Among the lithium manganates, which are known to ionically conduct, perhaps the best studied is the lithium manganese oxide, $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as an intercalation compound. $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (LMO) belongs to the class of 4-V intercalation hosts, and

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-173.jpg?height=473&width=1186&top_left_y=201&top_left_x=169)
Fig. 6.12 Schematic representation of the structure of $A B_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel lattices. (a) the smallest (primitive) cubic unit cell of normal spinel ( $F d 3 m$ S.G.) and (b) the unit cell of the $1: 3$ ordered spinel ( $P 4_{1} 32$ S.G.). The structure is composed of alternating octants of $A \mathrm{O}_{4}$ tetrahedra and $B_{4} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cubes to build the $f c c$ unit cell

crystallizes with a cubic spinel-like structure. The structural relationship between the spinel $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and the lithium deficient materials was described by Hunter [21]. Its crystallographic structure possesses the symmetry $F d 3 m$ and has a general structural formula $(A)_{8 \mathrm{a}}\left[B_{2}\right]_{16 \mathrm{~d}} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, where the $B$ cations reside on the octahedral $16 d$ sites, the oxygen anions on the $32 e$ sites, and the $A$ cations occupy the tetrahedral $8 a$ sites. The approximately cubic close-packed (ccp) array of oxide ions incorporates $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra sharing two opposing corners with $\mathrm{LiO}_{4}$ tetrahedra. The spinel structure is primarily characterized by structural groups as follows. (1) $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra, connected to one another in three dimensions by edge sharing. (2) $\mathrm{LiO}_{4}$ tetrahedra, sharing each of their four corners with a different $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ unit but essentially isolated from one another. (3) A three-dimensional network of octahedral ( $16 c$ ) and tetrahedral (primarily $8 a$ ) sites, lithium ions moving through the ( $1 \times 1$ ) channels of the spinel lattice [65]. The atomic arrangements of normal ( $F d 3 m$ S.G.) and ordered ( $P 4_{1} 32$ S.G.) spinel lattices are illustrated in Fig. 6.12.

Early proposed to replace $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$, LMO has several advantages; (1) lower cost resulting from natural abundant manganese compared to Co and Ni , (2) lower toxicity and (3) high cell voltage. However, LMO is confronted with two major difficulties: (1) it is difficult to prepare samples of good quality, (2) although the LMO spinel is expected not to undergo JT distortion in the $4-\mathrm{V}$ domain, the formation of tetragonal $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ layer has been observed at the surface of the LMO particles due to local conditions of discharge [66], (3) $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ transforms into a tetragonal phase upon lithium insertion of more than one Li per Mn. Spectroscopic studies have confirmed that the cooperative JT effect from the $\left(\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$ octahedral is the mechanism for this transition. The capacity fade mechanism in this material concerns the large volume expansion associated with this structural modification that is problematic as both cubic and tetragonal phases coexist. As we show below, this coexistence is confirmed by the presence of a voltage plateau at 3 V . Limiting the cycling to the 4 V plateau reduced but did not
eliminate the structural distortion, while reducing the available theoretical capacity to $140 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$.

Various techniques were used for the synthesis of LMO such as solid-state reaction by heating at $\approx 800{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ a stoichiometric mixture of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ and EMD- $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ in air for 24 h [67], sol-gel [68], Pechini technique [69, 70], emulsion dry method [71, 72], ultrasonic spray pyrolysis [73], precipitation method [74]. LMO was prepared by various wet-chemical techniques such as sol-gel method assisted by succinic acid as chelating agent [75]. LMO was also synthesized by chemical insertion of threefold excess Li into $\gamma-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ followed by heattreatment in oxygen [76]. Among the nanostructured LMO materials, nanoparticles were prepared by combustion method [77, 78], and by high-energy ball milling method [79]. Mesoporous LMO spinels were synthesized by polymer template method [80]; nanospheres LMO, 35 nm diameter, were prepared by ultrasonic method using LiOH and nano- $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ [81]. Kim et al. [82] successfully fabricated highly ordered mesoporous LMO nanospheres through a chemical reaction in combination with ultrasonic waves. LMO nanorods, 300 nm diameter, were obtained by hydrothermal method using $\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ as precursor [83, 84]; LMO nanowires have been shown to grow in the ( 220 direction [85]. Fluorination as a means of changing the structure and electronic properties of oxides became an established route to stabilize their structure. It was used in the quest for improved high temperature storage and cycling of LMO spinels [86, 87]. Improvement of the cycling performance of LMO was also realized by coating the surface of particles by various oxides such as $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ [88], $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ [89], $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ [90], $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ [91], MgO [92], carried out through chemical processes, solution based techniques such as ) sol-gel transformation, solution precipitation, and micro-emulsion. Such a surface modification avoids the formation of polymeric species occurring intensively at high temperature ( $50-60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), which increase the electrode impedance and even isolate electrically part of the active mass [93]. The electrochemical properties of quaternary spinel solid-solution phases $\mathrm{Li} M_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{O}_{4}(M=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Ti}, \mathrm{Ge}$, $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Zn}$ ) were studied for different conditions of sample preparation and different degrees of cation substitution [94, 95]. Several hundred papers reported the physical properties of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ such as structural evolution on charging and discharging [96], electrical conductivity driven by the small polaronic mechanism [97], lattice dynamics [98, 99], magnetic properties [100, 101], electronic properties [102]. Wang et al. [103] reported the effect of the annealing temperature on the cubic lattice parameter of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ synthesized by a sol-gel precipitation method.

Figure 6.13 shows the charge-discharge curves at the first cycle of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cell, under galvanostatic conditions at $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The cell was charged and discharged at current densities of $1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$, while the voltage was monitored between 3.0 and 4.4 V. In this potential domain, the charge-discharge curves correspond to the voltage profiles characteristic of the spinel material associated with lithium occupancy of tetrahedral sites [104]. The variation of the potential for the complete cell reveals the presence of two regions during the lithium insertion-extraction processes. The first region (I) is characterized by an S-shaped voltage curve, whereas the second region (II) corresponds to a plateau portion. In region I, the charge

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-175.jpg?height=473&width=1178&top_left_y=203&top_left_x=173)
Fig. 6.13 (a) First charge-discharge profile of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cell in the $4-\mathrm{V}$ and $3-\mathrm{V}$ region and (b) cyclic voltammogram carried out at sweep rate $1 \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{min}^{-1}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-175.jpg?height=315&width=929&top_left_y=822&top_left_x=298)
Fig. 6.14 Phase diagram of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ showing the plateaus occurring in the 4- and 3-V region

voltage increases continuously in the voltage range of $3.80-4.05 \mathrm{~V}$. In region II, the charge voltage is stable around 4.15 V . Furthermore, these results describe the phase diagram of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ compounds against the lithium content.

The phase diagram of the $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(0 \leq x \leq 2)$ system showing the plateaus occurring in the 4- and 3-V domains is reported in Fig. 6.14. From the results shown in Fig. 6.14, a two-phase system is recognized in the region II corresponding to the upper voltage plateau, whereas the region I can be attributed to a single phase characterized by an S-shaped voltage curve. The two regimes of intercalation are clearly depicted when the derivative voltage, $-d x / d V$, is plotted vs. cell voltage. For the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cell, the upper 4-V plateau that is the region available for cycling provides a capacity over $120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [105]. The lithium extraction/insertion from/ into the $8 a$ tetrahedral sites occurs at around 4 V (Fig. 6.13) with the maintenance of the initial cubic spinel symmetry, while the extraction/insertion from/into the $16 c$ octahedral sites occurs at around 3 V by a two-phase mechanism involving the cubic spinel $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and the tetragonal lithiated spinel $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$. Palacin et al. [106] found two additional steps at 4.5 and 3.3 V , characteristics of double hexagonal layers upon oxidation. Although they both involve the same $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+/ 4+}$ couple, the $1-\mathrm{V}$ difference between the two processes is a reflection of the differences in the site energies [107]. A deep energy well for the $8 a$ tetrahedral $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions and the high activation energy required for the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions to move from one $8 a$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-176.jpg?height=268&width=925&top_left_y=216&top_left_x=298)
Fig. 6.15 Illustration of the JT distortion in manganese oxides: (a) $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}: 3 d^{3}$ with cubic symmetry (no JT distortion) and (b) $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}: 3 d^{4}$ with tetragonal symmetry (JT distortion)

tetrahedral site to another via an energetically unfavorable neighboring $16 c$ site lead to a higher voltage of 4 V . The cubic to tetragonal transition when going from Li $\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ to $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is due to the Jahn-Teller (JT) distortion associated with the single electron in the $e_{\mathrm{g}}$ orbitals of a high spin $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}: 3 d^{4}\left(t_{2 g}^{3} e_{g}^{1}\right)$ ion (Fig. 6.15). A cooperative distortion of the $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra with long $\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds along the $c$ axis and short $\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds along the $a$ and $b$ axes results in a macroscopic tetragonal symmetry for $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ shown in Fig. 6.15.

Although two lithium ions per $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ formula unit could be reversibly extracted/inserted, the cubic to tetragonal transition is accompanied by a $16 \%$ increase in the $c / a$ ratio of the unit cell parameters and $6.5 \%$ increase in unit cell volume. Let us recall that this change is too severe for the electrodes to maintain structural integrity during the discharge/charge cycle and so $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ exhibits rapid capacity fade in the $3-\mathrm{V}$ region. As a result, $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ could be utilized only in the $4-\mathrm{V}$ region with a limited practical capacity of around $120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ (Fig. 6.13), which corresponds to an )extraction/insertion of 0.4 lithium per Mn and compared well with the theoretical capacity of $140 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ we have mentioned earlier. Unfortunately, even with a limited capacity, $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ tends to exhibit capacity fade in the 4-V region as well, particularly at elevated temperatures $\left(50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. Various reasons have been attributed to the capacity fade in the $4-\mathrm{V}$ region. For example, the dissolution of manganese into the electrolyte originating from a disproportionation of $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ into $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ and $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ [108] and the formation of tetragonal $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ on the surface of the particles under conditions of non-equilibrium cycling [66] have been thought to be the source of )capacity fade. In addition, the $4-\mathrm{V}$ region involves the formation of two cubic phases and that may also play a role in the capacity fade. The difficulties of lattice distortions in $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel have motivated strategies to suppress JT distortion. One way to suppress the JT distortion is to increase the average oxidation state of manganese since $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}: 3 d^{3}\left(t_{2 g}^{3} e_{g}^{0}\right)$ does not undergo JT distortion. The oxidation state of manganese can be increased either by aliovalent cationic substitutions or by increasing the oxygen content in $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4+\delta}$ [109-112]. Additionally, cationic substitutions with other transition metals $M=\mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni}$ and Cu in $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2-y} M_{y} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ have been pursued to improve the capacity retention of spinel manganese oxides [113-116]. This strategy is linked to the fact that the substitution of some Mn
cations for another ion of valence $\leq+3$ (bulk doping) will keep the Mn oxidation state above 3.5 during cycling over the $4-\mathrm{V}$ plateau, thus reducing the )JT distortion on discharge. In addition, surface doping can decrease the manganese dissolution by reducing the apparent contact area with the electrolyte and inhibiting the surface Jahn-Teller distortion. Many efforts have been made recently to synthesize doped LMO. The numerous works on the preparation and doping modes (bulk, surface, and combined doping) of doped LMO have been recently reviewed [117]; therefore, they are not reviewed here, and we simply redirect the reader to this paper for the different doping elements and processes and a discussion on there effectiveness to reduce the capacity fading of LMO. However, such a strategy leads to a decrease in capacity in the 4-V region with a development of two plateaus for the extraction/ insertion of lithium from/into the $8 a$ tetrahedral sites: one around 4 V corresponding to the oxidation of $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ to $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ and the other around 5 V corresponding to the oxidation of the other transition metal ions. Although an increase in the cell voltage to 5 V is attractive from an energy density point of view, the $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2-\mathrm{y}} M_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ oxides are prone to suffer from oxygen loss from the lattice and safety concerns in the $5-\mathrm{V}$ region.

### 6.4.2 Surface Modified LMO

The dissolution of manganese in the electrolyte is of major concern [118]. The reaction of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ with the electrolyte increases with the temperature, so that $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-based batteries need being maintained at room temperature. In this context, the study of surface modifications of LMO is of primary importance to protect the material against reactions with the electrolyte and avoid the dissolution of $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ in order to achieve two goals: (1) improve the calendar life and cycling life to make it competitive with other cathode elements, in order to get rid of the lamellar component in the cathode, (2) restore the thermal safety of the battery. The most commonly used coating materials are metal oxides such as $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$, $\mathrm{ZnO}, \mathrm{SiO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, which can successfully protect electrode from HT attack. A review of developments in the surface modification of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as cathode material of power lithium-ion battery can be seen in ref. [119]. To explore the change in the electrochemical properties of the spinel electrodes, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out for the pristine and coated material upon cycling in the charged state for cells maintained at $55^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Nyquist plots derived from the analyses of uncoated and $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$-coated $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ are shown in Fig. 6.16a, b, respectively. Each EIS spectrum consists of two semicircles and a slope. The first semicircle in the high-to-medium frequency region is attributed to resistance of surface film ( $R_{\mathrm{sf}}$ ) that covers the electrode particle. The semicircle at medium-tolow frequency region is associated with the charge transfer resistance ( $R_{\mathrm{ct}}$ ) coupled with a double-layer capacitance. The slope at the low-frequency region is assigned to lithium-ion diffusion in the bulk material. On the basis of this mechanism, the equivalent circuit used for analysis is given as insert. $R_{\mathrm{w}}$ represents the electrolyte

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-178.jpg?height=532&width=1167&top_left_y=205&top_left_x=180)
Fig. 6.16 Nyquist diagrams of (a) uncoated $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as a function of the state-of-charge after 50 cycles in the potential range $4.0-4.3 \mathrm{~V}$ and (b) after $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ coating. Cells were cycled at $1 C$ rate and maintained at $T=55^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The equivalent circuit used for the analysis is shown as insert (see text for symbol meaning)

resistance, $\mathrm{CPE}_{\mathrm{dl}}$ is a constant phase element and $W_{z}$ is the Warburg element corresponding to the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion diffusion in the host material. After 50 cycles, the increase of $R_{\mathrm{ct}}$ of the uncoated material is due to the chemical evolution at the electrode-electrolyte interface rather than $\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{II})$ migration. The impedance spectra of $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$-coated $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ clearly display the contribution of the low frequency element due to the modified SEI layer, which reduces the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$-ion transport through the coating.

It has been reported that LMO particles coated with either borate glass $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}- 2 \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ (LBO) or with fluorine exhibit good high-temperature electrochemical performance. LBO-coated LMO electrode via solution method has an excellent cycling behavior ( $112 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) without any capacity loss even after 30 cycles at $1 C$ rate [120]. Fluoride is also used to coat LMO to improve its cyclability because it is very stable even in HF. Lee et al. [121] reported that the BiOF-coated spinel electrode had excellent capacity retention at $55^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, maintaining its initial discharge capacity of $96 \%$ after 100 cycles against $84 \%$ for the pristine material because the oxyfluoride layer provides a strong protection against HF attack and scavenges HF .

### 6.4.3 Defect Spinels

The technological interest of the defect spinels $\mathrm{Li}_{1-\delta} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-\delta} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as positive electrodes comes from their ability to reversibly intercalate/deintercalate lithium. Considering the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ phase diagram (Fig. 6.17), the area that falls within the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}-\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}-\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ tie triangle constitutes a region of defect spinel compositions. The defective $\mathrm{Li}_{1-\delta} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-\delta} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ manganospinels, which contain vacancies at both tetrahedral and octahedral manganese sites can lead to high capacity

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-179.jpg?height=611&width=939&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=293)
Fig. 6.17 Expanded region of the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ phase diagram. The $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}-\mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}-\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$ tie triangle includes the region of defect spinel compositions

( $>230 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ). Such compounds also have a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$ ratio of 0.5 and have an average $\mathrm{Mn}^{+}$valence state varying from 3.5 to 4 (depending on the value of $\delta$ ) $[16,122]$. For a value of $\delta=0.11$, this compound, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$, is a nonstoichiometric form with $\mathrm{Mn}^{+}$oxidation state of 4.0 . The higher is the value of $\delta$, the lower is the capacity at 4 V , the smaller is the lattice parameter, and the better is the cyclability in the $3-\mathrm{V}$ region. It was then speculated that the oxygen-rich lithium manganospinels can deliver high steady capacities in excess of $150 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ (Fig. 6.17).

Defect spinel with the formula $\mathrm{Li}_{0.50} \mathrm{MnO}_{2.25}\left(\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{0.51} \mathrm{MnO}_{2.20}$ were synthesized by sol-gel method via the citrate route. The structure analysis yielded powders of cubic structure ( $F d 3 m$ S.G.) with lattice parameters $a=8.193 \AA$ for $\mathrm{Li}_{0.52} \mathrm{MnO}_{2.1}$ and $a=8.162 \AA$ for $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$ that can be compared with that of stoichiometric $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(a=8.248 \AA)$ and that of $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}(a=8.137 \AA)$. The formula of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$ is written $\left(\square_{0.11} \mathrm{Li}_{0.89}\right)_{8 \mathrm{a}}\left[\square_{0.22} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.78}\right]_{16 \mathrm{~d}} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ in spinel notation. $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$ accommodates $0.95 \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$ to a cutoff of 2.0 V and shows a slight transformation from cubic to tetragonal for 0.9 Li (Fig. 6.18). This was evidenced by the appearance of a second phase in the Raman spectrum of $\mathrm{Li}_{4.4} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$. Similar features are observed for $\mathrm{Li}_{0.51} \mathrm{MnO}_{2.20}$, which can insert $0.86 \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}$.

### 6.4.4 Li Doped Spinels

For wide scale application, the major drawback of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ comes from the difficulty to prepare samples of good quality because the Jahn-Teller $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ ion favors lattice distortions. Several investigations have been made to overcome the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-180.jpg?height=469&width=1173&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=176)
Fig. 6.18 Charge-discharge profiles of defect spinels (a) $\mathrm{Li}_{0.5} \mathrm{MnO}_{2.25}\left(\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Mn}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}\right)$ and (b) $\mathrm{Li}_{0.51} \mathrm{MnO}_{2.2}$. Measurements were carried out at $C / 20$ rate in the potential range $2-4 \mathrm{~V}$

capacity fading by replacing Li for Mn [16]. The reduction of $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ concentration has been obtained by substitution of monovalent ions, which enhances the stability of the $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel phase. In the system $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{Li}_{1+x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-x} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinels exist as a continuous series of solid solution in the range $0.00 \leq x \leq 0.33$. It is generally accepted that Li-rich spinels behave with improved electrochemical performance. Recently, numerous papers reported the physical properties of such materials [100, 101, 123]. Both structural and magnetic analyses reveal that the physical and electrochemical properties are importantly influenced by the presence of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$ impurity phase. The presence of this additional phase reduces the average oxidation state of manganese according to disproportionation reaction, so that the final composition of the spinel phase is $\mathrm{Li}_{3.8} \mathrm{Mn}_{5.2} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ (or $\mathrm{Li}_{1.27} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.73} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ ). Due to the important geometric frustration of the magnetic interactions, and dilution of the antiferromagnetic interactions, no magnetic ordering is observed in the temperature range investigated. The anomalous magnetic properties, including the Dysonian profile of the ESR line, show that the material is metallic. The Sommerfeld constant deduced from the specific heat measurements is $308 \mathrm{~mJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-2}$ per mole of $\mathrm{Li}_{1.27} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.73} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, which shows that this material belongs to the class of heavy-fermion systems like $\mathrm{LiV}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{LiTi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. These heavy fermions are the minority-spin $t_{\mathrm{g}} \downarrow$ electrons of $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ ions that have a reduced effective masse $m / m_{0}=467$. The electrochemical properties show that the specific capacity of $163 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for $\mathrm{Li}_{3.8} \mathrm{Mn}_{5.2} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ at charge rate $1 C$ is a large value due to the possible insertion of Li up to the composition $\mathrm{Li}_{6.8} \mathrm{Mn}_{5.2} \mathrm{O}_{12}$. The origin of the disproportionation, and the flat voltage in the lithiation process have been discussed in the framework of the stability of the Mott insulator phase with respect to the metallic phase [75].

Figure 6.19a displays the discharge-charge curve of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cell. As reported previously [16, 124], the $\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{\text {IV }}\right] \mathrm{O}_{6}$ framework of $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Li}_{1 / 3} \mathrm{Mn}_{5 / 3}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel is an attractive host structure for lithium insertion-extraction reactions because it provides a three-dimensional network of face-sharing tetrahedra and octahedra for lithium-ion diffusion. $\mathrm{Li}_{4 / 3} \mathrm{Mn}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ has a capacity of $163 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the potential

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-181.jpg?height=575&width=1180&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=171)
Fig. 6.19 (a) Charge-discharge curves as a function of the lithium content in $\mathrm{Li}_{4+x} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ cell. Arrows indicate the composition for analysis. (b) The Raman spectra of $\mathrm{Li}_{4+x} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ electrode materials at different amounts of lithium insertion. The spectrum of the final product shows the features of the tetragonal structure

range $3.5-1.5 \mathrm{~V}$. Upon intercalation (discharge) the phase transition process from spinel to rock-salt type phase with 3 Li uptake in $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ :

$$
\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}+x \mathrm{Li}++x e^{-} \leftrightarrows \mathrm{Li}_{7} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12} .
$$

Note that this phase transformation starts to dominate the electrode kinetics. The OCV curve vs. $x(\mathrm{Li})$ displays a voltage plateau in the Li composition range $0.05 \leq x \leq 0.85$. This curve slightly decreases from 0.9 before to drop sharply at $x \approx 0.95$. A two-phase electrochemical reaction occurs as a flat voltage response; in this reaction, a distinct change in voltage is evident as one phase becomes depleted, thus providing an end-of-charge indicator [125]. Figure 6.19b shows the phase evolution upon Li insertion studied by Raman scattering (RS) spectroscopy. Spectra (curves A-E) of electrochemically lithiated $\mathrm{Li}_{4+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ spinels were recorded on material taken from a cell discharged in the range $3.2-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$. The RS spectrum of $\mathrm{Li}_{6.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ shows clearly the structural change that occurred in $\mathrm{Li}_{4 / 3} \mathrm{Mn}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ upon Li insertion from cubic ( $O_{\mathrm{h}}{ }^{7}$ ) to tetragonal ( $D_{4 \mathrm{~h}}^{19}$ ) structure. This structural modification induces three typical RS bands at wave numbers 255, 282 and $397 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The high-frequency band appears at $628 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ with a shoulder at $592 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. Similarly to $\mathrm{Li}_{7} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$, spectral features $\mathrm{Li}_{6.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ are consistent with the tetragonal $I 4_{1} /$ amd symmetry. A tetragonal distortion is expected to occur with the Li insertion in $\mathrm{Li}_{4 / 3} \mathrm{Mn}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ because of appearance of the $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ Jahn-Teller ions at the end of the discharge. The net effect is a lowering in the crystal symmetry from cubic $F d 3 m$ to tetragonal $I 4_{1} /$ amd [126].

### 6.5 Five-Volt Spinels

To improve the poor cycling performance of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, several works have reported transition metal substituted spinel materials $\mathrm{Li} M_{y} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-y} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ with $M=\mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Cr}$, etc. For a review see ref. [127]. Their electrochemical properties strongly depend on the kind and content of $3 d$-transition metal. However, they exhibit high-voltage plateau at around 5 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$[128, 129]. Among them, $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is of special interest for its dominant potential at around 4.7 V . The plateau at 4.7 V results from $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}^{4+}$ redox couple while the plateau at 4.1 V is due to the $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} / \mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ redox couple. In contrast to the tetragonal structure formation upon insertion of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$into undoped spinel, the spinel $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ remains cubic to from $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{Ni}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$.

The structure is identified as a primitive cubic cell $a=8.166 \AA$ with a space group $P 4_{1} 32$ instead of $F d 3 m$ for normal spinel lattice. The space group $P 4_{1} 32$ allows to place the larger $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ ions (ionic radius $0.69 \AA$ ) in bigger $4 b$ site instead of $16 d$ site of normal spinel structure (Fig. 6.12). The smaller unit cell dimension is primarily due to the change in Mn valence state. Despite the replacement of a fraction of Mn ions by bigger $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ ions, the Mn valence change effect is prevailing. Figure 6.20 presents the RS spectra of $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ powders synthesized by wet chemical method. Using different procedure of calcination, two types of materials are obtained, i.e., the ordered- and the normal-spinel framework. The former compound exhibits additional Raman bands due to the symmetry lowering [130].

Fig. 6.20 Raman spectra of $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ powders with the ordered- and the normal-spinel framework
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-182.jpg?height=832&width=735&top_left_y=1240&top_left_x=609)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-183.jpg?height=525&width=1167&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=182)
Fig. 6.21 Charge-discharge curves of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cells with (a) normal-spinel and (b) ordered-spinel framework

Figure 6.21 shows the first charge-discharge profile of the spinel $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cells in the voltage range 3.5 to 4.9 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$for (a) normal $A \left[B_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel lattice and (b) ordered 1:3 spinel framework [130]. Our data are consistent with these of prior results in terms of their high-voltage characteristics. As can be seen in Fig. 6.21, the $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ samples reveal operating voltage higher than 4.5 V . The curves are characteristic of a two-step lithium intercalationdeintercalation behavior. The small plateau around 4.0 V is related to the redox couple $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} / \mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$. Pure and stoichiometric $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ should not contain $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ ions. However, due to a small loss of oxygen during the synthesis at high temperature, part of the inactive $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ ions are reduced to active $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ due to charge neutrality. The plateau around 4.7 V has been attributed to the oxidation of $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ to $\mathrm{Ni}^{4+}$. The normal-spinel phase shows predominantly one-step reaction at 4.65 V . The voltage profile of the ordered-spinel structure transforms from a sloping curve to flat curve at 4.72 V . In the voltage range $3.5-4.9 \mathrm{~V}$, the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ delivers a capacity $133 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ during the first discharge. The capacity retention is over $97 \%$ of the initial capacity for cells discharged at $C / 10$ in the voltage range $3.5-4.9 \mathrm{~V}$.

The cycling performance of the normal $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is better than that of the ordered spinel material due to the higher diffusion coefficient of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$in the former. Unfortunately, the disordered state favors the oxygen deficiency responsible for the presence of the $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ Jahn-Teller and the presence of $\mathrm{Li}_{y} \mathrm{Ni}_{1-y} \mathrm{O}$ impurity, which is damageable to the electrochemical properties. Therefore, different cation substitutions have been made aiming to overcome this problem. The best result has been obtained with substitution of Cr for Mn . In particular, $\mathrm{LiMn}_{1.45} \mathrm{Cr}_{0.1} \mathrm{Ni}_{0.45} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel prepared by a co-precipitation method assisted by a post-annealing at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ that reoxidizes the manganese to the $\mathrm{Mn}^{+4}$ state showed improved capacity at any $C$-rate up to $5 C$, with only $6 \%$ capacity lost after 125 cycles [131].

### 6.6 Vanadium Oxides

Vanadium shows versatile oxide phases of mixed valence states between $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$ and $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$. They have higher electronic conductivity arising from the hopping mechanism from $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$ to $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ and can be denoted by the nominal formula of $\mathrm{V}_{n} \mathrm{O}_{2 n+1}(n>2)$. Lithium insertion reaction has been shown in vanadium oxides such as $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$, $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B}), \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ and $\mathrm{LiV}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8}$. Their structure consists of edge-shared octahedra forming single zig-zag strings and double zig-zag ribbons with infinite extension normal to the plane of the paper. The single and double sheets are then joined together by sharing corners to give a three-dimensional lattice. Murphy et al. [132, 133] have investigated some distinct phases of vanadium oxides such as $\mathrm{V}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7}$, $\mathrm{V}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$, and $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$.

### 6.6.1 $\quad \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$

$\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ is a black material which derives from the $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$ structure and is intermediate in composition between $\mathrm{VO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$. In this family, $\mathrm{V}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{9}$ appear to have an intermediate structure between that of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$. The monoclinic structure of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ contains edge-shared distorted $\mathrm{VO}_{6}$ octahedra forming single and double zig-zag chains linked together by further edge sharing corner-shared (Fig. 6.22). The resulting sheets (single and double) are interconnected by corner sharing, thus giving a 3D framework [134]. This structure contains tri-capped cavities joined through shared square faces. The three open faces of the cavity should permit lithium-ion diffusion along (010) with the possibility of exchange between pairs of adjacent channels. Stoichiometric $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ can be written as $\left(\mathrm{V}^{4+}\right)_{4}\left(\mathrm{~V}^{5+}\right)_{2}\left(\mathrm{O}^{2-}\right)_{13}$ as far as the valency state of the vanadium ions are concerned.

Fig. 6.22 The
crystallographic structure of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ showing the shear lattice derived from the $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$. The structure consists of distorted $\mathrm{VO}_{6}$ octahedra sharing corners and edges
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-184.jpg?height=564&width=511&top_left_y=1508&top_left_x=833)

Fig. 6.23 The dischargecharge curve of the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ cell carried out at $C / 24$ in the potential range $1.5-3.5 \mathrm{~V}$. The plateaus correspond to the various redox states of vanadium in the $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$-like structure. Reproduced with permission from [135]. Copyright 1983 Elsevier
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-185.jpg?height=580&width=744&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=605)

The structure of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ is interesting from an electrochemical viewpoint due to the theoretical maximum limit of lithium uptake giving an energy density of $890 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$. The stoichiometric $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ structure is believed to accommodate 8 Li per formula unit as determined by the available electronic sites rather than the structural cavities [135]. The limit corresponds to a situation when all the vanadium ions are present in the trivalent $\mathrm{V}^{3+}$ state. As a function of the stoichiometry, the maximum uptake goes to 1.35 Li for $\mathrm{VO}_{2.18}$ oxide. Reversible chemical and electrochemical insertion of lithium into $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ was first demonstrated by Murphy et al. [132,136], and its potential as an active cathode material in practical batteries has since been more fully investigated [135]. The discharge curve exhibits three distinct plateaus, reflecting the sequential filling of non-equivalent sites in the host structure (Fig. 6.23) [137].

Figure 6.24a shows the Arrhenius plot of the electrical conductivity of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}(0 \leq x \leq 6)$. The pure material has a conductivity of $1 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature and exhibits a semiconductor behavior. The electronic conduction in $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ is due to the electron hopping between $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$ and $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ states. The decreasing conductivity corresponds to a steadily decrease with addition of Li-ions in the $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ framework. Intercalation of Li -ions is believed to lower the valence state of vanadium ions by transfer of electrons. The relative concentration of reduced cations results in the lowering of the conductivity towards a poor electronic semiconductor. Electronic conductivity of pressed $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ powders indicates a sharp fall in two steps with increasing Li content [138, 139]. For lithiated $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$, we observe a continuous decrease of the electrical conductivity. Lowest conductivity of $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ has been measured in $\mathrm{Li}_{6} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$. This is also accompanied by an increase in activation energy, a general phenomenon observed in any oxide with small-polaron conduction. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ ( $x=0.0$ and 3.2) compounds reveals the transition from metal-like to small-polaron features (Fig. 6.24b). In pure $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$, a Drude edge is observed around $200 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$, which is the contribution of the free charge-carriers in the $3 d$ band. For lithiated

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-186.jpg?height=561&width=1176&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=173)
Fig. 6.24 (a) Arrhenius plot of the electrical conductivity of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ in the composition range $0 \leq x \leq 6$. Measurements were carried out on pellets using the van der Pauw method. (b) FTIR absorption spectra of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{3.2} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$

$\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$, we observe a continuous decrease of the electrical conductivity, which is also recorded in the far-infrared spectrum by the disappearance of the Drude absorption [137].

### 6.6.2 $\mathrm{LiVO}_{2}$

$\mathrm{LiVO}_{2}$ adopts the $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ structure of $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$. Although lithium could be readily extracted from $\mathrm{LiVO}_{2}$, the vanadium ions migrate to the lithium planes for $(1-x)<0.67$ in $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{VO}_{2}$ [140,141]. Similarly, the $\mathrm{LiV}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel suffers from the migration of vanadium ions during the charge-discharge process.

### 6.6.3 $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$

The $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ phase is a metastable form of $\mathrm{VO}_{2}[142]$ that exhibits high capacity with good cyclability. The shear structure derived from the hypothetical $\mathrm{VO}_{3}$, $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$-like structure consists of distorted $\mathrm{VO}_{6}$ octahedra sharing corners and edges [143-145]. Additionally, $\mathrm{VO}_{2}$ (B) contains one dimensional tunnel for alkali insertion. Metastable $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ crystallizes in a monoclinic structure clearly identified ( $a=12.03, b=3.693, c=6.42 \AA, \beta=106^{\circ}$ ) and distinct from the closely related structure of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$. However, $\mathrm{VO}_{2}$ (B) is very difficult to prepare by conventional high temperature route because the phase change from metastable to thermodynamically more stable rutile phase $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{R})$. Two other $\mathrm{VO}_{2}$ phases are known: $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~A})$ which exhibits the shift of oxygen vacancies in the fcc lattice and the

Fig. 6.25 The initial two charge-discharge curves of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{VO}_{2}$ (B) cell cycled at C/12 rate
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-187.jpg?height=586&width=766&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=578)

metallic $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{M})$ phase [142]. Nanostructured $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ material was prepared in various forms such as nanowires, nanobelts, nanoribbons, nanoneedles, and nanorods [146-150]. The synthesis of $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ by slow reduction of $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ in $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ shows a preferential growth in platelets with surfaces parallel to the $a b$-plane [151]. Nanorods $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ were prepared by solvothermal reduction of $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ by formaldehyde or isopropanol [152]. Vanadium oxide aerogels were used as a precursor for preparing nanotextured $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ by low-temperature heat treatment under vacuum. The $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ material prepared from aerogel precursor retains the fibrous morphology and high surface area of the aerogel. The electrochemical behavior of $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ shows specific capacity for lithium as high as $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and good stability when cycled between 4 and 2.4 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$[153]. Nanoflowerlike $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ synthesized via one-step hydrothermal process had the first discharge capacity of $180 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [154]. $\mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ nanobelts synthesized by simple hydrothermal route have rectangular cross-section with mean length $\sim 1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$, mean width $\sim 80 \mathrm{~nm}$ and thickness $\sim 50 \mathrm{~nm}$. Electrochemical tests show an initial discharge capacity of $321 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with voltage plateau near 2.5 V [155]. Figure 6.25 presents the initial two charge-discharge curves of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{VO}_{2}(\mathrm{~B})$ cell cycled at $C / 12$ rate.

### 6.7 Concluding Remarks

Numerous efforts are done to replace the cobalt oxide used in commercial lithiumion batteries by materials with low cost and environmental concerns. The development of new materials needs new synthesis procedure such as sol-gel processing, ion-exchange reaction, hydrothermal reaction, etc. The chemical and structural stabilities of the transition-metal oxide electrodes have been compared by studying bulk samples. In this respect, the various physicochemical techniques are welcome

Table 6.3 Comparison of the electrode characteristics of some $3 d$ transition-metal oxides
| Oxide | Practical capacity $(\mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g})^{-1}$ | Average voltage (V) | Reversible Li ${ }^{+}$ per $M$ atom | Energy density ( $\mathrm{mWh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{VO}_{2}$ | 290 | 2.6 | 0.9 | 750 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | 120 | 3.8 | 0.4 | 455 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Na}_{y} \mathrm{MnO}_{z} \mathrm{I}_{\eta}$ (amorphous) | 275 | 2.6 | 1.5 | 715 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{CoO}_{2}$ | 140 | 3.7 | 0.5 | 520 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{MnCoO}_{4}$ | 125 | 4.8 | 0.4 | 600 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{NiO}_{2}$ | 140 | 3.8 | 0.5 | 530 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{Ni}_{0.85} \mathrm{Co}_{0.15} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ | 180 | 3.75 | 0.6 | 675 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Fe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ | 110 | 3.6 | 0.8 | 400 |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{FePO}_{4}$ | 170 | 3.3 | 1.0 | 560 |


to design the best structure. Synthesis of amorphous compounds could help in the knowledge of microstructures in this regard.

The search for materials that exhibit superior cycling performance has led to the study of transition-metal- and cation-substituted materials; Effect of doping have been successful in many cases such as $\mathrm{LiNiO}_{2}, \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}, \mathrm{LiMnO}_{2}, \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. The $\mathrm{LiCo}_{1-y} M_{y} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ system showing relatively better chemical stability can replace the conventional $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ with respect to oxygen loss. However, the problem of thermal instability of these lamellar compounds is not solved [156]. New systems such as $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.5} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.5} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ show interesting electrochemical features but need better control of their crystal chemistry.

In view of maximizing the cell voltage and energy density, transition metal oxide hosts have emerged as the choice for cathodes. The electrode properties of some of the $3 d$ transition metal oxides are summarized in Table 6.3. Among the various oxide hosts, the layered $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{O}_{2}(M=\mathrm{Co}$ and Ni$)$, spinel $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, and olivine $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ containing lithium have emerged as the leading cathode candidates as they can be coupled with the presently available anode host carbon, which does not contain lithium. However, only $40-65 \%$ of the theoretical capacity of the layered $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{O}_{2}$ and spinel $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ could be practically utilized due to various difficulties such as chemical and structural instabilities, which are discussed in a later chapter.

The future challenge is to develop simple oxide cathodes without having other elements such as P or Na in which at least one lithium ion per transition metal ion could be reversibly extracted/inserted while keeping the materials cost and toxicity low; such a cathode can nearly double the energy density compared to the present level. There are also possibilities to increase the capacity of anode hosts perhaps by focusing on amorphous materials. An alternative approach is to develop cells with lithium-containing anodes and lithium-free cathodes. This strategy will allow the use of some of the already known high capacity cathodes such as the vanadium oxides that have better chemical stability and safety characteristics. From a safety, and cycle and shelf life points of view, cathodes with a lower voltage ( $3-4 \mathrm{~V}$ ) are
preferred, but this is at expense of the energy density. In particular, we see in the next chapter that frameworks built with $\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)^{3-}$ polyanions are very promising materials. The phospho-olivine structure $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ exhibits a very stable framework upon electrochemical cycling, and carbon-coated $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ is an excellent cathode element for high-power batteries, but its energy density is smaller than that of lamellar compounds, although recent studies indicate that $100 \%$ of the theoretical capacity of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ can be utilized.
