We have followed a logical structure starting with synthesis, crystallographic properties, the characterization of the crucial carbon-coating layer at the surface of LFP particles, magnetic properties, reaction with water and humidity, and finally electrochemical performance of optimized material at room temperature, $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and in full Li-ion cells. Depending of the synthesis procedure, the fundamental properties can be modified because of impurities poisoning this material. These impurities are identified, and quantitative estimate of their concentrations is deduced from the combination of analytical methods. The most powerful technique used so far is the SQUID magnetometry, which is very sensitive to detect $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions. Note that such iron-based impurities may be at the origin of iron dissolution in the electrolyte producing short circuit of the battery. Thus an
optimized preparation provides materials with carbon-coated particles free of any impurity phase, insuring structural stability and electrochemical performance. Raman scattering spectroscopy is the tool to probe the quality of the carbon layer, especially its degree of graphitization.

The structural properties have been correlated with the electrochemical performance of the positive electrode materials. It appears that a severe control of synthesis conditions is needed to obtain materials with good performance under high current density.

The product reacts with water, but not with dry atmosphere, so that storage of the LFP powder in a dry chamber at $5 \%$ relative humidity is needed, which is sufficient to guarantee that the product does not age before manufacturing the battery. The carbon coat is not protective. Exposure to humidity induces delithiation of the surface layer. However, the resulting $\mathrm{FePO}_{4}$ layer that is formed within minutes of exposure to humidity is very protective, so that the damage remains limited to the surface layer (about 3 nm thick) unless the time of exposure to humid atmosphere is very large (months).

All these researches justify the use of the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ material as a cathode element in new generation of lithium secondary batteries operating for powering hybrid electric vehicles and full electric vehicles, and also to store the energy from windmills or photoelectric plants to solve the intermittence problem on smart grids.

Only the phosphate polyanion has been considered here. For completeness, we can also mention a pyrophosphate, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoP}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}$, considered as a 4.9 V cathode [218]. This pyrophosphate crystallizes in the monoclinic structure ( $P 2_{1} / c$ S.G.), in which Li occupies five sites; two are tetrahedrally coordinated, one forms bipyramidal sites, and two Li share them occupancy with Co bipyramids. The material synthesized using a two-step solid-state method delivered a discharge capacity of ca. $80 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 20$ rate, which illustrates the superiority of the phosphate compounds as active cathode elements. The fluoro-polyanionic compounds, however, deserve a special attention, and the next chapter is devoted to them.

In addition to the phosphate polyanion compounds we have considered, we can also mention for completeness a pyrophosphate, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoP}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}$, considered as a 4.9 V cathode [218]. This pyrophosphate crystallizes in the monoclinic structure ( $P 2_{1} / c$ S.G.), in which Li occupies five sites; two are tetrahedrally coordinated, one forms bipyramidal sites, and two Li share them occupancy with Co bipyramids. The material synthesized using a two-step solid-state method delivered a discharge capacity of ca. $80 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 20$ rate, which illustrates the superiority of the phosphate compounds as active cathode elements. Among the other $X \mathrm{O}_{4}(X=\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Si}$, Mo, W)-based compounds, we have selected the silicates $X=\mathrm{Si}$, because recent progress revive the interest in these materials; a more exhaustive review on other polyionic compounds can be found in a review [250]. The fluoro-polyanionic compounds, however, deserve a special attention, and the next chapter is devoted to them.

## Chapter 8 <br> Fluoro-polyanionic Compounds

### 8.1 Introduction

During the last decade, numerous studies have been devoted to replace oxides by materials with a polyanion-based framework that are considered as safe alternatives for the traditional oxide electrodes [1]. For instance, $M_{x}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{y}$ sulfate-based and $M_{x}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{y}$ phosphate-based compounds ( $M$ is a transition-metal ion) that house interstitial $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions such as $\mathrm{LiFe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ [2], $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ [3], $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ [4], $\mathrm{Li}_{2.5} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ [5], $\mathrm{LiVOPO}_{4}$ [6-8], and $\mathrm{LiVP}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}$ [8, 9] have all been considered as thermally stable. All these materials usually exhibit excellent stability on longterm cycling by comparison to lithium metal oxides and essentially no release of oxygen from the lattice or reactivity with the electrolyte. However, the materials were found to be poor electronic conductors [3].

The search of new cathode materials aiming to maintain a good mix of properties, with focus on electrochemical and safety parameters, has resulted in the improvement of the electrochemical performance using two strategies: (1) substitution of fluorine for oxygen or (2) fluorine coating of the active particles. As a result, fluorinated compounds display several advantages such as high voltage redox reactions, stabilization of the host lattice, protection the electrode particle surface from HF attack and electrolyte decomposition that impedes a side reaction, and easy transport of mobile $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions [10-15]. Accordingly, anion substitution is expected as an effective way to enhance the electrochemical performance of spinel and layered compounds, especially for NMC materials [16] due to the strong electronegativity of the $\mathrm{F}^{-}$anion, which will make the structure more stable. Among the metal fluorides as surface fluorination (coating) agents of oxide-based cathode particles, the most popular are $\mathrm{ZrF}_{x}$ [17], $\mathrm{AlF}_{3}$ [18], $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ [19], and LiF [20]. While these materials are treated in the first and third chapters, attention hereunder is focussed on technological developments of fluorophosphates and fluorosulfates [21-23]. The present chapter gives the state of the art in the understanding of the properties of these F -containing materials. Owing to the progress in
this field, these compounds are promising active cathode elements for the next generation of Li-ion batteries to improve the technology of the energy storage and electric transportation. This chapter is organized as follows. The preliminary considerations are dedicated to a brief recall of the energetic properties of these compounds. The second section is devoted to the structural and electrochemical properties of fluorophosphate materials. Then the fluorosulfates are treated in Sect. 8.3. Finally, some concluding remarks are given with emphasis on the quality that insures the reliability and the optimum electrochemical performance of these materials.

### 8.2 Properties of Polyanionic Compounds

According Eq. (1.5), the average voltage of an intercalation compound is directly related to the energies of the end states (charged and discharged), the voltage depends on the structure of the cathode element [24]. Several computer simulation techniques have been employed to address the voltage, diffusion and nanostructural properties of cathode materials [25, 26]. DFT +U methods have also been used recently to investigate the structural and electronic properties of both tavorite and triplite $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ polymorphs. Islam and Fisher [25] have pointed out that the difference in voltage is mainly due to the difference in the stabilities of the delithiated states $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, which can be rationalized in terms of the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}-\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ repulsion in the edge-sharing geometry of the triplite structure [27].

Considering the crystal field concept developed by Goodenough [28], the redox potential of an insertion electrode material is governed by the ionocovalency of the $M-X$ bond: the more ionic bonds, the higher potential. Thus, the redox potential strongly depends on the electronegative ion: substitution of fluorine $\mathrm{F}^{-}$for oxygen $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ results in higher potential, while the opposite is observed for sulfur. As an example, the redox potential of the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ tavorite phase is 850 mV higher than that of the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ olivine phase, because the ionicity in $\mathrm{Fe}-\mathrm{F}$ bonds is larger than that of $\mathrm{Fe}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds. A computational investigation on fluorinated-polyanionic compounds has demonstrated that the more ionic $M-\mathrm{F}$ bond and the resulting stabilization of the energy of the antibonding $3 d$ orbitals of the transition-metal ion by fluorine substitution for oxygen is the law acting on the electrochemical properties [28].

Another aspect of tuning the redox potential of an electrode material has been demonstrated by Goodenough et al. [3, 29]. They have shown that the use of polyanions $\left(\mathrm{XO}_{4}\right)^{n-}$ such as $\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)^{2-},\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)^{3-},\left(\mathrm{AsO}_{4}\right)^{3-}$, or even $\left(\mathrm{WO}_{4}\right)^{2-}$ lowers $3 d$-metals redox energy to useful levels compared to the Fermi level of the Li anode. Thus, the most attractive key point of the polyanion frameworks can be seen in the strong $X$-O covalency, which results in a decrease of the $\mathrm{Fe}-\mathrm{O}$ covalency. This inductive effect is responsible for a decrease of the redox potential in comparison to the oxides [29, 30]. The polyanion $\mathrm{PO}_{4}{ }^{3-}$ unit stabilizes the olivine structure of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ and lowers the Fermi level of the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/ 3+}$ redox couple through

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-267.jpg?height=552&width=1175&top_left_y=210&top_left_x=176)
Fig. 8.1 Energy of the redox couples of iron (a) and vanadium (b) phosphate frameworks relative to the Fermi level of metallic lithium

the Fe-O-P inductive effect which results in a higher potential for the olivine material. The discharge voltage 3.45 V is almost 650 mV higher than that of $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{Fe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ [3]. It is also 350 mV higher than that of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ [2], which is consistent with the stronger Bronsted acidity of sulfuric vs. phosphoric acid. In the case of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FeSiO}_{4}$, the lower electronegativity of Si vs. P results in a lowering of the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/ 3+}$ redox couple [31]. On the other hand, the higher thermal stability of the phospho-olivines and their lower tendency to release oxygen is explained by the strong $X$-O covalency and the rigid $\left(\mathrm{XO}_{4}\right)^{n-}$ units decreasing the safety risks. However, $A M X \mathrm{O}_{4}$ compounds and $A M\left(X \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)_{3}$ as well ( $A$ is an alkali ion) exhibit a very low electronic conductivity because of the separation between $M \mathrm{O}_{6}$ octahedra and $\mathrm{XO}_{4}$ tetrahedra that induces a large polarization effect during charge-discharge reaction [32]. Figure 8.1 illustrates the changes in redox energies relative to the Fermi level of Li for the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/ 3+}$ and $\mathrm{V}^{n+/(n+1)+}$ couples. For instance, the electrochemical insertion properties of lithium vanadium fluorophosphate, $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, indicate that the $\mathrm{V}^{3+} / \mathrm{V}^{4+}$ redox couple in $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is located at a potential around 0.3 V higher than in the lithium vanadium phosphate, $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ [32]. This property characterizes the impact of structural fluorine on the inductive effect of the $\mathrm{PO}_{4}{ }^{3-}$ polyanion. The electrochemical characteristics of various lithiated compounds with polyanionic framework are listed in Table 8.1 [32-36].

Most of these compounds crystallize in a structure similar to tavorite $\mathrm{LiFe}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$ (OH) [37]. Using high-throughput density-functional-theory calculations, Mueller et al. [38] have evaluated tavorite-structured oxyphosphates, fluorophosphates, oxysulfates, and fluorosulfates. The activation energies for lithium diffusion through the tavorite frameworks of $\mathrm{LiVO}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right), \mathrm{LiV}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right) \mathrm{F}$, and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~V}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right) \mathrm{F}$ showed that these materials are capable of reversibly inserting two lithium ions per redox active metal at very high rates.

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |

### 8.3 Fluorophosphates

Numerous inorganic material factors including the nature of the transition-metal ion and its number of valence states affect the structural and transport properties. For instance, lithium fluorophosphates glasses (LFG) formed by $\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ glass former and LiF glass modifier with addition of dopant are well known to exhibit enhancement of their conductivity and decrease of their glass transition temperature without altering the phosphate network [38, 39]. The crystallized materials with the framework-structured Li-containing fluorophosphates of $3 d$-metals, described by general formulae $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, have been explored as perspective high-voltage cathode materials for rechargeable lithium batteries [22]. The crystallographic parameters of $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(M=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni})$ compounds are listed in Table 8.2.

### 8.3.1 Fluorine-Doped LiFePO4

With the goal of increasing the voltage of the redox reaction, Liao et al. [44] were among the first groups who investigated the effects of fluorine substitution on electrochemical behavior of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} / \mathrm{C}$ composite electrode material. LiFe $\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{1-x} \mathrm{~F}_{3 x} / \mathrm{C}(x=0.01,0.05,0.1,0.2)$ was synthesized by a solid-state carbothermal reduction route at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ as dopant. F-doped $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} / \mathrm{C}$ nanoparticles were prepared either via a low-temperature hydrothermal reaction followed by high-temperature treatment at $850^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 5 h under Ar atmosphere [45, 46] or via sol-gel process using LiF [48]. Nanostructured C-LiFePO ${ }_{3.98} \mathrm{~F}_{0.02}$ composite was synthesized by an aqueous precipitation of precursor material in molten stearic acid [48]. The excessively F -substituted $\mathrm{LiFe}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{0.9} \mathrm{~F}_{0.3} / \mathrm{C}$ composite showed the most attractive high rate performance and the cycling life at high temperatures ( $T>50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) due to the fact that the more ionic $\mathrm{Fe}-\mathrm{F}$ bond stabilizes the energy of the antibonding $3 d$ orbitals of the transition-metal ion.

However, two proposals about the fluorine ion occupy were given: (1) the first one suggested that $3 \mathrm{~F}^{-}$ions replace $\mathrm{PO}_{4}{ }^{3-}$ group as a whole [44, 45], and

| Compound | Space group | $a(\AA)$ | $b(\AA)$ | $c(\AA)$ | $\alpha\left({ }^{\circ}\right)$ | $\beta\left({ }^{\circ}\right)$ | $\gamma\left({ }^{\circ}\right)$ | $V\left(\AA^{3}\right)$ | References |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | C2/c | 8.1553(2) | 8.1014(1) | 8.1160(2) | 90 | 118.089(1) | 90 | 319.00(8) | [35] |
| $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | $P \overline{1}$ | 5.1830(8) | 5.3090(6) | 8.2500(3) | 82.489(4) | 108.868(8) | 84.385(8) | 184.35(0) | [32] |
| $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | $P \overline{1}$ | 5.15510(3) | 5.3044(3) | 8.2612(4) | 108.358(5) | 108.855(6) | 98.618(5) | 1832.91(2) | [40] |
| $\mathrm{LiTiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | $P \overline{1}$ | 5.1991(2) | 5.3139(2) | 8.2428(3) | 106.985(3) | 108.262(4) | 98.655(4) | 186.10(2) | [40] |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | $C 2 / c$ | 8.2255(1) | 8.9450(1) | 8.3085(1) | 90 | 116.881(1) | 90 | 384.53(8) | [38] |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | $P \overline{1}$ | 5.3846(3) | 8.4438(3) | 5.3256(4) | 109.038(2) | 94.423(6) | 108.259(9) | 189.03(4) | [34] |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | Pbcn | 5.0550(2) | 13.5610(2) | 11.0520(3) | 90 | 90 | 90 | 858.62(1) | [41] |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | Pnma | 10.4520(2) | 6.3911(8) | 10.8840(2) | 90 | 90 | 90 | 826.40(3) | [42] |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{NiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ | Pnma | 10.4830(3) | 6.2888(8) | 10.8460(1) | 90 | 90 | 90 | 814.33(2) | [43] |


(2) the second one declared that $\mathrm{F}^{-}$could only be replaced at the oxygen sites [46-48]. Considering the second proposition, there are three nonequivalent randomly occupied O sites, namely the $\mathrm{O}(1), \mathrm{O}(2)$, and $\mathrm{O}(3)$ site, in the crystalline elementary cell of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$. As compared with the pure olivine material, its discharge capacity at $10 C$ rate was $110 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with a flat discharge voltage plateau of $3.3-3.0 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$. Lu et al. [46] reported that the length of $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds increased and those of $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds decreased due to F doping. This implies that $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$diffusion between lithiated phase and delithiated phase could be improved, due to the F -doping that weakens the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds. Fluorine ions preferably occupy specific $\mathrm{O}(2)$ oxygen sites. It seems that fluorine doping leads to additional electron density on the lithium sites indicating the formation of $\mathrm{Fe}_{\mathrm{Li}+}-\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{Fe}-}$ antisite pairs. Such fluorine doping closes the gap in the electronic structure, which results in a finite density of states at the Fermi level. Enhanced high rate performance and improved cycle stability of F-doped olivine-phosphate was also reported in more recent studies [46-48]. C-LiFePO ${ }_{3.98} \mathrm{~F}_{0.02}$ composite delivered the capacity of $164 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 10$ rate. The first principles computational results indicate that electronic properties, the lithium insertion voltage, and general electrochemical behavior, are very sensitive to the placement of fluorine ions in the structure of this compound [48]. It was suggested that $\mathrm{LiVSiO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{0.5} \mathrm{FePO}_{3.5} \mathrm{~F}_{0.5}$ lithium deinsertion causes a too large $M-\mathrm{F}$ distance (indicative of $M-\mathrm{F}$ bond breaking), being the predicted lithium insertion voltage about 0.3 V lower than that of the parent compound.

### 8.3.2 $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$

The vanadium-containing phosphate polyanion $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ has been initially proposed and described in detail by Barker et al. [32, 49-59] as a novel 4-V positive electrode materials for Li -ion batteries. Initial testing of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ has shown that this material is substantially safer than the traditional oxide materials, and therefore should also be considered as a cathode replacement for the current generation of Li ion cells [32]. $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is isostructural with the native mineral tavorite, which belongs to the lithium-bearing pegmatite family with favorite $\mathrm{LiFe}^{3+}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right) \cdot(\mathrm{OH})$, amblylgonite ( $\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}) \mathrm{AlPO}_{4} \cdot(\mathrm{~F}, \mathrm{OH})$, and montebrasite $\mathrm{LiAlPO}_{4} \cdot(\mathrm{~F}, \mathrm{OH})$ polymorphs. The tavorite phase crystallizes in a triclinic structure ( $\mathrm{P} \overline{1}$ S.G.) and its framework consists of $\mathrm{V}^{3+} \mathrm{O}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ octahedra linked by fluorine vertices forming $\left(\mathrm{V}^{3+} \mathrm{O}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}\right)_{\infty}$ chains along the $c$-axis (Fig. 8.2). The connection of these chains with corner-sharing $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ tetrahedra form an open three-dimensional lattice with wide tunnels along the $a, b$, and $c$ directions that accommodate the Li ions in two sites, i.e., $\operatorname{Li}(1)$ is five coordinated with low occupancy ( $\sim 18 \%$ ) and $\operatorname{Li}(2)$ adopts a six coordination with high occupancy ( $\sim 82 \%$ ) on the $2 i$ Wyckoff positions [60-63].

In the early work of Barker et al. [32] the synthesis of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ was made by a two-step approach involving the initial carbothermal preparation of a $\mathrm{VPO}_{4}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-271.jpg?height=357&width=1165&top_left_y=210&top_left_x=182)
Fig. 8.2 Schematic view of the tavorite structure (P1 S.G.) along the $a$-, $b$-, and $c$-crystallographic directions

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-271.jpg?height=476&width=1162&top_left_y=765&top_left_x=182)
Fig. 8.3 (a) Charge-discharge profile of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} / / \mathrm{Li}$ cell cycled at different C -rates with $1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ solution in EC-DEC ( $1: 1$ ) as electrolyte. The first charge curve (red) is shown for comparison. (b) Variation of the derivative capacity ( $-\mathrm{d} Q / \mathrm{d} V$ ) as a function of the voltage during extraction (charge) and insertion (discharge) of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions into the $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ host

intermediate using $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}, \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$, and particulate carbon (super P grade) precursors, followed by a simple LiF incorporation process. The single tavorite phase displays lattice parameters such as $a=5.1830(8) \AA, b=5.3090(6) \AA, c=8.2500$ (3) $\AA, \alpha=82.489(4)^{\circ}, \beta=108.868(8)^{\circ}, \gamma=81.385(8)^{\circ}$, and $V=184.35(0) \AA^{3}$. Preliminary electrochemical studies of the $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ cathode material demonstrate a reversible lithium extraction/insertion reaction based on the $\mathrm{V}^{3+/ 4+}$ redox couple that indicates: (1) a discharge potential centered at around 4.19 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$, (2) a two-phase reaction mechanism coupled to phase nucleation behavior, (3) a reversible specific capacity around $115 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, a performance roughly equivalent to cycling of $x=0.84$ in $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$. In Fig. 8.3a, we present the charge-discharge profile of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} / / \mathrm{Li}$ cell cycled at different $C$-rates with $1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ solution in EC-DEC (1:1) as electrolyte. The first charge curve (red) is shown for comparison. The variation of the derivative capacity ( $-\mathrm{d} Q / \mathrm{d} V$ ) as a function of the voltage during extraction (charge) and insertion (discharge) of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions into the

Fig. 8.4 Phase diagram of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(0 \leq x \leq 2)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-272.jpg?height=396&width=759&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=585)

$\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ host is shown in Fig. 8.3b. In the charge profile, the two distinct peaks with a potential separation $\sim 60 \mathrm{mV}$ correspond to the $\mathrm{V}^{3+/ 4+}$ redox couple with the two $\operatorname{Li}(1)$ and $\operatorname{Li}(2)$ site occupancies. Typical thermal response of the fully delithiated $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ phase obtained by differential scanning calorimetry shows that, with a heat flow of $\sim 205 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the safety characteristics of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is vastly superior to known oxide cathode materials (for instance $345 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ for $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ ) [58].

Taking advantage of the multivalency of vanadium ions, an additional lithium insertion reaction at around 1.8 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$was reported to be associated with the $\mathrm{V}^{3+/ 2+}$ redox couple [49, 56-58]. $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ has two redox electric potentials based on the $\mathrm{V}^{4+/ 3+}\left(\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}\right.$ reaction $)$ and $\mathrm{V}^{3+/ 2+}\left(\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}\right.$ reaction) couples, which offers the possibility of using this material for the cathode as well as anode as shown in the diagram presented in Fig. 8.4. Ellis et al. [40] have investigated the end phases combining X-ray and neutron diffraction for $\mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ prepared by chemical oxidation and reduction of the parent compound. The crystal chemistry of these end phases is listed in Table 8.2. The delithiated phase $\mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ adopts a monoclinic structure ( $C 2 / c \mathrm{~S} . \mathrm{G}$.) in which corner-shared $\left(\mathrm{V}^{4+} \mathrm{O}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}\right)$ octahedral chains are interconnected by $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ tetrahedra. The lithiated $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ phase displays the same structural change to monoclinic symmetry ( $C 2 / c$ S.G.) in which lithium ions occupy two sites, $\mathrm{Li}(1)$ and $\mathrm{Li}(2)$, filled with equal probability [64]. The redox activity between the two $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ compositions is very facile and occurs with an $8 \%$ change in volume and displays a stable specific capacity of $145 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [34]. More recently, Ellis et al. [40] and Plashnitsa et al. [65] and have utilized $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ as both cathode and anode for fabrication of a symmetric Li -ion $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} / / \mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ cell with a nonflammable ionic liquid $\mathrm{LiBF}_{4}-\mathrm{EMIBF}_{4}$ electrolyte. This symmetrical cell displays a potential window of 2.4 V with a reversible specific capacity of $130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and has shown to be stable and safe at high temperature up to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

The incorporation of aluminum into the $\mathrm{LiV}_{1-y} \mathrm{Al}_{y} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ framework prepared by the two-step carbothermal reduction method has generated some interesting properties: (1) an almost linear decrease of the discharge capacity on the $\mathrm{V}^{3+/ 4+}$ redox couple with Al substitution, (2) a lower polarizability, (3) a gradual upshift in the $\mathrm{V}^{3+/ 4+}$ redox peak of 90 mV , and (4) the ratio of the two charge plateaus remains relatively constant for $0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq 0.25$ [59].

The reactivity of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ charged at $C / 5$ rate with $1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ solution in EC:DEC electrolyte was tested by accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) at high temperature. The self-heating rate ( $\mathrm{d} T / \mathrm{d} t$ ) of the CTR-synthesized $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ reacting with electrolyte is lower or about the same as that of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ olivine over the entire test temperature range $50-350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for powders of identical surface area $\left(15 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right)$ [66, 67]. Ma et al. [68] have investigated the effects of oxidation on the structure and the performance of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ as a cathode material. Two two-phase structural evolutions were detected upon $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$electrochemical extraction at average potentials at 4.26 and 4.30 V corresponding to the continuous transformation of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{0.82} \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{VPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ in the first charge process, while the discharge process occurs without the appearance of the intermediate phase. Changes in the Li environment and ion mobility in the tavorite $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ lattice have been observed by multinuclear solid-state ${ }^{6 / 7} \mathrm{Li}$ and ${ }^{31} \mathrm{P}$ NMR spectroscopies on chemically and electrochemically delithiated samples [68]. The ionic conductivity determined from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of tavoritestructured $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$. is $\sigma_{1}=0.6 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature with the activation energy of $E_{\mathrm{a}}=0.85 \mathrm{eV}$ determined from the Arrhenius plot [69]. These values differ from those of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \sigma_{1}=8 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ and $E_{a}=0.99 \mathrm{eV}$ reported by Recham et al. [35].

Various vanadium-based fluorophosphates were evaluated as cathode material for Li-ion batteries [35, 70-83]. Wang et al. [81] reported the electrochemical performance of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} / \mathrm{C}$ composite prepared by heating a precursor, obtained through ball milling with slurry of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ as reacting agent and carbon source, $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4}, \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{VO}_{3}$ and LiF . This material shows specific discharge capacities of 151 and $102 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $0.1 C$ and $10 C$, respectively, in the voltage range of $3.0-4.4 \mathrm{~V}$ and displays a discharge coulombic efficiency $90.4 \%$ at 10C-rate after 50 cycles. Reddy et al. [83] have examined the long-term behavior in the potential range $3.0-4.5 \mathrm{~V}$ at $0.92 C$-rate of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ prepared by CTR method. The capacity degrades slowly over 800-1260 cycles and the total loss is $\sim 14 \%$.

### 8.3.3 $\mathrm{LiMPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Ti})$

Following the discovery of Barker et al. [46], who described the preparation of $\mathrm{Li} M_{1-y} M^{\prime}{ }_{y} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ materials ( $M$ and $M^{\prime}$ are transition metals having a +3 oxidation state) several attempts were made to substitute transition-metal ions of the first raw for vanadium such as Fe and Ti . DFT calculations using plane-wave methods were performed for $\mathrm{Li}_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}, \mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, and $M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(M=\mathrm{V}, \mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni})$ to address their feasibility as high-voltage cathode materials ( $>3.5 \mathrm{~V}$ relative to Li metal) for Li-ion batteries [84]. Average open-circuit voltages of 4.9, 5.2 and 5.3 V were calculated for $\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{C}$, and Ni , respectively.
$\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ synthesized by CRT method or by a hydrogen reduction reaction crystallizes in the triclinic structure (P. $\mathrm{P} . \mathrm{G}$.) with lattice parameters $a=5.1528 \AA$, $b=5.3031 \AA, c=8.4966 \AA, \alpha=68.001^{\circ}, \beta=68.164^{\circ}, \gamma=81.512^{\circ}$ and cell volume $V=183.89 \AA^{3} . \mathrm{LiCrPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ prepared by the same methods crystallizes with the $\mathrm{P} \overline{1}$ space group with lattice parameters $a=4.996 \AA, b=5.308 \AA, c=6.923 \AA$, $\alpha=88.600^{\circ}, \beta=100.81^{\circ}, \gamma=88.546^{\circ}$ and cell volume $V=164.54 \AA^{3}$. Recham et al. [35] experienced the growth of crystalline $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ by either solid-state or ionothermal techniques. For the solid-state synthesis, the mixture of $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{FeF}_{3}$ was heated at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 24 h inside a platinum tube. For the ionothermal method, similar mixture stabilized with an ionic liquid and triflate produced nanostructured particles ( $\sim 20 \mathrm{~nm}$ ). This material shows a reversible specific capacity of $\sim 145 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with the average potential at 2.8 V corresponding to the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ redox couples. However, $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ cannot be oxidized, which limits its use in $\mathrm{Li}-i o n$ batteries [21]. The phase transition and electrochemistry of the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}- \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ have been studied by Ramesh et al. [34]. The fully lithium inserted phase, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, adopts a triclinic tavorite-type structure that is closely related to the parent phase (see Table 8.2). Despite the presence of two crystallographic distinct Fe sites ( $1 a$ and $1 c$ Wyckoff positions) in the $\mathrm{P} \overline{1}$ lattice, the electrochemical features of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ show an overall potential at 3 V with a reversible capacity of 0.96 Li corresponding to a capacity of $145 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$.

Ti-based $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is another tavorite structure built by distorted $\mathrm{TiO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ octahedra linked by fluorine ions, which was synthesized by either solid-state or ionothermal techniques [35]. Single-phase $\mathrm{LiTiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ powders prepared at low temperature ( $260{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) has lattice parameters $a=5.1991 \AA, b=5.3139 \AA$, $c=8.2428 \AA, \alpha=106.985^{\circ}, \beta=108.262^{\circ}, \gamma=98.655^{\circ}$ and cell volume $V=186.10 \AA^{3}$. Li extraction/insertion reaction from/into the $\mathrm{Li}_{1+x} \mathrm{TiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ framework occurs in the range $-0.5 \leq x \leq 0.5$ with the appearance of two pseudo-plateaus centered at 2.9 and 1.8 V corresponding to the $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$ and $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ti}^{2+}$ redox couples, respectively. However, due to the sensitivity of electrochemical features upon mild modifications of synthesis, Barpanda and Tarascon [21] conclude that $\mathrm{LiTiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is a poor cathode material for battery applications.

### 8.3.4 $\quad \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni})$

Fluorophosphates of general formula $A_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(A=\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}$ and $M=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Co}$, $\mathrm{Ni})$ crystallize in three structure types, which differ in the connectivity of $\left(\mathrm{MO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}\right)$ octahedra: face-shared ( $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ ), edge-shared ( $\mathrm{Li}_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}, M=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni}$ ) and corner-shared $\left(\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{MnPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}\right)[42,83]$. The $\mathrm{Li}_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(M=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Ni})$ crystallize in three different structures types; triclinic (tavorite) and two-dimensional orthorhombic (Pbcn S.G.) and tunnel-like monoclinic ( $P 2_{1} / n$ S.G.) [85]. In their prior work, Ellis et al. [41] have shown that $A_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(A=\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Li})$ could serve as a cathode in Li-ion cells. This compound possesses facile two-dimensional pathways
for $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$motion, and the structural modifications on redox reactions are minimal with a volume change of only $3.8 \%$ that contributes to the absence of distinct two-phase behavior. Single-phase $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ was prepared by ion exchange of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ in $1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiBr}^{a c e t o n i t r i l e ~ s o l u t i o n . ~ T h e ~ m a t e r i a l ~} \mathrm{LiNaFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Na}$ ratio $1: 1)$ was obtained by reducing $\mathrm{NaFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ with LiI in acetonitrile for $6 \mathrm{~h} . \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ crystallizes in the orthorhombic structure (Pbcn S.G.) with lattice parameters $a=5.055 \AA, b=13.561 \AA, c=11.0526 \AA, \beta=90^{\circ}$, and cell volume $V=858.62 \AA^{3}$. The open-circuit voltage is lower than the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ olivine ( 3.0 V vs. 3.45 V ); $80 \%$ of the theoretical capacity ( $135 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) is attainable on the first oxidation cycle. Recently, the same group [33] reported the crystal structure and electrochemical properties of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ fluorophosphates ( $M=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni}$ ) synthesized by solid-state and hydrothermal synthetic routes. New forms of lithium fluorophosphate cathode materials have been reported such as $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ obtained by cycling the orthorhombic $\mathrm{NaLiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ phase (Pnma S.G.) in an electrochemical lithium cell [85], single phase sub-stoichiometric nanocrystalline $\mathrm{Li}_{1-x} \mathrm{Fe}_{1-y} M_{y} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ ( $M=\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}$ ) [86], and $\mathrm{Li}_{2-x} \mathrm{Na}_{x} \mathrm{Fe}\left[\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{F}$ tavorite structure synthesized by ion exchange using LiBr in ethanol [87].

### 8.3.5 $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni})$

Several groups reported on high voltage electrochemical performance of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{NiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ [86]. Both $\mathrm{LiCoPO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ (isostructural with $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{NiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ ) crystallize in the orthorhombic system (S.G. Pnma, Z=8). Nevertheless, there are remarkable differences between the structures from a crystallographic point of view. $\mathrm{LiCoPO}_{4}$ has $\mathrm{CoO}_{6}$ octahedra, $\mathrm{LiO}_{6}$ octahedra and $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ tetrahedra. In contrast, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ has $\mathrm{CoO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ octahedra instead of $\mathrm{CoO}_{6}$ octahedra. In addition, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ has two kinds of Li sites, $4 c$ and $8 d$ [43]. It was confirmed that $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is a new class of $5-\mathrm{V}$ cathode materials similar to $\mathrm{LiCoPO}_{4}$ [13, 42, 89]. Dumont Botto et al. [85] have pointed out that, contrary to the Na phases which are quite simple to obtain, the synthesis of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} M \mathrm{PO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ remains difficult and requires either the ion exchange of the Na-counterparts or a lengthy solid-state reaction (at least $10-\mathrm{h}$ heat treatment) [83]. In the search of finding unconventional way to prepare $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, a shorter reaction down to 9 min was done by spark plasma sintering, which favors the formation of submicrometric particles ( $0.8 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ ).

A considerable theoretical upper limit of approximately $310 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ is expected for $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{NiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$. The theoretical estimation of the intercalation voltage of $\sim 4.9 \mathrm{~V}$ for $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ cathode [88] is in good agreement with the voltage plateau observed at ca. 5 V [42]. A fault of both $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and lithiated cobalt phosphate is a high irreversible capacity (especially in the first cycles), which is related to decomposition of electrolyte at high anodic potentials. Experimentally, $\mathrm{LiNiPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ discharge voltage is demonstrated to be close to 5.3 V [88]. Khasanova
et al. [89] have investigated the electrochemical performance and structural properties of the high-voltage cathode material $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$. The cyclic voltammetry and coulometry under potential step mode in the voltage range $3.0-5.1 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. Li revealed a structural transformation at potentials above 4.8 V . This transformation occurring upon Li -extraction appears to be irreversible: the subsequent Li -insertion does not result in restoration of the initial structure, but takes place within a new "modified" framework. According to the structure refinement, this modification involves the mutual rotations of $\left(\mathrm{CoO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}_{2}\right)$ octahedra and $\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$ tetrahedra accompanied by the considerable unit cell expansion, which is expected to enhance the Li transport upon subsequent cycling. The new framework demonstrates a reversible Li-insertion/extraction in a solid-solution regime with stabilized discharge capacity at around $60 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ is prepared by a two step solid state method, followed by the application of wet coating containing various amounts of $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$. Among the samples, the $5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ coated $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ material shows the best performance with an initial discharge capacity of up to $144 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ within the voltage range of $2-5.2 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$at current density $10 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}{ }^{-1}$ [83]. XRD patterns of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ electrodes at different stages of cycling during the first charge-discharge cycle were investigated by Wang et al. [83]. Electrodes discharged from 5.0 V maintain the same trend as the fresh electrodes; however, a slight difference indicates that the structural relaxation of the framework of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ occurs at a voltage greater than 5.0 V , which is consistent with the CV measurements.

### 8.3.6 $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ Hybrid-ion Cathode

The sodium vanadium fluorophosphate materials have demonstrated reversible Li-ion insertion behavior [87, 88]. The $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ phase prepared using a solid-state carbothermal reduction (CTR) approach involving the precursors $\mathrm{VPO}_{4}$ and NaF crystallizes in the tetragonal space group $P 4_{2} / m n m$ with lattice parameters $a=0.0388$ (3) $\AA, c=10.8482$ (4) $\AA$ and $V=888.94$ (6) $\AA^{3}$. The framework structure is best described in terms of $\left(\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8} \mathrm{~F}_{3}\right)$ bi-octahedra and $\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)$ tetrahedral. The bi-octahedra are linked by one of the fluorine atoms, whereas the oxygen atoms are all interconnected through the $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ units. Electrochemical properties of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Na}_{3-x} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ carried out in a metallic Li half-cell in the potential range $3.0-4.6 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$reveal that mobile Na is rapidly exchanged for Li (Fig. 8.5). The voltage response corresponded to the reversible cycling of two alkali ions per formula unit. The associated specific capacity was around $120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, at an average discharge voltage of around 4.1 V vs. Li metal anode [88]. The differential capacity data for the cell cycled at $\mathrm{C} / 20$ rate for charge and discharge is shown in Fig. 8.5a. Thus, when fully charged to 4.6 V vs. Li , a cathode composition approximating to $\mathrm{NaV}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ is produced, a condition in which all the vanadium has been oxidized to $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$. Such a hybrid-ion cathode was used in Li -ion battery with

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-277.jpg?height=510&width=1167&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=180)
Fig. 8.5 Differential capacity $(\mathrm{d} Q / \mathrm{d} V)$ curves of $(\mathbf{a}) \mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ cell and (b) the first and fifth cycle for a $\mathrm{Li}_{4 / 3} \mathrm{Ti}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4} / / \mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ hybrid-ion cell. The electrolyte comprised a $1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ solution in ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate ( $2: 1 \mathrm{wt} \%$ )

Fig. 8.6 Variation of the $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{V}$ ratio in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Na}_{3-x} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ as a function of cycle number showing the Li-Na exchange process. Insert presents the change during the first cycle
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-277.jpg?height=536&width=669&top_left_y=903&top_left_x=673)

$\mathrm{Li}_{4 / 3} \mathrm{Ti}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as Li source. The differential capacity ( $-\mathrm{d} Q / \mathrm{d} V$ ) curves of the first and fifth cycle for $\mathrm{Li}_{4 / 3} \mathrm{Ti}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4} / / \mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ hybrid-ion cell cycle are displayed in Fig. 8.5b. During the initial charge of the cell sodium ions are extracted from the $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ cathode. These results show that the $-\mathrm{d} Q / \mathrm{d} V$ response for the first cycle is broad and symmetrical, confirming the energetic reversibility of the cell chemistry with a capacity of $\sim 120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. However, sodium insertion into $\mathrm{Li}_{4 /} { }_{3} \mathrm{Ti}_{5 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is not favored. On subsequent cycles, the $2.28 / 2.23 \mathrm{~V}$ redox peaks shift by 100 mV to lower potential due to the $\mathrm{Li}^{+} / \mathrm{Na}^{+}$ionic exchange that is now characterized by sharp redox peaks. Thus, change to a predominate lithium insertion mechanism occurred at the cathode [88]. Variation of the $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{V}$ ratio in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Na}_{3-x} \mathrm{~V}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{3}$ as a function of cycle number showing the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Na}$ exchange process is presented in Fig. 8.6. As a result, the almost entire $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Na}$ exchange was completed after ten cycles. Similar results for $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ have been reported by

Ellis et al. [31]. By ion-exchange reaction, the entire Na content in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ may be replaced with Li to yield $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, which exhibits a slightly higher redox potential than the parent compound because of the more electronegative nature of Li compared to Na [43]. Later on, the same authors have shown that $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} / /$ graphite Li-ion cell delivered a capacity $130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and an average discharge voltage of 4.06 V . Long-term cycling at $C / 5$ rate displays a coulombic efficiency around $90 \%$ after 500 cycles [48].

### 8.3.7 Other Fluorophosphates

To follow the initial investigation of Barker et al., several compounds of the same structure have been proposed. Park et al. [89] have prepared the $\mathrm{Li}_{1.1} \mathrm{Na}_{0.4} \mathrm{VPO}_{4.8} \mathrm{~F}_{0.8}$ phase from the pseudo-layered structure $\mathrm{Na}_{1.5} \mathrm{VPO}_{5} \mathrm{~F}_{0.5}$ using an ion-exchange process between $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$in 1-hexanol at its boiling point ( $160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) under reflux with LiBr as the lithium source. The lattice is made up of $\mathrm{VO}_{5} \mathrm{~F}$ octahedral and $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ tetrahedra units, where two $\mathrm{VO}_{5} \mathrm{~F}$ are linked by a bridging fluorine ion to form $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{10} \mathrm{~F}$ bi-octahedron. These units are repeatedly connected in the $a b$ plane via $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ units sharing oxygen to construct an open framework having a layer-like spacing into which $\mathrm{Na}^{+} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions are inserted [90]. This compound shows reversible extraction and insertion of $\sim 1.1 \mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions at an ideal 4 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$to provide a capacity of $156 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with coulombic efficiency $98 \%$ after 100 cycles at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Among fluorophosphates used as electrode materials, the layered structure $\mathrm{Li}_{5} M\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ with $M=\mathrm{V}, \mathrm{Cr}$ operates as $4-\mathrm{V}$ cathodes for lithium-ion batteries [91, 92], but the capacity is lower than $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$.

### 8.4 Fluorosulfates

Recently, the concept of inductive effect have been applied to replace $\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)^{3-}$ for $\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)^{2-}$ in polyanionic cathode materials [35, 36, 93-119]. Table 8.3 summarizes the structural properties of some fluorosulfate compounds. The fluorosulfates $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ constitute a wide family showing a good mix of properties, especially, both electrochemical and safety issues. However, we notice that the electroactive compounds appeared only in 2010 after the synthesis of newest member of the tavorite family $\mathrm{LiFe}^{2+}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right) \mathrm{F}$ [36]. For instance, a simple substitution in Nasicon $\mathrm{Li}_{x} M_{3}\left(\mathrm{XO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ networks increases the redox potential by 800 mV independently of the $3 d$ transition-metal ion [2]. Recent review by Rousse and Tarascon [119] deals with the crystal chemistry and structural-electrochemical relationship of new fluorosulfate polyanionic $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ electrode materials. The lithiated fluorosulfates present an interesting family from the view point of crystal chemistry with the three main types of structure that depend on the nature of the transition-metal ion:

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-280.jpg?height=437&width=1025&top_left_y=210&top_left_x=252)
Fig. 8.7 Schematic representation of the crystal chemistry of the triplite (a) compared with the tavorite phase (b)

tavorite ( $M=\mathrm{Fe}$ ), triplite ( $M=\mathrm{Mn}$ ), or sillimanite ( $M=\mathrm{Zn}$ ). Figure 8.7 shows the difference between the tavorite ( $P \overline{1} \mathrm{~S} . \mathrm{G}$. ) and triplite ( $P \overline{1} \mathrm{~S} . \mathrm{G}$. ) structure.

### 8.4.1 $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$

Single phase tavorite $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ cannot be prepared by typical solid-state methods because its low thermodynamic stability imposes that the crystallization must be effected at low ( $<400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) temperatures in hydrophobic ionic liquids [36, 100]. Tripathi et al. [99] reported the easy synthesis of $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ by reaction in hydrophobic tetraethylene glycol at $220^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to give a highly electrochemically active material. Reversible Li insertion is easy because of the close structural similarity of the lattice with that of the tavorite-type monoclinic $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}\left(C 2_{1} / c\right.$ S.G.). $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ can be readily oxidized to produce the empty host $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ but the volume contraction (from 182.4 to $164.0 \AA^{3}$ ) is greater than that of the olivine $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ framework [99]. The temperature dependence of the magnetic susceptibility gives evidence of a transition to long-range antiferromagnetic order at $T_{\mathrm{N}}=100 \mathrm{~K}$ in $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, while such ordering appears at 25 K in $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ [108]. This large increase of $T_{\mathrm{N}}$ upon delithiation is not only linked to the change of valence of the iron ions, since it is not observed in the case of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ for instance. Instead, it gives evidence of the shortening of the bonding path responsible for the superexchange interactions and is thus another evidence of the large contraction of the lattice. An ionothermal route (soft chemistry) has been developed for the synthesis of fluorosulfates [35, 100], in which the nucleation is facilitated by ionic liquids decomposing at temperatures $\sim 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. In such a synthesis route the $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ monohydrate is employed because of the structural similarity with the $\mathrm{LiMgSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ tavorite [93]. The galvanostatic cycling of ionothermal synthesized $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ at $C / 10$ rate shows a reversible capacity $130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ involving the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+/ 3+}$ redox reaction at 3.6 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$[35]. Note that the cell voltage is

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-281.jpg?height=475&width=1167&top_left_y=210&top_left_x=180)
Fig. 8.8 (a) Charge-discharge profiles and (b) derivative $-\mathrm{d} x / \mathrm{d} V$ for the tavorite and triplite $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ polymorphs

enhanced by 150 mV over $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$. Ultra-rapid microwave synthesis of triplite $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ has been performed by Tripathi et al. [120]. Using the solid-state reaction method, the triplite was obtained upon long annealing at $320^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in water-containing autoclaves; after 6 days the complete tavorite $\rightarrow$ triplite conversion was done [110]. The Fe triplite intercalates Li at 3.9 V , which is 0.3 V higher than its ordered tavorite analogue, attributable to longer $\mathrm{Fe}-\mathrm{O}$ bond length [113]. The origin of the voltage difference between the tavorite and the triplite phase has been discussed by Ben-Yahia et al. [113] from results of DFT +U calculations. The voltage increase originates from the difference in the anionic networks of the two polymorphs, due to the change in the electrostatic repulsions induced by the configuration of the fluorine atoms around the transition-metal cations. The potential difference between the two $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ polymorphs is illustrated in Fig. 8.8.

Recently, Ati et al. [110] have discussed the nucleation of the triplite phase of $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ using X-ray diffraction and TEM studies. Besides preparing triplite $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ from dry precursors, it was found that this phase could be obtained from the tavorite via a heating process at $320^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for few days. Sobkowiak et al. [118] demonstrated the dependence of the electrochemical features of the tavorite $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ on the synthesis conditions. The importance of the surface chemistry has been pointed out and the optimized cycling performance can be achieved by removing the unwanted residues and applying a conducting polymer coating such as PEDOT film.

### 8.4.2 $\mathrm{LiMSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Mn})$

The redox potential of the fluorosulfates $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}(M=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Mn})$ are expected to exhibit redox potentials of $4.25,4.95$, and 5.25 V , respectively. Barpanda et al. [100] succeeded in preparing the $\mathrm{Li}\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{1-x} M_{x}\right) \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ solid solutions only
when we used the corresponding monohydrate solid solutions as precursors, hence providing further evidence for the topotacticity of the synthesis reaction. None of these fluorosufates was shown to present any electrochemical activity up to 5 V . Evidently, $\mathrm{Co}^{2+} / \mathrm{Co}^{3+}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}^{3+}$, and $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ redox reactions do not occur within the explored galvanostatic cycling potential window, contrary to the case of the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ redox reaction. Subsequently, the electrochemical activity of pure $\mathrm{Li} M \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ phases were tested at different rates ( $C / 2-C / 10$ ) by cycling up to 5 V using an aluminum plunger for the cathode. Therefore, even at such high voltages prone to electrolyte oxidation/decomposition, the activity of the corresponding $M^{2+} / M^{3+}$ redox couples did not be triggered. Among the Co-based polyanionic insertion compounds, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CoP}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}$ [121] is considered as a 4.9 V cathode. This pyrophosphate crystallizes in the monoclinic structure ( $P 2_{1} / c$ S.G.), in which Li occupies five sites; two are tetrahedally coordinated, one forms bipyramidal sites, and two Li share them occupancy with Co bipyramids. The material synthesized using a two-step solid-state method delivered a discharge capacity of ca. $80 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 20$ rate. Sillimanite-structured $\mathrm{LiZnSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ was produced by low temperature synthesis ( $<300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ). The $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion conductivity has been enhanced by a monolayer of ionic liquid grafting [115]. On the other hand, it has been pointed out that the $\mathrm{LiMnSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ polymorph is electrochemically inactive [103]. However, the effect of Zn substitution in the $\mathrm{LiFe}_{1-y} \mathrm{Zn}_{y} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ forms an electrochemically active solid solution. The Fe-rich phase is obtained in the triplite structure, while the Zn-rich phase crystallizes in the sillimanite structure. The redox potentials are observed at 3.6 and 3.9 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$for the $\mathrm{LiFe}_{0.8} \mathrm{Zn}_{0.2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ (sillimanite) and $\mathrm{LiFe}_{0.9} \mathrm{Zn}_{0.1} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ (triplite), respectively. In the same way, while $\mathrm{LiMnSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ that crystallizes in the triplite phase is inactive, $\mathrm{LiFe}_{1-x} \mathrm{Mn}_{x} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ can be electrochemically active. Fe-rich solid solutions ( $0<x<0.2$ ) tavorite polymorphs have a redox activity at 3.6 V close to that of $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ and characteristic of the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{3}+$ redox potential in the tavorite structure, with a standard two-phase voltage-composition curve [102]. On the other hand, Fe -rich $\mathrm{LiFe}_{1-x} \mathrm{Mn}_{x} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ triplite solid solution polymorphs show a redox activity at 3.9 V . This value characteristic of the $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ redox potential in the triplite structure is large. Unfortunately only the iron shows an activity, so that the capacity decreases linearly with $x\left(120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\right.$ for $x=0.2$ [21]).

### 8.5 Concluding Remarks

The combination high electronegativity of F with the inductive effect of polyanion allows for the tuning of the redox potential of many fluorine-based polyanionic compounds in the electrolytic window, which make them promising electrodes for Li -ion batteries. Among them $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ can deliver a capacity of $145-150 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, which is comparable to the capacity delivered by $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$, but the operating voltage ( 4.1 V ) is larger than that of the olivine ( 3.45 V ). The $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} / / \mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ cell works at 2.4 V with a reversible capacity $130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and is safe at $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$,
which is comparable to the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} / \mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ that also works at this temperature [122]. However, the performance of $\mathrm{LiVPO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ at high C -rates is smaller than that of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$, since the capacity reduces to $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $10 C$ in the best case (preparation by the CRT method). Even at $1 C$ a capacity loss of $14 \%$ is observed between 800 and 1260 cycles, while that of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ is stable under such condition. Among the other fluorophosphates that we have reviewed, the best results are obtained with $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$, which, however cannot be oxidized. Again, the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FePO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ reaction gives a reversible capacity of $145 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, but it takes place at an overall potential at 3 V only. An ideal 4 V -battery is obtained with $\mathrm{Li}_{1.1} \mathrm{Na}_{0.4} \mathrm{VPO}_{4.8} \mathrm{~F}_{0.8}$, providing a capacity of $156 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with coulombic efficiency $98 \%$ after 100 cycles at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The corresponding energy density is thus better than that of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ owing to the larger operating voltage. Again, however, the advantage of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ will be its high-rate performance that allows for higher power densities, and the cycling life that exceeds 30000 cycles at room temperature [122].

Among the fluorosulfates, $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ in the triplite phase is promising, owing to the high $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ redox potential at 3.9 V , but the kinetics is very sluggish, and only a small fraction of the lithium can be extracted so far. The voltage is decreased to 3.6 V in the tavorite phase, but then the capacity is raised to $130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and this smaller capacity with respect to $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ is compensated by the larger voltage. The capacity of $\mathrm{LiFeSO}_{4} \mathrm{~F}$ tavorite, however, decreases at $C$-rate lager than $1 C$, and this cathode does not operate au $10 C$ rate so far.

At present time, the fluorophosphates and fluorosulfates cannot compete in energy density with lamellar compounds, and cannot compete in power density and cycling life with $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{LiFe} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$. It should be noted, however, that these materials have been studied for Li-ion battery applications only recently, while it took about 15 years to optimize $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{LiFe} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ and bring it to the position of winning a part of the market. In addition, the structural relationship between the precursor and the final product underlying the topotactic reaction makes possible the synthesis of many fluorophosphates and sulfates, and many of them have still to be discovered. Therefore, the research in this field will be very active in the years to come, justified by the fact that this family of materials is promising for applications in electrochemical energy storage.
