## Chapter 9 <br> Disordered Compounds

### 9.1 Introduction

Until now, the materials investigated to find suitable intercalation host structures for the positive electrode of electrochemical generators with an alkali metal or silver anode have been essentially crystalline structures. Yet the discovery in the 1950s of the semiconducting properties of phosphorus pentoxide-based glasses [1,2] has opened the area of amorphous and disordered semiconductors. The amorphous material structures were considered merely as "accidents" until only recently. Today, however, they are studied in their own right due to certain interesting characteristics related to the disordered state. The higher capacity of the amorphous over crystalline materials, in $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$, for example, has been pointed out by Whittingham et al. [3]. This is presumably associated with either the more open lattice in amorphous compounds or the disordered framework that prevents the decomposition in some materials. The energy-storage capacity of lithium insertion in amorphous materials is very high, and some of them are receiving increasing attention. An intercalation reaction is topotactic in nature, the structure of the host being changed only by atomic displacements; the reaction does not involve diffusive rearrangement of the host atoms. As an example, for $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{MoS}_{3}$, the initial energy density on discharge is $1.0 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$; this may be compared with 0.48 and $0.8 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ for crystalline $\mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$, respectively. The counterpart of the amorphous state is the low mobility of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$-ions in the framework, which is a limitation for high current densities. However, several examples have shown that the amorphous phase of cathode active materials can be used for improvement of cell rechargeability [4-9]. A typical example developed by Sakurai and Yamaki [8] is a $x \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}-(1-x) \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ glassy material, but despite good discharge-charge characteristics, a low rate of the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$-ion transport has been reported. In the case of $\mathrm{Li}_{1.211} \mathrm{Mo}_{0.467} \mathrm{Cr}_{0.3} \mathrm{O}_{2}$, the high electrochemical performance is due to the facile lithium diffusion in the disordered phase; Lee et al. [10] reported that this unexpected behavior is due to percolation of a certain type of active diffusion channels

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-291.jpg?height=451&width=925&top_left_y=216&top_left_x=302)
Fig. 9.1 Comparison between structures (a) crystalline of quartz and (b) $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ glass

in the disordered Li-rich framework. Nano-silicon-based disordered carbon composites prepared by mechanical milling and heat-treated at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ have been examined as anodes of lithium cells showing the charge-discharge capacity $548 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [11].

When we evoke disordered or amorphous materials, the first question is: how to define them? The classical definition is "an amorphous material is a solid in which there is no long-range order of the positions of the atoms" (Fig. 9.1). In principle, this class of materials does not display X-ray diffraction patterns, but other techniques such as FTIR and Raman spectroscopy, NMR, ESR, etc. are used to analyze the structure at the short-range scale. As an example, the structure of $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ is such that the tetrahedral formed by the $\mathrm{SiO}_{4}$ group must touch each other at their corners, but can do so at widely varying angles. The result of the flexibility in the bridge bonds is that $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$, while it has many crystalline phases, can easily form glasses. However, amorphous materials have their own characteristics and exhibit new properties after intercalation for two reasons: the modification of the pathways for ions in the host lattice and the change in the electronic structure with the appearance of new states and/or tail of the electronic bands in the energy gap. Since the opencircuit voltage of a Li cell is the difference between Fermi energies, this kind of situation explains the lower $V_{\text {oc }}(\mathrm{a})$ of an amorphous material compared with respect to that of the crystalline phase, $V_{\text {oc }}$ (c) (Fig. 9.2).

The aim of this chapter is to present a selection of appropriate disordered materials and to illustrate their properties upon lithium intercalation. This chapter is organized in six sections as follows. First, in Sect. 9.2, we show an interesting material of the transition-metal dichalcogenide group $\left(\mathrm{MoS}_{2}\right)$. Sections 9.3-9.6 are devoted to transition-metal oxides such as $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}, \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}, \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$; polycrystalline glasses and thin films are examined. Finally, we treat the physicochemical properties of disordered spinel structures $\left(\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}\right)$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-292.jpg?height=473&width=988&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=255)
Fig. 9.2 Electronic band structure of crystalline semiconductor (SC) (a) and amorphous SC (b) vs. Li metal. As the open-circuit voltage is given by the difference of the Fermi's energy, amorphous SC displays lower open circuit voltage, $V_{\mathrm{oc}}(\mathrm{a})$ than the crystalline phase, $V_{\mathrm{oc}}(\mathrm{c})$

### 9.2 Disordered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$

The electrochemical properties of crystalline molybdenum disulfide ( $\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ ) have been early reported [3,12,13]. The specific energy density of a $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ cell is very low, about $100 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{1}$, for the utilization as a primary cell. Haering et al. [14] have discovered that a lithium molybdenum disulfide compound exhibits several distinct stages of operation when used as a cathode in a battery having a lithium anode. In this case, the cell is reversible and the specific energy density is twice. The electrochemical properties of a disordered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}\right)$ phase has been reported by Jacobson et al. [15] and Julien et al. [16, 17]. It has been demonstrated, that by including such a material in a lithium cell, it is possible to increase the specific energy density for its possible application in a high-rate rechargeable batteries.

A highly disordered sample of $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ was prepared by heat treatment of crystalline $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ (Ventron) at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 4 h under 1 Pa pressure. X-ray diffraction patterns show an intense but rather broad diffraction (002) line. The structure is a highly folded but disordered stacking pattern of layered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ with a tendency to grow in-plane and weak tendency for the layers to stack. The broadness of the diffracted lines manifests the high disorder lattice of the sample. Figure 9.3 shows the temperature dependence of the electrical resistivity of $\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$. The room temperature resistivity is $0.3 \Omega \mathrm{~cm}$ which is a low value compared with $1.25 \Omega \mathrm{~cm}$ of c-MoS ${ }_{2}$. Similar influence of the disorder on electrical parameter has been reported on sputtered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ films [18]. The difference between electrical characteristics demonstrates that the transport mechanism is driven by the scattering at inter-crystallite boundaries in d- $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$. The band-bending at grain boundaries, which leads to the formation of potential barriers, is responsible for the low resistivity of disordered samples.

The typical discharge curve for electrochemical cell containing d-MoS ${ }_{2}$ is shown in Fig. 9.4. An initial $V_{\mathrm{oc}}=2.4 \mathrm{~V}$ was observed which dropped continuously to

Fig. 9.3 Electrical conductivity of (a) disordered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ compared with (b) crystalline $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-293.jpg?height=593&width=764&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=580)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-293.jpg?height=914&width=1015&top_left_y=889&top_left_x=257)
Fig. 9.4 Discharge profiles of several $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ cells as a function $x$ of Li concentration in $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$. Phases I and II are the 2 H - and 1T-polytype ( $\beta-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ ) of crystalline $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$, respectively. Phase III is a thin film grown by rf-sputtering and phase IV is the disordered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ prepared by heat treatment of crystalline phase at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 4 h under vacuum. Insert shows the incremental capacity of the crystalline phase

1.1 V at $\mathrm{Li}_{3.0} \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$. For comparison, the open circuit voltage (OCV) of the same cell with crystalline $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ cathode is also shown. An abrupt change in OCV vs. $x$ curve at $x \approx 1$ is the indication of the phase transformation discussed above. The constant part of the curve reflects the two-phase region of the material. The disordered phase of $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ shows significantly higher storage charge-capacity compared with that of the crystalline form. The discharge curve of $\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ resembles the $\beta$-phase discharge curve described by Haering et al. [14]. We remark that the discharge is not rigorously smooth, since small plateaus appear at 1.9, 1.7, 1.5 , and 1.3 V. However, in the compositional range $0.1 \leq x \leq 3$, the discharge curve can be fit by the linear expression:

$$
E(\text { Volt })=E^{*}-k x,
$$

where $E^{*}=1.85 \mathrm{~V}$ and $k=9 \mathrm{RT} / F$ have been obtained for a $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{d}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ cell, where $R$ is the ideal gas constant, and $F$ the Faraday constant. Note also that the two characteristic plateaus of the OCV vs. $x$ curve observed at 1.1 and 0.5 V for the $2 \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ crystalline samples are not observed with d- $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$. This result shows that the disorder stabilizes the solid solution with respect to the two-phase structure.

Figure 9.5 displays the chemical diffusion coefficient in $\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ as a function of the degree of Li insertion. The apparent $D^{*}$ in disordered $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ at room temperature has been calculated on the assumption of uniform $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$distribution at any composition in the solid solution electrode. We observe a continuous decrease of the value of $D^{*}$ with the increase of lithium content. The high value $10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ of the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$diffusion coefficient at low lithium concentration $0 \leq x \leq 0.2$ may result from the smaller diffusion path length of the lithium ion in the disordered phase compared to that of crystalline sample. The variation in the diffusion coefficient can

Fig. 9.5 Chemical diffusion coefficient of lithium in $\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ as a function of lithium concentration. The diffusivity parameter is deduced from the potential step polarization method
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-294.jpg?height=726&width=764&top_left_y=1346&top_left_x=580)

be approximated by a relationship of the form $D^{*}=D_{\mathrm{o}} \exp (-\beta x)$, with $D_{\mathrm{o}}=1.7 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $\beta=2.8 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ for d- $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ [16]. This behavior reflects the loss of structural integrity on repeated cycles of intercalation and deintercalation. Nevertheless, even after long-term cycling the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$mobility remains sufficient for practical application of nano-sized d-MoS2 cathode materials. In fact this is a general feature of most of the insertion compounds [19].

### 9.3 Hydrated $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$

Molybdenum is known to exist in a number of oxidation states and a variety of oxides, sub-oxides, hydroxides, and hydrated complexes [20]. These oxides and oxide-hydrates of molybdenum in its highest oxidation state display a variety of structural types involving linked $\mathrm{MoO}_{6}$ octahedra. Of the anhydrous oxides, the well-known orthorhombic form, denoted $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ is stable at room temperature and its structure can be described in terms of layered lattice in which distorted $\mathrm{MoO}_{6}$ octahedra share edges and vertices to form corrugated two-dimensional sheets separated by a van der Waals gap. According to the X-ray data, molybdenum trioxide reacts readily with lithium, forming two well-defined discharge products that are different, but similar to the known high temperature $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ phase [21]. However, $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ only reacts with about $1.5 \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mo}$. The typical dischargecharge profile of the anhydrous $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ phase is compared with that of hydrated $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot n \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(n=0.66,1.00)$ materials in Li cell in Fig. 9.6. The electrochemical lithium insertion into the $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ framework can be described assuming the reduction

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-295.jpg?height=608&width=846&top_left_y=1337&top_left_x=338)
Fig. 9.6 Typical discharge-charge curves of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}-y_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ cathode materials ( $y=0.0,0.66 ; 1.0$ ). Measurements were carried out at $0.1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ in Li cell using nonaqueous electrolyte $1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ in EC-DEC

from $\mathrm{Mo}(\mathrm{VI})$ to $\mathrm{Mo}(\mathrm{V})$ and $\mathrm{Mo}(\mathrm{IV})$ oxidation states. The capacity of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ observed here is approximately in agreement with the theoretical gravimetric capacity $280 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Since the $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ reduction process is reversible, the construction of rechargeable cells is possible. Satisfactory charge-discharge efficiency and storage capability are other favorable features for $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot n \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ compounds that exhibit similar electrochemical characteristics. Upon lithiation of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$, the electronic conductivity increases from $10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ for $x=0$ to ca. $10^{-1} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ for $0.3 \leq x \leq 0.9$ [22]. Li diffusion coefficients in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ powder depend on $x$. A maximum value of ca. $10^{-9} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ has been reported for $x \approx 0.6$. The Li mobility was shown to slightly decrease upon subsequent discharge-charge cycles due to irreversible structural and morphological changes of the host matrix. However, upon recharging, reoxidation of Mo produces a resistive compound which induces a large polarization of the cell for potential of $\approx 3.5 \mathrm{~V}$ (Fig. 9.6). This is very advantageous from the technology point of view, since this material acts as a self-limiting voltage medium at the end of the charge. The suitability of molybdenum oxide-hydrates, $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot n \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, as cathode materials for nonaqueous lithium batteries has been assessed by several workers [23-28]. As a result, the discharge capacity of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot n \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ increases with decreasing water content, but the cycle life increases with increasing water content in the composition range $0.33<n<1.00$. Monoclinic monohydrate, $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, having one coordinated water molecule, showed a discharge capacity of about $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ of acid weight and a discharge potential around 2.5 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$. This cathode material displayed a good charge-discharge cyclic behavior at a capacity below $1 \mathrm{e}^{-} / \mathrm{Mo}$, while keeping the original layered lattice on cycling. The oxide-hydrate material prepared by sol-gel [23] shows a good performance and could be a candidate for practical primary lithium battery. Amorphous $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ cathode exhibits stepwise discharge behavior, including two plateaus; the first step up to $0.3 \mathrm{e}^{-} / \mathrm{Mo}$ and the second one up to $1.0 \mathrm{e}^{-} / \mathrm{Mo}$. This amorphous phase delivers a discharge capacity of $260 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ corresponding to $1.5 \mathrm{e}^{-} / \mathrm{Mo}$. On the other hand, the profile of the discharge cell voltage using the crystalline $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \cdot 1 / 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ cathode is rather monotonous without any plateau. This electrochemical behavior is attributed to the large cavities available for Li ions which prevent repulsive forces between inserted ions. Cathodes formed with this hemihydrate oxide gave a practical energy density of $630 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ for $1.5 \mathrm{e}^{-} / \mathrm{Mo}$. X-ray diffraction patterns of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}-^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ measured after electrochemical titration show that the monoclinic structure remains largely unchanged upon lithiation with the strongest (001) Bragg line shifted toward lower angles. This suggests that during the discharge, $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions are inserted between the layers, leading to a small increase of the interlayer spacing. This can be explained by a model of rigid hydrated- $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ layers in their original state separated by intercalated $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$cations.

The electrochemical behavior of nonstoichiometric molybdenum oxides $\mathrm{MoO}_{3-\delta}$ suggests that both channel/site size and electronic conductivity are the predominant factors influencing the extent of reversible lithium incorporation by metal oxides with framework structures shear-related to those of $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$. Distortions and nonequivalence of the sites available for $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$accommodation should affect the thermodynamics of the reduction processes. The nonstoichiometric

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-297.jpg?height=758&width=846&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=338)
Fig. 9.7 Electrochemical characteristics of lithium/Mo-based cells. Cathode materials can be classified into three classes according their energy density

molybdenum oxide $\mathrm{Mo}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{14}\left(\mathrm{MoO}_{2.8}\right)$ with framework structure based on mixed networks of polyhedra is permeated by large open channels [29]. $\mathrm{Mo}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{14}$ was obtained by dehydration and annealing treatment of molybdenum monohydrate at $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The open-circuit voltage is 3.1 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$for the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{MoO}_{2.8}$ cell. The first discharge displays a stepped behavior with a voltage plateau at ca. 2.2 V followed by a potential decline for $x>0.7 \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mo}$. The limiting reversible lithium capacity is 1.5 $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mo}$ for $\mathrm{Mo}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{14}$. This compound can be assigned the following Mo valence distribution: $\mathrm{Mo}_{3}{ }^{6+} \mathrm{Mo}_{2}{ }^{5+}$. If reversible lithium incorporation produced all $\mathrm{Mo}^{4+}$, the expected lithium uptake would be $8 / 5(1.6) \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mo}$; a value close to that measured by electrochemical titration. The gravimetric capacity was much higher than the $280 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ of anhydrous $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$. Figure 9.7 summarizes the electrochemical characteristics of Mo-based lithium cells. According the energy density, three classes of materials can be distinguished from the voltage-capacity graph.

## $9.4 \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ Thin Films

Molybdenum trioxide thin films can be easily grown by various deposition techniques: rf-sputtering, thermal evaporation, flash evaporation, deep-coating, pulselaser deposition (PLD), atomic layer deposition (ALD), etc. providing various forms of crystal chemistry by tuning the substrate temperatures, $T_{\mathrm{s}}$, and/or the oxygen partial pressure, $p\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)$, in the deposition chamber [30, 31]. For example,

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-298.jpg?height=604&width=841&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=343)
Fig. 9.8 Discharge curves of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ microbatteries including thin films deposited by flash evaporation method onto silicon substrate maintained at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=30$ and $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Measurements were carried out at current densities $10 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$

$\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films grown by flash-evaporation onto silicon substrates maintained in the temperature range $30-300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ revealed a predominant $\alpha$-phase with a well-defined ( 0 k 0 ) orientation. The surface morphological studies show that films prepared in the range $200-300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are crystalline with elongated crystal geometry. The layered nature of the film is shown on each crystallite [31-33]. Discharge curves of Li// $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ cells with cathode thin film deposited by flash-evaporation method onto silicon substrate maintained at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=30$ and $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig. 9.8. These microbatteries display electrochemical features as follows. The initial voltage 3.2 V of a Li-cell with $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin-film is higher than that recorded on the galvanic cell using crystalline and stoichiometric material. This could be attributed to the oxygen-deficient structure of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3-\delta}$ films. The cell voltage decreases continuously as a function of the degree of Li insertion and the steadily behavior is a function of the structural arrangement in the film, which depends on the substrate deposition temperature [34]. The electrochemical process seems to be a classical intercalation mechanism for the lithium ions with no voltage plateau occurring during the discharge, and $1.5 \mathrm{e}^{-}$transferred in the host material. The reduction process is accompanied by coloration of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films. These results suggest that the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$diffusion is anisotropic and limited by grain boundary effects which affect the discharge curve. The discharge curve of the cell fabricated with the $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ film deposited at $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (curve b) is quite stable. This can be attributed to the unique layered structure of $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ with nanosized grain ( 43 nm ) in the film. A second possibility is the presence of the mixed $\alpha-\beta$ phase, which would enhance the standard potential by 120 mV . A third explanation may be oxygen-defects in the host structure involving a lower Fermi level in such a semiconducting film that provides higher open-circuit voltage. This hypothesis is verified for $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films deposited by rf-sputtering in different partial oxygen pressure in the chamber as
shown ion Fig. 9.9. As an experimental result, films deposited under $p\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)=$ 150 mTorr exhibits a less disordered lattice.

Investigations of the electronic structure of transition metal-oxides, i.e., $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{WO}_{3}$, have demonstrated that these films show an n-type semiconductor property due to oxygen vacancies with a large electron affinity ( 6.7 eV ) and ionization potential (9.7) eV as well as a high work function ( 6.8 eV ). $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin films had a valence band edge at 5.3 eV and the conduction band edge at 2.3 eV [35, 36]. In a simplest semiconductor model, electrochemical properties of $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films can be illustrated by an electronic band scheme as shown in Fig. 9.10 [37]. The cell voltage can be viewed as the difference in the Fermi energies between

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-299.jpg?height=613&width=841&top_left_y=686&top_left_x=343)
Fig. 9.9 Discharge curves of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ microcells including thin films deposited by rf-sputtering method onto nickel substrate as a function of the partial oxygen pressure. Measurements were carried out at current densities $20 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$

Fig. 9.10 Schematic view of the electronic band structure of (a) $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal and (b) $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin film. The energy of the Fermi level $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ lies with the localized electronic band upon Li insertion in the host material
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-299.jpg?height=615&width=757&top_left_y=1504&top_left_x=587)

lithium metal and positive electrode. In $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$, there are five $\mathrm{O}\left(p_{\pi}\right)$ and three Mo ( $t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ ) orbitals which interact to form $\pi$ and $\pi^{*}$ bands forming the valence and conduction bands, respectively [38]. The antibonding $\pi^{*}$ states hold the extra electrons supplied by the inserted lithium ions. The narrowing of the conduction band is expected to lead to an increase in the effective mass which will affect the position of the Fermi energy in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ phases. In Li -intercalated $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal, the Fermi energy lies either with the donor level near the bottom of the conduction band or in the conduction band itself (Fig. 9.10), while in $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films the density-of-states might show a band in the middle of the gap and the Fermi level lies in the band. This last description is supported by absorption measurements [31]. Thus, as $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ is a semiconductor with a wide band gap of 3.1 eV , the higher voltage displayed in thin-film cells is attributed to the difference between Fermi levels ( $>0.5 \mathrm{eV}$ ) in the crystal and thin-film.

The ionic transport properties of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions into the $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ film network were investigated by the modified galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) from the variation of the potential vs. time during a relaxation period following a short pulse of current in the discharge process [39]. Transport parameters such as the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$chemical diffusion coefficient, thermodynamic factor, and ionic conductivity are investigated during the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$insertion process and discussed with respect to the crystallinity of the cathode material. Figure 9.11 shows the chemical diffusion coefficient of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions as a function of the degree of intercalation in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-300.jpg?height=769&width=841&top_left_y=1174&top_left_x=343)
Fig. 9.11 Composition dependence of the chemical diffusion coefficient, $D^{*}$, of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions in $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films deposited by flash-evaporation method onto Si substrate maintained at two temperatures $T_{\mathrm{s}}$. $D^{*}$ of $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal is shown for comparison

materials. In these experiments, the evolution in the voltage $E^{*}$ after applying a current pulse at $t=0$ through the cell obeys the relation:

$$
E^{*}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{IWRT}}{F^{2} c^{*} A}\right)\left[\left(\frac{4 t}{\pi D^{*}}\right)^{1 / 2}-\frac{t}{\delta}\right]
$$

where $I$ is the current intensity of the pulse, $t$ the time, $c^{*}$ the Li -ion concentration of the host, $W$ the thermodynamic factor, $A$ and $\delta$ the surface and thickness of the electrode, $T$ the absolute temperature, $F=96485 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $R=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The thermodynamic factor is defined as:

$$
W=\frac{\partial\left(\ln a^{*}\right)}{\partial\left(\ln c^{*}\right)}
$$

which $a^{*}$ is the activity of the intercalant species in the solid solution electrode. $W$ is the ratio between the chemical diffusion coefficient $D^{*}$ and the component diffusion coefficient $D_{0}$ :

$$
W=D^{*} / D_{0}
$$

Equation (9.1) is valid: (1) if the duration of the preceding discharge is longer than a critical time:

$$
t_{0}=\frac{\delta^{2}}{4 D^{*}}
$$

and (2) for a limited period of relaxation $t<t_{0}$ of the electrochemical cell. In the compositional range $0.2 \leq x \leq 1.2$ the values of the chemical diffusion coefficient of lithium varies from $9 \times 10^{-11}$ to $1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ in the $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal. The compositional dependence of $D^{*}$ is approximately a quadratic function due to the nature of the empty sites in the host structure. According to the model of Basu and Worrell [40], the chemical diffusivity is related to the composition:

$$
D^{*}=\beta x^{2}(1-x)+x\left(1-x^{2}\right)
$$

where $\beta$ is an interaction parameter related to the repulsive interaction energy between alkali ions. According to Eq. (9.6) $D^{*}$ shows a maximum at the halffilling site number. Although the uncertainties in the chemical diffusion data shown in Fig. 9.11 prelude an adequate test of Eq. (9.6), the maximum in the $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ data suggests an interaction energy lower than 0.2 eV , which is consistent with the value obtained from thermodynamic measurements. The value of the chemical diffusion coefficient in $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin films (Fig. 9.11, curves $\mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ ) is lower than in $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ single crystal.

For $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films prepared at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ a maximum value of $5 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ is obtained at $x=0.8$ (Fig. 9.11, curve b). $D^{*}$ varies with the composition $x$ according to a quadratic law:

$$
D^{*}=\kappa\left[\left(2 x-x^{2}\right)\right],
$$

where $\kappa=2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The chemical diffusion coefficient behavior in $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin film deposited at $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ differs from the previous one. We observe that $D^{*}$ has an almost constant value of $1.5 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ in the range $0.1<x<1.2$ and increases at higher concentration of intercalant ions. We tentatively attribute this complex behavior to the polycrystalline state of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films grown at high substrate temperature for which the enhancement factor is high. In this case, the intercalation process is partly controlled by the number of ion occupancies in the host lattice of the crystallite in the film. Figure 9.12 shows the thermodynamic factor, $W$ (in a logarithmic scale), as a function of the degree of intercalation in the $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ host material. In the compositional range $0.2<x<1.4, W$ varies from 1.8 to 30 for $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystals (Fig. 9.12, curve a). Considering that $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ is a layered host for the intercalant, the model of ion-ion interaction can be applied. Armand [41] has proposed such a model to describe the variation of the chemical potential in intercalation compounds. The thermodynamic factor is related to the interaction factor, $g$, as:

$$
W=\left[(1-x)^{-1}+g x\right] .
$$

Experimental data are well fitted using Eq. (9.8), which provides an interaction factor $g=7.5$. This value is of the same order as in $\mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ [42]. The thermodynamic

Fig. 9.12 Thermodynamic factor of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ cathodes: (a) crystalline $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ phase and (b) thin film grown on a substrate maintained at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Full lines represent the fit using Eq. (9.8)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-302.jpg?height=747&width=762&top_left_y=1325&top_left_x=582)

factor of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin films varies from 50 to 800 in the compositional range $0.2<x<1.5$. We observe a quasi-linear variation of the composition dependence of $W$ which may be associated with the oxygen-defects in the host lattice. It is a fact that numerous of such defects exist in the film structure, even when the crystallinity is improved by different conditions of preparation. Here, we remark that $W$ is two orders of magnitude higher than that for $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal. Using Eq. (9.8) a fit of experimental data is obtained with a high value of the interaction factor $(g=400)$. Considering that $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ films are oxygen-deficient materials, the model of charge transport in internal defect-materials can be applied [43]. Defects are Li interstitials, $\mathrm{Li}^{*}$, and conduction electrons, $\mathrm{e}^{\prime}$, for example. Maeir [44] showed that, in a solid solution where no internal defect reactions occur, the thermodynamic factor is related to the defect concentration (if dilute defects exist) as:

$$
W=\Phi_{\mathrm{Li}}^{-1}+\Phi_{\mathrm{e}^{\prime}}^{-1}
$$

where $\Phi_{\mathrm{Li}}^{-1}=c_{\mathrm{Li}^{*}} / c_{\mathrm{Li}^{+}}$and $\Phi_{\mathrm{e}^{\prime}}^{-1}=c_{\mathrm{e}^{\prime}} / c_{\mathrm{Li}^{+}}$. Equation (9.4) provides a linear variation of the thermodynamic factor with the degree of intercalation, as shown in Fig. 9.12 (curve b). The large increase of $W$ may be also associated with the decrease of the electronic mobility in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ film. For the case in which the chemical diffusion is predominantly determined by ionic species, Eq. (9.4) yields, with the substitution of $D_{0}$ by the conductivity $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}$ and the thermodynamic factor, for the partial conductivity [45]:

$$
\sigma_{\mathrm{i}} t_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{F}{\mathrm{RT}}\left(\frac{q c^{*} D^{*}}{W}\right),
$$

where $t_{\mathrm{e}}$ is the transference number defined by:

$$
t_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\sigma_{\mathrm{e}}}{\sigma_{\mathrm{e}}+\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}}
$$

By combining the chemical diffusion coefficient and the thermodynamic factor values, the lithium ion conductivities $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}$ in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ materials are shown in Fig. 9.13. Discharging the cell results in the formation of a mixed conductor; the compound is an electronic and ionic conductor. If the sample is predominantly an electronic conductor, i.e., $\sigma_{\mathrm{e}} \gg \sigma_{\mathrm{i}}$, the ionic conductivity can be deduced from Eq. (9.8). The conductivity is found to increase with lithium concentration in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal to reach about $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $x=0.6$ (Fig. 9.13, curve a). One can remark that the ionic transport parameters in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystal are comparable with those of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ [40]. Figure 9.13 (curve b) displays the lithium ionic conductivity of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ thin-film cathode. The ionic conductivity is estimated with an average value of $9 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $x=0.8$. We remark that the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ionic conductivity is much smaller than for $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ crystals. This behavior may be attributed to the disordered structure of the film. In such a material the conduction

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-304.jpg?height=773&width=843&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=343)
Fig. 9.13 Partial ionic conductivity of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ cathodes: (a) crystalline $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3} \mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ phase and (b) thin film grown at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

paths are not defined as well as they are in the crystalline material because in thin film: (1) the van der Waals plane is not quasi-infinite, (2) the potential barriers are more important, and (3) the $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions can be trapped by the structural defects.

### 9.5 Disordered Vanadium Oxides

Amorphous vanadium oxides were obtained by quenching molten mixtures of $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ with glass formers such as $\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{TeO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{GeO}_{2}$. Lower valence transition-metal ions are generally produced due to the loss of oxygen from the melt resulting in a mixed valence in the glass and in an enhanced semiconducting conduction, which occurs by electron hopping between $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$ and $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ states. Transition-metal oxides (TMO) glasses have some potential advantages over crystalline solids for use as cathode materials in high energy density lithium batteries [37]. For instance, the superior performance was attributed to the diffusivity of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions that is larger in glass $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ than in polycrystalline materials [46,47]. Nabavi et al. [48] reported the electrochemical properties of amorphous $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ positive electrode prepared by splat-cooling from the pure oxide melted at $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in air. The amorphous oxide is able to give $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ phase via electrochemical insertion of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions with reversible uptake of $1.8 \mathrm{Li}^{+}$per $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$. Figure 9.14 displays the discharge curves of various vanadium-based cathodes in lithium cells. These results show clearly the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-305.jpg?height=769&width=841&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=343)
Fig. 9.14 Discharge curves of disordered vanadium oxides compared with crystalline $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(\mathbf{a})$ : $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}-\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ glass (b), $0.8 \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}-0.2 \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ glass (c), $0.67 \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}-0.33 \mathrm{CuO}$ glass (d) and disordered $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ material

glasses $x \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}-(1-x) \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ exhibit good electrochemical features. Contrary to crystalline $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$, the discharge potential continuously decreases with the amount of inserted $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$suggesting that no phase transition occurred in the amorphous material. In particular, the composition $0.66 \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}-0.4 \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ showed reversible insertion process leading to a high voltage of $3.6 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{vs} . \mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$, a small degree of lattice expansion of about $2 \%$ in volume and a large stored energy density of $750 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$. Sakurai et al. [49] demonstrated the rechargeability of the $\mathrm{Li} / / x \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \cdot(1-x) \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ systems by cycling the $95: 60 \mathrm{~mol} . \% \mathrm{~V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ in the voltage range $2.0-3.5 \mathrm{~V}$ at current density $0.5 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$. Figure 9.14 presents the discharge curves of several vanadium-based electrodes including $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$-type glasses and disordered $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ compared to crystalline vanadium pentoxide.

Amorphous vanadium oxide thin-film electrodes synthesized by various methods including thermal evaporation [50], flash-evaporation [51], rf-sputtering [52], pulse-laser deposition (PLD) [53], electron-beam evaporation [54], xerogel method [55], sol-gel casting [56] were all investigated for their $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion intercalation capabilities. The microstructure of $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ film, which is very sensitive to the growth conditions, strongly influences their performance in thin-film solid-state microbatteries and other electrochemical devices [57]. Among the $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \cdot n \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ materials with $n=1.6,0.6$, and 0.3 prepared from the sol-gel route the $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \cdot 0.3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ film exhibits the best $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$intercalation performance, with an initial capacity of $275 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and a stabilized capacity of $185 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ under a high current density of $100 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ after 50 cycles. Such an enhanced electrochemical
property by thermal treatment is ascribed to the reduced water content, the retained interlayer spacing and the dominant amorphous phase in the film [56]. Specific capacity and cyclic stability of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion intercalation in $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ films could be attributed to surface defects $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$ and/or $\mathrm{V}^{3+}$ associated to oxygen vacancies in less crystallized vanadium pentoxide. Note that the specific capacity is limited because the valence of the metal cations fixes the number of electrons withdrawn from each metal center [58-61]. Swider-Lyons et al. [62] discussed the point defects introduced into the oxide network using various heat-treatment to modify the defective structure of polycrystalline $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$. The $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ cathode half-cell reaction can be expressed in the Kröger-Vink notation under the form of the following equilibrium reaction in defective oxides [63]:

$$
\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{V}}^{x}+\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}^{x}+\mathrm{Li}_{(\mathrm{sol})}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{V}}^{\prime}+\mathrm{OLi}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet}
$$

in which $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{V}}^{x}$ designates a $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ ion at a vanadium site in the $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ lattice, and $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}^{x}$ is an $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ion on an oxygen site. In Eq. (9.7), one considers that one electron is consumed when a $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$from an electrolyte solution ( $\mathrm{Li}_{(\text {sol })}^{+}$) inserts into a $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ cathode and a $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ ion is reduced to $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$. The occupation of the $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ site with a $\mathrm{V}^{4+}$ ion results in an effective one-negative charge on it, and is denoted by $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{V}}^{\prime}$. The inserted $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion is associated with an oxygen site, $\mathrm{OLi}_{o}{ }^{\bullet}$, and has an effective on-positive charge ( ${ }^{\circ}$ ). The $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions are alternatively introduced into an interstitial site, written as $\mathrm{Li}_{i}{ }^{\bullet}$. As an example of electrochemical properties of vanadium oxide thin-films, Figure 9.15 presents the discharge curves of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ microbatteries as

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-306.jpg?height=656&width=841&top_left_y=1287&top_left_x=343)
Fig. 9.15 Discharge profiles of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ microbatteries as a function of the substrate temperature, $T_{\mathrm{s}}$ compared to the response of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ crystal. Measurements were carried out at $10 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$

a function of the substrate temperature $T_{\mathrm{s}}$ compared to the response of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ crystal [58]. It is worth noting the influence of the substrate temperature on the electrochemical characteristics of the active cathode films. The film $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ deposited at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ displays the typical discharge curve of an amorphous material with uptake of 1 Li per vanadium, while the discharge profile of the film deposited at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ resembles to that of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ crystal with the appearance of a plateau at $\sim 2.2 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$. These electrochemical features can be assigned to the modification of the electronic states in the 3d-subband, induced by the distorted $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ film structure and the change in $\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{V}$ distance [64], responsible for the shift of the OCV. The chemical diffusion coefficients of $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{~V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ remain constant over the composition range $0 \leq x \leq 6$ with a mean value of $10^{-13} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. This value is about three orders of magnitude lower than that of the stoichiometric phase, which is typical of less structured material such as in $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ films [65]. Note that the diffusion path through the cavities extending in the (010) direction of the 3D framework of $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ is sensitive to the synthesis conditions. The difference between the oxide prepared in crystalline form and the films can thus be ascribed to differences in lattice perfection, i.e., static disorder or short chains with undistorted cavities [58].

## 9.6 $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ Thin Films

Polycrystalline thin films of lithium cobalt oxide were grown by PLD technique [66, 67]. Films of $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ were deposited onto Si substrates heated at temperature lower than $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ from a sintered composite target ( $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ) irradiated with a Nd: YAG laser. Under these deposition conditions, i.e., substrate temperature and partial oxygen pressure temperature, $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ films grown from target without $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ additive display the presence of cobalt oxide impurities. In addition to the peaks belonging to $\mathrm{LiCoO} \mathrm{O}_{2}$, two small peaks attributed to $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ are present at $2 \theta=45$ and $59^{\circ}$. Moreover, a large peak around $2 \theta=70^{\circ}$ is observed that could be mainly associated to the silicon substrate. As the amount of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ increased in the target, the XRD patterns develop features expected for the regular layered phase. They are indexed using the $R \overline{3} m$ symmetry. Figure 9.16 illustrates a highly textured (003) film obtained when a target with $15 \% \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ was used. The X-ray diffraction pattern displays three sharp and intense peaks at $2 \theta=19,38$ and $58^{\circ}$, which are unambiguously attributed to the (003), (006), and (009) Bragg lines, respectively. That of $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ films deposited at low substrate temperature reveals the amorphous nature of the layer. The typical peaks of the polycrystalline layered phase in $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ films appear upon increasing the substrate temperature ( $T_{\mathrm{s}}=300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) in oxygen partial pressure $P\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)=50 \mathrm{mTorr}$ using a lithium-rich target. The cobalt-oxygen framework is well defined in this structure [66].
$\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ films obtained by PLD with polycrystalline morphology were successfully used as cathode materials in lithium microbatteries. Typical charge and discharge curves of a $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ cell using pulsed-laser deposited film grown at

Fig. 9.16 X-ray diffraction patterns of $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ powder used as PLD target and $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ textured film grown into 50 mT orr $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ partial pressure
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-308.jpg?height=617&width=764&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=580)

Fig. 9.17 First chargedischarge curves of a $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ microbatteries using a $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ films grown by PLD technique in 50 mTorr oxygen pressure onto substrate heated in the range $25 \leq T_{\mathrm{s}} \leq 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-308.jpg?height=611&width=766&top_left_y=914&top_left_x=578)

$T_{\mathrm{s}}=300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig. 9.17. Electrochemical measurements was carried out at a rate $\mathrm{C} / 100$ in the potential range $2.0-4.2 \mathrm{~V}$; as such, the voltage profile should provide a close approximation to the OCV. The cell-voltage profiles display the typical profile currently observed for $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{CoO}_{2}$ cathodes. From the electrochemical features, we can make the following general remarks. An initial voltage about 2.15 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$was measured for $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ thin-film cathode cells, which is lower than the value recorded on galvanic cell using crystalline cathode [68]. The cell voltage is a function of the structural arrangement in the film and thus depends on the substrate deposition temperature (Fig. 9.17). These potentials slightly increased for films grown at high substrate temperature. This is consistent with many literature data and ensures that at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the material particles are electrochemically active [68]. Specific capacity as high as $158 \mathrm{mC} \mathrm{cm}^{-2} \mu \mathrm{~m}$ was

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-309.jpg?height=662&width=850&top_left_y=210&top_left_x=336)
Fig. 9.18 Specific capacity of $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiNi}_{0.8} \mathrm{Co}_{0.15} \mathrm{Al}_{0.05} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ cells as a function of the substrate temperature. PLD films were grown onto various substrates at $25 \leq T_{\mathrm{s}} \leq 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in $p\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)=$ 50 mTorr . Films deposited onto Ni foil exhibit the best discharge capacity

measured on $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ polycrystalline films [67]. The capacity of films with a layered ( $R \overline{3} m$ S.G.) structure appears to be strongly dependent on the morphology that is greatly influenced by the substrate temperature and the nature thereof. As an example, Fig. 9.18 shows the variation of the specific capacity of the Ni-rich $\mathrm{LiNi}_{0.80} \mathrm{Co}_{0.15} \mathrm{Al}_{0.05} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ (NCA) films deposited onto three different substrates: Ni foil, Si wafer and ITO/glass. As an experimental result, NCA cathode deposited onto nickel at $T_{\mathrm{s}}=500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ by PLD technique exhibits the highest specific capacity of $\approx 100 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2} \mu \mathrm{~m}$.

### 9.7 Disordered $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$

Among the most promising compounds for $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{ion}$ battery electrodes, the 3D spinel $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is able to reversibly uptake about 0.5 Li per transition-metal atom [69]. However, there are some problems that postpone its implementation, such as difficulties encountered in controlling cationic order/disorder in these systems during either their synthesis or cycling [70]. Other problems associated to cationic distribution within $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ are also severe because spinel compounds of general formula $A B_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ are prone to cationic mixing between tetrahedral and octahedral sites, leading to either normal $[A]_{\text {Tet. }}\left[B_{2}\right]_{\text {Oct. }} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ or inverse $[B]_{\text {Tet. }}[A B]_{\text {Oct. }} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinels. $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ was shown to be a normal spinel, but deviations from such a cationic distribution exist depending on the synthesis history, namely annealing temperature and cooling rate of the material. Here, we present the properties of two $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$

Fig. 9.19 The voltage composition profile for a $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ using a spinel electrode synthesized by the combustion urea-assisted method. The cell was charged and discharged at current density $0.05 \mathrm{~mA} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-310.jpg?height=658&width=764&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=580)

spinel oxides, the first material was synthesized by the combustion urea-assisted method [71] and the second one was obtained by milling of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.

Using a wet-chemical technique assisted by urea as combustion agent, $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel oxides exhibit grains with a quite small domain size of $0.5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ The voltage composition profile for a $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ using a spinel electrode synthesized by wet-chemical technique assisted by urea as combustion agent is shown in Fig. 9.19. In the voltage domain between 3.0 and 4.5 V , the charge-discharge curves correspond to the voltage profiles characteristic of the spinel $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cathode material associated with lithium occupation of tetrahedral sites, in agreement with previous works. From the variation of the cell potential with $x$ in $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, one can distinguish the presence of two regions. The shape of the voltage curves indicates whether the delithiated $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ exists as a single- or a multiple-phase. In the latter case the potential is expected to be essentially invariant with composition. The first region (I) is characterized by a S-shaped voltage curve, whereas the second region (II) corresponds to a plateau portion. In region I, the charge voltage increases continuously in the voltage range of $3.80-4.05 \mathrm{~V}$. In region II, the charge voltage is stable around 4.10 V . The lithium extraction/ insertion reactions in regions I and II proceed in a matrix having a cubic symmetry. In the region II corresponding to the upper voltage plateau, a two-cubic phase system is recognized, whereas the region I is attributed to a single cubic phase characterized by an S-shaped voltage curve. The upper 4-V plateau provides a capacity over $110 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ based on the active material utilization with an excellent cyclability [71].

Mechano-chemical technique is an efficient method to obtain high dispersed compounds directly during mechanical activation at room temperature [72]. $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ was synthesized by mechanical activation in plenary mill with steel balls from the mixture $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ electrochemically activated (EMD) [73].

Fig. 9.20 Evolution of the lattice parameters of ceramic $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (filled triangle) and mechanosynthesized Li-Mn-O (filled square) with grinding. $a_{\text {cub }}$ is given with an accuracy of $0.01 \AA$, whereas due to a large inaccuracy on its value, the 10-h ground ceramic lattice parameter is not reported. The dashed line points out the lattice parameter $a_{\text {cub }}=8.247 \AA$ for stoichiometric $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-311.jpg?height=615&width=762&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=582)

Evolution of the structure upon mechano-milling is as follows. After 1 h of grinding, the major peaks of a spinel-type phase are visible. Higher milling times, between 2 and 8 h , lead to a better crystallization of the materials. The narrowing of the X-ray peaks (i.e., better crystallization) may be correlated to a decrease in the lattice strain and an increase in the crystallite size, which slightly varies from around $20 \AA$ after 1 h of grinding to $80 \AA$ after 8 h of grinding, respectively. Further grindings ( $t_{\mathrm{m}} \geq 10 \mathrm{~h}$ ) lead to a decomposition of the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Mn}-\mathrm{O}$ spinel-type oxides coupled with the appearance of new phases mainly identified as $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. The evolution of the cell parameters with milling time for non-reactive and reactive grinding are reported in Fig. 9.20. In both cases, the lattice parameter $a_{\text {cub }}$ increased with milling time, and with the synthesis temperature. The $8.238 \AA$ value, slightly smaller than $8.247 \AA$ reported in the literature for stoichiometric $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, indicates a small departure from the $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mn}=1 / 2$ ratio for the ceramic sample [74]. The strong increase in the cell parameter from 8.238 to $8.318 \AA$ after non-reactive grinding during 5 h is still not completely understood. Indeed, to our knowledge, such a high cell parameter value has never been reported for spinel-type lithiated manganese oxides. In the literature, a cubic parameter $a_{\text {cub }}$ higher than $8.24 \AA$ is generally attributed either to an excess in lithium or an oxygen deficient spinel. Xia et al. [75] determined an $8.2485 \AA$ value for ceramic $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ heat treated $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 24 h in air exhibiting an oxygen deficiency of 0.08 (e.g., $\mathrm{Li}_{0.938} \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3.92}$ ).

The product prepared by reactive milling for 5 h shows a rather disordered spinel structure as observed by Raman scattering spectroscopy (Fig. 9.21a). For this compound the Raman active $A_{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ mode at $625 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ is shifted to $633 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ and broadened in comparison with that of a $\lambda-\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ ceramic. This disordered structure is also at the origin of the electrochemical behavior shown in Fig. 9.21b. The first charge-discharge curve recorded at the $C / 10$ rate shows deviation from the standard electrochemical profile of $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel. We do not observe the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-312.jpg?height=522&width=1169&top_left_y=210&top_left_x=180)
Fig. 9.21 (Left) Raman spectra of $\lambda-\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinel and disordered spinel grown by mechanosynthesis from the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}-\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ mixture. (Right) The first charge-discharge of the disordered $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ sample

characteristic plateaus at 4.05 and 4.15 V occurring during in the charge process. This is attributed to the strong distortion of the $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra as evidenced by the red shift of the $A_{1 \mathrm{~g}}$ Raman mode.

### 9.8 Disordered $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$

Among the various vanadates, crystallized $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ shows promising electrochemical behavior as a 4-V electrode material [76-78]. Crystallized $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ synthesized by the classical high-temperature route can reversibly react with about seven Li ions per transition metal when discharged to voltages lower than 0.2 V [79], so that specific capacities as $800-900 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ could be achieved. This vanadate was shown to become amorphous upon the first electrochemical discharge, which must be performed at a slow cycling rate to ensure a proper function of the electrode material upon subsequent cycles.
$\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ has been synthesized by a wet-chemical method, which consists of a low-temperature reaction assisted by glycine as combustion agent. The powder mass was annealed at 350 and $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in air for 6 h for improving the crystallinity of the $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ final product. XRD date show that $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ belongs to inverse spinel structure (space group $F d \overline{3} m-O_{\mathrm{h}}{ }^{7}$ ) with a cubic lattice parameter of $8.222 \AA$. The pentavalent vanadium is located on the tetrahedral ( $8 a$ ) sites, whereas Li and Ni are distributed on the octahedral ( $16 d$ ) sites, the distribution being disordered. Figure 9.22 a presents the voltage-composition curve for the Li insertion into an amorphous $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$. Upon the first reduction, $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ reacts with ten Li ions per Ni , while only seven $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Ni}$ can be removed after subsequent recharges.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-313.jpg?height=486&width=1169&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=180)
Fig. 9.22 (a) The voltage-composition curve for the Li insertion into an amorphous $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$. (b) Raman spectra of a $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ cathode discharged at 1.0 and 0.6 V vs. lithium anode

Figure 9.22b shows the Raman spectra of Li intercalated $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ phase. Spectra were recorded at the beginning of the two plateaus at 0.9 and 0.4 V occurring during the $\mathrm{Li} / / \mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ cell discharge ( Li intercalation). These plateaus should be at the origin of biphasic transformation due to the high reduction of vanadium ions. The Raman features of $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ have been discussed elsewhere [78]. The spectra are dominated by a broad band in the $700-850 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ region, which is assigned to a vibration between the oxygen and the highest-valency cation. This vibration corresponds to the stretching mode of $\mathrm{VO}_{4}$ tetrahedron having the $A_{1}$ symmetry, whereas the band situated at $335 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ corresponds to the bending mode of $\mathrm{VO}_{4}$ tetrahedron with an $E$ symmetry. Therefore, one observes either the bending vibrations of $\mathrm{VO}_{4}$ tetrahedron or the vibrations involving the $\mathrm{NiO}_{6}, \mathrm{LiO}_{6}$ octahedral environments. If one considers that all the Li ions are accommodated in octahedral $\mathrm{LiO}_{6}$ environments, the $F_{1 \mathrm{u}}$ modes are normally split into ( $A+2 B$ ) Raman-active and IR-active components. Therefore, IR modes having ( $A+2 B$ ) symmetry are intense, whereas Raman modes are expected to be very weak. These two modes are observed at 416 and $435 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9.22b, there are important modifications of the Raman spectra of $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ upon Li intercalation. When the cathode is discharged, the stretching mode of $\mathrm{VO}_{4}$ tetrahedra is shifted toward the low-wave number side corresponding to the reduction of the highestvalency cation. At 0.6 V the Raman efficiency is very weak. Thus, the host lattice is highly intercalated and the Raman peaks of $\mathrm{LiNiVO}_{4}$ decrease and a strong luminescence band appears at about 1.8 eV for $\mathrm{Li}_{5} \mathrm{NiVO}_{4}$.

## Chapter 10 <br> Anodes for Li-Ion Batteries

### 10.1 Introduction

Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) have made considerable progress since the first commercial one by Sony in 1991, which had $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ and graphite as active elements of the positive and negative electrodes, respectively. The LiBs are now the primary energy storage devices in the transportation and communications, from portable use in computers, up to electric and hybrid vehicles. More recently, they have also been used as back-up supply units, frequency regulators (load leveling) to integrate on smart grids the electricity produced by windmills and photovoltaic plants (for a recent review, see [1]). These applications require high energy densities, high power, and safety among others. Considerable efforts have been made to propose other electrodes to fulfill these requirements. We have recently reviewed the works and progress concerning the materials for the positive electrode [2-6]. The present work is devoted to the materials for the negative electrode counterpart. It is common to use the terminology anode and cathode for the negative and positive electrodes, respectively, although the electrodes play alternatively the role of anode and cathode during the charge and discharge process. We shall use this terminology in the following for conciseness purposes only.

An ideal active anode element should fulfill the following requirements as follows: (1) It must be light and accommodate as much Li as possible to optimize the gravimetric capacity. (2) Its redox potential with respect to $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$must be as small as possible at any Li-concentration. The reason is that this potential is subtracted to the redox potential of the cathode material to give the overall voltage of the cell, and smaller voltage means smaller energy density. (3) It must possess good electronic and ionic conductivities since faster motion of the lithium ions and the electrons also mean higher power density of the cell. (4) It must not be soluble in the solvents of the electrolyte and not react with the lithium salt. (5) It must be safe, i.e. avoid any thermal runaway of the battery, a criterion that is not independent of the previous one, but deserves special attention, especially for use in transportation
such as electric vehicles and planes. (6) It must be cheap and environmentally friendly. The different materials proposed as anode elements for Li-ion batteries must be discussed with respect to these different criteria.

The graphite is still the most used anode material and is still the reference. The first works giving evidence of the insertion of lithium in graphite date from 1955 [7], confirmed by the synthesis of $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$ in 1965 [8]. The synthesis of $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$, however, was not obtained by electrochemical process at that time. Another decade was spent before Besenhard and Eichinger discovered the reversible intercalation of lithium in graphite, proposing this material as an anode in 1976 [9, 10]. Increasing the Li content in $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$ is possible [8], but no reversible cycling beyond $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$ could ever be obtained. The graphite anode can thus be cycled between Li and $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$, at which the stage I intercalation is achieved with the Li atoms located between the carbon planes. The theoretical capacity associated to the cycles between C and $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$ is $372 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Therefore, the requirement labeled (1) in the previous section is not very well satisfied: one goal of the research on anode material is the increase of this capacity. There is a tendency, however, to focus too much attention on this parameter. The reason is that the capacity is limited more by the cathode than the anode element: typically, the capacity of a cathode element is in the range $140- 200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The gain in capacity of the total cell by increasing that of the anode element alone is thus limited [11]. This is illustrated in Fig. 10.1, which shows that the total capacity of an 18,650-type cell as a function of the anode capacity. The total capacity almost saturates when the anode capacity reaches typically $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. On the one hand, it shows that the tests of anode materials that are usually performed on half-cells (i.e. with lithium metal counter-electrode) are misleading. On the other hand, the capacity $372 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ of the graphite is significantly below this saturation limit, and the research on anodes with higher capacity is clearly justified. The requirement (2) is well satisfied, since the potential of graphite versus Li metal is only $0.15-0.25 \mathrm{~V}$. So is the requirement (3). Graphite is a semimetal with an electronic conductivity at room temperature that exceeds

Fig. 10.1 Total capacity of a $18,650 \mathrm{Li}$-ion cell as a function of the anode capacity, for two cathodes having capacities 140 and $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Reproduced with permission from [11]. Copyright 2007 Elsevier
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-319.jpg?height=577&width=765&top_left_y=1495&top_left_x=582)

$10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$, while $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$ is a metal with a high Li ion mobility $10^{-8}-10^{-10} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Requirement (4) poses questions. The graphite must be protected against side reactions with the electrolyte. This is the role of ethylene carbonate (EC) that is used as a solvent in the electrolyte of batteries with graphite anode. Owing to the EC-based solvent, a solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) is formed at the surface of graphite particles during the first cycles. This SEI prevents excessive solvent intercalation and acts as a good Li ion conductor [12-16]. Moreover, it protects the strongly reducing $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$ formed at the end of charge from direct contact with the electrolyte. On another hand, the use of more conductive solvents such as propylene carbonate (PC) is forbidden with graphite, because of side reactions, and the flow of Li ions arriving on the graphite must be small enough to give the ions the time to insert into the graphite, otherwise the Li deposition generates safety issues $[17$, 18]. Therefore, efforts on the research for new cathode elements also aim to find materials that are more performing with respect to the criteria (4) and (5). Concerning the requirement (6), graphite is not expensive, except specialty graphites like MesoCarbon MicroBeads (MCMB) graphite or microcrystalline graphite that imply expensive manufacturing processes.

One can easily understand from this discussion that it is difficult to replace graphite, since it satisfies many requirements. The only alternative anode that has been introduced on the market is the composite $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{Co} / \mathrm{C}$ developed by Sony in 2005 [19]. Here, Sn is the electro-active element, which exemplifies the efforts that are made in research on the so-called alloy anodes including metals metalloids, and alloy-compounds reviewed in [20]. Cobalt and carbon are inactive matrix elements that help in better cycling. A reversible capacity circa $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ has been achieved, which is comparable to that of graphite, but the advantages are an increase of safety and a lower cost with respect to specialty graphite. Another anode, $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$, is very promising and this material is already commercialized, but the batteries equipped with this anode are still made at the laboratory scale. Results obtained with this anode prior 2010 have been reviewed in [21]. The active research of topical interest has motivated many reviews on the other anode compounds as well through the last decade [22-67]. They include metal-containing anodes in the form of metals, intermetallics [20], oxides, and oxysalts including phosphates and carbonates [22], vanadium oxides [34], molybdenum oxides [34, 68], $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$-based nanostructures [ $15,31,35,38,53,69-73$ ], more generally conversion electrodes [27, 74] including fluorides [27, 75], sulfides [27], selenides [25], nitrides [27], phosphides [25, 27], antimonides [25], and graphene-based nanocomposites [76]. Different reviews have also been focused on silicon anodes [77-86], which illustrates the tremendous amount of works on this very promising anode, boosted by the new nanotechnologies from which various advantages are expected: (1) nano-sized active particles can suffer huge dilatation/contraction upon Li insertion/extraction without damage, contrary to bigger particles. (2) Larger surface to volume ratio implies a larger specific capacity, and a larger contact area with the electrolyte leading to high lithium-ion flux across the electrode/electrolyte interface. (3) Lithium diffusion and electronic conductivity are improved due to the reduction of the electron and Li path length, leading to batteries with enhanced
power capability. Therefore, many works have been devoted to the synthesis and analysis of the properties of the nano-sized anode particles that have been specifically reviewed [49, 87]. Some reviews have been focused on such nano-particles based on carbon (carbon nanotubes, graphene composites) [41, 88-93].

The aim of the present chapter is to review the different works that have been accomplished on the different types of anode materials that have been proposed recently, focusing attention on the tremendous amount of work that has been done in this field during the last 5 years. Elder works can be readily found in the different reviews that have been cited in this introduction. A comparative study is made between the different anodes with respect to the requirements (1)-(5) we have listed.

### 10.2 Carbon-Based Anodes

Two types of carbon can be used as active anode materials [94, 95]: soft carbon, also called graphitized carbon where crystallites are oriented almost in the same direction, and hard carbon where crystallites have disordered orientations. Both are used in lithium-ion batteries, but they do not have the same properties.

### 10.2.1 Hard Carbon

Before the recent results obtained with soft carbon anodes, hard carbon had the advantage of higher reversibility and high reversible capacity. Currently, the capacity of hard graphite is in the range $200-600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [96-100]. Usually, the problem with hard carbon is not the capacity, but the poor rate capability coming from the slow diffusion process resulting from the many voids and defects associated to the random alignment of the graphene sheets. This problem seems to have been overcome in nano-porous hard carbons synthesized from pyrolyzed sucrose, which deliver a reversible capacity $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with a good cycle life and a good rate capability due to the fast lithium diffusion $4.11 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ for hierarchical nanoporous hard carbon [101].

### 10.2.2 Soft Carbon

The most popular graphite) materials commercialized for anodes are Mesocarbon Microbead (MCMB), Mesophase-pitch-based carbon fiber (MCF), vapor grown carbon fiber (VGCF) and Massive Artificial Graphite (MAG). These soft carbon anodes have a long cycling life and a coulombic efficiency larger than $90 \%$ [54, 102-106]. Their specific capacity is close to the theoretical value $372 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$
corresponding to the formation of $\mathrm{LiC}_{6}$, which, as discussed in the introduction, is a limiting factor, and efforts are currently done to improve this capacity [107]. Two approaches are investigated: use porous carbon, and nano-sized carbon. Actually, mesoporous carbon is also a form of nano-sized carbon. For instance, the length of the mesoporous carbon that delivered a capacity of $1100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the first cycle and $850 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the 20th cycle in [108] was 10.5 nm , the uniform size of the pores was 3.9 nm , and the thickness of the carbon wall was 6.6 nm . Indeed, the nano-sized carbon under its various forms (nanotubes, nanorings, graphene) is most promising. The idea is to decrease the size to such a small scale (typically 10 nm ) that quantum confinements of the electron states modify the electronic structure and electronic properties with respect to those of the bulk material. This modification introduces novel properties that are beneficial to the storage capacity by removing the limitation of the capacity to $372 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [109-114]. Not only quantum effects, but also the increase of the number of storage sites available for the lithium ions in small closed spaces in nanostructured carbon has been proposed to explain the large capacities that have been observed [99, 115, 116]. One example is provided by carbon nanorings with 20 nm outer diameters and $3.5-\mathrm{nm}$ wall thickness (Fig. 10.2), which deliver a capacity $1200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and over a hundreds of cycles at the current density of $0.4 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$. Even at the higher current rates of $45 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$, the capacity is as high as $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [117].

### 10.2.3 Carbon Nanotubes

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are often used together with other active anode elements to improve the electrochemical performance by taking advantage of their superior electronic conductivity, mechanical and thermal stability [107, 118]. The CNTs are divided into single (SWCNTs) and multiwall (MWCNTs) depending on their thickness and the number of coaxial layers. The highest reversible capacity is predicted for SWCNTs and is estimated to be $1116 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in stoichiometry $\mathrm{LiC}_{2}$ owing to the intercalation of lithium into stable sites located on the surface of pseudographitic layers and inside the central tube as well [119-122]. This theoretical prediction is confirmed by experiments, since purified SWCNTs, produced by laser vaporization procedure, yield a capacity larger than $1050 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [123], the largest capacity obtained with SWCNT anodes. This performance has been obtained for a purified SWCNT obtained by laser vaporization. It is very difficult, however, to prepare CNTs that do not contain defects or impurities that degrade importantly the reversible capacity and the coulombic efficiency. Therefore, many efforts are presently devoted to the mode of preparation [107, 119], and the morphology (thickness, porosity, shape) [124, 125].

Until recently, MWCNTs had not reached the same performances as the SGCNTs. Commercial MWCNTs have a typical capacity close to $250 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. After purification, the capacity raises to a $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. However, chemically drilled MWCNT (DMWCNT) has been prepared recently by solid state process [126].

Fig. 10.2 Schematic illustrations and HRTEM images of the different stages in the fabrication of carbon nanorings (CNRs) in the cobalt(II)-aluminum (III) layered double hydroxide (LDH) containing co-intercalated dodecyl sulfonate (DSO) anions and methyl methacrylate (MMA) CoAl-DSO-MMA-LDH matrix: (a) the (CoAl-DSO-MMA-LDHCoAl-DSO-MMA-LDH precursor, (b) CoAl-DSO-MMA-LDH after calcination in an Ar atmosphere at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and (c) isolated CNRs obtained after dissolution of the matrix. Reproduced with permission from [117]. Copyright 2013 Wiley
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-323.jpg?height=1506&width=672&top_left_y=208&top_left_x=676)

In this work, cobalt oxide particles have been deposited on the surface of the nanotubes, and have been removed after an oxidation process, leading to $4 \mathrm{~nm}-$ sized holes. The reversible capacity of the DMWCNT cycled between 0.02 and 3.0 V exceeded $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, remaining constant over the 20 cycles were the material has been tested. An improvement has been achieved for an interface-controlled MWCNT structure, synthesized through a two-step process of catalyst deposition and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and directly grown on a copper current collector [127],
which delivered a capacity $900 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and no capacity degradation up to 50 cycles. The best result with MWCNTs so far has been obtained through directly applying a 10 nm thick layer of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ by atomic layer deposition (ALD) on the MWCNTs anode; the resultant anode demonstrated a stable capacity of $1100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in 50 charge-discharge cycles at the current rate of $372 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [128]. The effects of the ALD- $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ coating have been understood by simulations showing that the coating effectively blocks electron tunneling to the adsorbed EC molecules of the electrolyte, and thus decreases the decomposition of the electrolyte [129]. Therefore, both the experiments and the simulations show that the $\mathrm{ALD}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ coat acts as an "artificial" SEI. Note, however, that this outstanding performance is obtained when directly coating the pre-fabricated electrodes made from bare powders. On another hand, bad results are obtained when coating bare electrode materials via ALD and then fabricate the coated materials into electrodes. In this latter case, the poor performance of the power anode is due to the insulating $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ film inhibiting theses electron conduction paths between the current collector and the active anode element [130]. The recent results mentioned above show that it is now possible to prepare MWCNTs-based anodes with good electrochemical properties. The drawback of the MWCNTs, however, is their tendency to suffer from Li-induced embrittlement [131].
