The $C$-rate capability was also found to be excellent, with 50 and $24 \%$ capacity retention at $20 C$
rate, in comparison to the values at $0.05 C$ rate. Long-term cycling studies, up to 1000 cycles at 5C rate and at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, showed a fading of capacity during the first 200 cycles, from 150 to $120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, a slow degradation up to 750 cycles and finally stabilized at $105 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the range $750-1000$ cycles. This corresponds to a capacity retention of $70 \%$. The reason why the rutile $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ has such outstanding electrochemical properties down to 0.1 V is unknown. It has been suggested [22] that amorphization of nanorutile $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ occurs during the first discharge at least in the few surface layers, resulting in the formation of a composite, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} \cdot \mathrm{TiO}_{y}$ ( $y \leq 1.5$ ), and Li cycling occurs through a "conversion reaction".

### 10.6.1.3 $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$

The bronze polymorph is the last one that accepts insertion of Li . It adopts an open framework structure that enables easy Li transport in it. However, it can only be prepared by the proton exchange of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Ti}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7}$, followed by heat treatment at $400- 500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and this synthesis leads to residual $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ in the $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ and formation of nonnegligible amount of the anatase phase. Nevertheless, $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ - B is an attractive anode material with large reversible capacity. The fast kinetics are illustrated by the performance of porous $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ microspheres with 12 nm pore size, which delivered $120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 60 C rate [346]. At 10 C rate, the sixth reversible capacity was $166 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and retained a value $149 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 5000 cycles. This material was obtained by a five-step template-assisted ultrasonic spray pyrolysis followed by refluxing, ion-exchange, and heat treatment at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Another example is the mixture of $96 \% \mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ nanoribbons, $4 \%$ nanotubes, and $1 \%$ nanospheres obtained by the solution-refluxing method $120^{\circ}$ for 1 week, followed by heat treatment at 400 and $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ [289]. The nanoribbons were 30 nm width, $6-\mathrm{nm}$ thick, and $1-2 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in length, and the BET surface area was $115 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Reversible capacities of 200,150 , and $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ were obtained at rates $C / 3,3 C$, and $15 C$, respectively ( $1 C=330 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ); the capacity loss up to 500 cycles at 3C rate was only $5 \%$. Note, however, that these results were obtained in electrodes with $50 \mathrm{wt} \%$ carbon black loading, an amount of carbon that is prohibited in any commercialized battery. Hierarchical porous $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ with 5-10 thick nanosheets and BET surface area $151 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ delivered a reversible capacity of $211 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $10 C$ rate and retained a capacity at $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles at $10 C$ rate [347]. Nanosheets of pure $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ were also obtained by using multilamellar tetratitanate nanosheets as the reassembly component when $\mathrm{K}^{+}$replaced $\mathrm{H}^{+}$as guest ions for the selfreassembly of nanosheets [348]. The nanosheets were 200 nm in width, $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in length and $4-\mathrm{nm}$ thick, with a BET surface area $66 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. They exhibited a large discharge capacity of $258 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the fifth cycle and maintained a large discharge capacity of $253 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after ten cycles.
$\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ nanowires encapsulated inside and an amorphous carbon layer coating the outside were obtained by hydrothermal process [349]. These carbon- $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ nanowires exhibited a high reversible capacity of $560 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at the current density of $30 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, good cycling stability, and rate capability
( $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ when cycled at the current density of $750 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ). $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ anatase hybrid nanowires with a bicrystalline structure consisting of $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ core and anatase shell was performed by the hydrothermal process and post treatments [350]. The phase fractions by weight were determined to be $92.8(5) \%$ of $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ and $7.2(1) \%$ of anatase. The composite electrode exhibited a highly reversible initial specific discharge and charge capacity of over 256.5 and $232 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively, in the first cycle. It maintained a high capacity of $\sim 196 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at $C / 10$ rate, delivered a capacity $125 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $15 C$, and after the application of $15 C$, recovered to $219.3 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 10$. The high rate capability of $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ has motivated the coupling with $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ counterelectrode, like in the case of Ge . The $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4} / / \mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ cell was tested with $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ nanofiber bundles [351]. The balancing mass ratio of the anode and cathode materials was designed to be $1: 1.5$ by using the specific capacity of $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ of the prepared $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ and $165 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ of the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$, separately. The battery delivered an average voltage 1.8 V with capacities of $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ based on the weight of the $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ in anode. Typically, the battery studied herein exhibited an initial discharge capacity of $160 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the rate of $1 C$; more than 140 and $120 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ discharge capacity was delivered at a rate of $2 C$ and $5 C$, respectively. The battery studied herein exhibited a discharge capacity of $80 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the high rate of $20 C$ and an average capacity loss of no more than $0.05 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ per cycle. The full lithium battery maintained $81 \%$ of its initial capacity after 300 cycles. In summary, it is difficult for $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ to compete with silicon. Like $\mathrm{Si}, \mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ has a low electronic conductivity, but its reversible capacity is much smaller. Nevertheless, $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ has the advantage of reduced volume change upon cycling, which is beneficial to the safety and cycle life. Anatase has always been considered as the best phase for anode applications. However, the recent results showing remarkable properties in the full voltage range $0-3 \mathrm{~V}$ make the rutile phase competitive with respect to anatase. $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}-\mathrm{B}$ remains the phase that allows for the highest rate capability. In fact, $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ is suitable for mass production and it is cost effective. The problem is that its electrochemical properties, irrespective of the polymorph phase, require nano sizes and preferentially composites, so that an anode-based $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ at the end is not cheap. Therefore, the titanium oxide will presumably remain a niche market as an anode material, especially as it also has to compete with another titanium compound, namely $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$.

### 10.6.2 $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$

The $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ (LTO) spinel is considered as the most appropriate titanium-based oxide for use as anode in Li -ion batteries, and is actually considered as a viable anode for Li -ion batteries. It exhibits excellent Li -ion reversibility according to a two-phase reaction maintaining the constant voltage 1.55 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$with a very small hysteresis between charge and discharge; this potential is high, which guarantees safety conditions as the formation of the SEI is mitigated, and the
development of dendrites observed with carbonaceous, and in particular graphite anodes, is avoided. It is cheap, very safe, environmental friendly, and has a remarkable structural stability. Li cycling involves very little change in the cubic lattice parameter so that LTO is a "zero strain" material ideally suited as an anode material. That is why a huge amount of papers has been devoted to this material, and again we guide the reader to the previous reviews on $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ [21,22,38] to focus attention here on the progress achieved in the recent years. The lithium insertion/extraction reaction for $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ can be summarized as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(\mathrm{Li}^{+}\right)_{3(8 \mathrm{a})} \cdot \square 16 \mathrm{c}\left[\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left(\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}\right)_{5}\right]_{(16 \mathrm{~d})}\left(\mathrm{O}^{2-}\right)_{12(32 \mathrm{e})}+3 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \\
& \quad \rightarrow \cdot \square(8 \mathrm{a})\left(\mathrm{Li}^{+}\right)_{6(16 \mathrm{c})}\left[\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left(\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}\right)_{3}\left(\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}\right)_{2}\right]_{(16 \mathrm{~d})}\left(\mathrm{O}^{2-}\right)_{12(32 \mathrm{e})}
\end{aligned}
$$

This reaction equation shows that the migration of the lithium between the tetrahedral ( $8 a$ ) sites and the octahedral ( $16 d$ ) sites oxygen coordination. The theoretical capacity according to this equation is $175 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The electronic conductivity of LTO is small ( $\sigma_{\mathrm{e}} \sim 10^{-12}-10^{-13} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature), due to the fact that the $t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ states of $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$ that form the conduction band of LTO are empty. Fortunately, it improves upon lithiation, because the reaction equation show that some of the titanium ions shift to the $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ valence state, in which one $t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ is occupied, enabling the hopping between $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$ ions. Nevertheless, the consequence of the low value of $\sigma_{\mathrm{e}}$ of virgin LTO is that the theoretical capacity can be approached only at low $C$-rate, especially as the ionic conductivity is also small $\left(3 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right)$. To overcome this problem, the solutions have been the same as for any anode element, starting with the decrease of the size of the particles to the nano-range, and favor porous LTO to increase the active surface area while minimizing the length of the electron and ion path.

The electronic spray deposition (ESD) technique is a way of engineering nanostructured anodes [352]. Already in 2005, the ESD spurs the formation of porous nanoscaled LTO by using lithium acetate and titanium butoxide as the precursors [353]. The obtained LTO showed fractal-like morphology. No obvious structure change (see Fig. 10.8) was observed after annealing at a high temperature ( $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), demonstrating that the LTO film exhibited very good thermal stability. It was found that the anode produced by the ESD provided high energy capacity ( $175 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) at cycling rate $C / 18$, close to its theoretical value in the initial cycle. A capacity of about $155 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was still achieved after ten cycles and remained constant up to 70 cycles. Mesoporous LTO microspheres ( 300 nm -spheres made of 20 nm -thick primary particles were prepared by template-free hydrothermal process in ethanolwater mixed solution aimed at the formation of mesoporous structure, and subsequent heat treatment. This material delivered $114 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 30 C and good capacity retention of $125 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles at $20 C\left(1 C=170 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\right)$. Next step was to associate the nano-LTO with a conducting element under the form of a composite or a coating to improve the rate capability. Nanocrystalline LTO has also been synthesized by single-step-solution-combustion method in less than 1 min [354].

LTO particles thus synthesized are flaky and highly porous in nature with a surface area of $12 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. The primary particles are agglomerated crystallites of varying size between 20 and 50 nm with a 3 -dimensional interconnected porous network. A capacity value close to the theoretical value of $175 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ has been reached at $C / 2$ rate. The electrodes also exhibited promising capacity retention with little capacity loss over 100 cycles at varying discharge rates together with attractive dischargerate capabilities yielding capacity values of 140 and $70 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 10 and $100 C$ discharge rates, respectively. Another synthesis route to fabricate hierarchically porous LTO microspheres made of well-crystalline nanoparticles is a hydrothermal treatment of commercial anatase $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ powder in LiOH solution and a following calcination procedure [355]. The surface area thus obtained was $57.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. At rate $2 C$, this material delivered $147 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with $95 \%$ retention after 200 cycles. Soft chemistry can also be used to prepare spherical LTO particles made of nanoparticles. In particular, such a sample prepared by solvothermal synthesis based on the "benzyl alcohol route" delivered a capacity of $155 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1 C$ rate, with capacity retention of $95 \%$ over 200 cycles, for a surface area of $8 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Porous LTO was also synthesized from mixture of LiCl and $\mathrm{TiCl}_{4}$ with $70 \mathrm{wt} \%$ oxalic acid by a modified one-step solid state method [356]. In this case, the initial capacity was 167 and $133 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 0.5 and $1 C$ charge/discharge rate, respectively, and the capacity retention was maintained above $98 \%$ after 200 cycles. A capacity of $70 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at charge/discharge $10 C$ rate after 200 cycles.

The morphology of the LTO in all these works was the same, namely spheres made of primary nanoparticles. However, other geometries have also been obtained. Hierarchical mesoporous nest-like LTO prepared by hydrothermal synthesis with large surface area $219.2 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ delivered $135 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles at $14 C$ rate, and $113.6 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ were obtained at 57C [357]. Ten nanometer thick nano flowerlike LTO has been synthesized by hydrothermally treating amorphous $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ beads under the additive of LiOH precursor [358]. This sample delivered a capacity of $148,143,141,138,133,126$, and $118 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $0.5,1,3,5,10,20,30 C$, respectively. The reversible capacity at 30 C even remains over $80 \%$ of that at $C / 2$. The superior $C$-rate performance has been associated with the nano flower-like structure, facilitating lithium transportation ability during cycling. To obtain even better results, the nano-LTO (nanorods, hollow spheres, nanoparticles) has been carbon-coated [359, 360]. The results obtained with C-LTO particles of size 90 nm [268] are illustrated in Fig. 10.8. The carbon coat covers the catalytic active sites of LTO particles. Then some authors have deduced that the carbon coat separates the LTO particles from the electrolyte, so that a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film is formed on the carbon layer during the formation process, which can prevent the further reduction decomposition of electrolyte at around 0.7 V , making possible the extension of the voltage range to lower potential [361]. This explanation, however, is not satisfactory, because the SEI forms on the anodes, whether they are carbon, graphite, or metal oxides, as soon as the voltage is lower than 1 V during Li insertion. This is due to the reduction of the solvents present in the electrolyte, namely ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC), aided by the presence of the Li

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-352.jpg?height=510&width=1178&top_left_y=207&top_left_x=173)
Fig. 10.8 (Left) TEM image showing the carbon coat of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ composite (particles of size 90 nm ) and (right) the charge curves of the ${\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Li}_{4}}^{\mathrm{Ti}}{ }_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12} / 1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ in EC-DEC $(1: 1) / \mathrm{Li}$ cell at different $C$ rates. Reproduced with permission from [268]. Copyright 2012 Elsevier

salt $\left(\mathrm{LiPF}_{6}\right)$. That is the reason why all the electrochemical performance reported here have been obtained with the lower voltage at 1 V , unless specified. In addition, the interest in the carbon coating (and actually any coating) is that the coat is porous and does not prevent the contact between the electrolyte and the particles, otherwise the particles would not be active. To the contrary, carbon-coating improves the electrochemical performance. For example, a chain-structured carbon coated LTO prepared by a filter paper templated sol-gel route reached $165 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 5$ and $110 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a high rate of $12 C$ [362]. 100 nm -sized C-LTO primary particles with surface area $12 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ were synthesized by a simple solid-state reaction using $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}, \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$, and pitch [363]. The $5 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{C}$-coated sample had a uniform 3 nm thick carbon coat. At rate $1 C$, the reversible capacity was $165 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with $99 \%$ retention over 100 cycles. A very good cyclability was also obtained at $5 C$ and $10 C$ rates. A $5 \mathrm{wt} \%$ carbon-coated LTO has also been prepared by template-free solvothermal synthesis [364]. In this case, the LTO consisted in $0.5-1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ sized porous microspheres composed of 11 nm -sized crystallites (pore diameter 4.3 nm ). When cycled in the range $1-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$, this C-coated LTO delivered 158 and $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1 C$ and $50 C$, respectively without noticeable capacity fading. In another work, porous LTO particles prepared by spray drying method have been coated with nitrogen-doped carbon that turns out to give better results than non-doped carbon derived from sugar [365]. The best results were obtained with $7 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{~N}$-doped carbon-coated LTO made of spherical particles, size $3-5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ composed of nano-sized aggregates. The capacities at $5 C$ and $10 C$ rates were 145 and $129 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively, stable for at least 20 cycles. At $2 C$ rate, the initial capacity was $150 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, with a capacity retention of $83 \%$ after 2200 cycles. The reduction of the thickness of the carbon coat to 1 nm owing to the use of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a surfactant significantly improved the rate performance of carbon coated LTO by facilitating the diffusion of Li through the carbon layer [366]. The specific capacities of such a C-LTO material with lump
morphology prepared by hydrothermal process at charge and discharge rates of 0.1 , $1,5,10$, and $20 C$ were $176,163,156,151$, and $136 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$.

Another process to improve the performance of LTO has been the synthesis of composites. In particular, just as in the case of the other anode materials reviewed in the previous sections, the composite LTO-graphene. Composites of $1 \mathrm{wt} \%$ graphene and nano-LTO was prepared with a surface area of $170 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ by electrospinning [367]. At a discharging and charging rate of $22 C$, the initial reversible specific capacity of the graphene-embedded LTO nanocomposites was around $110 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. After 1300 cycles a capacity of $101 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was observed ( $91 \%$ of the initial capacity). Composites with other forms of carbon have also been prepared. Urchin-like LTO-carbon nanofiber delivered $123 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ when charged/discharged at $15 C$ [368]. LTO nano-platelets of size $10-20 \mathrm{~nm}$ have been uniformly dispersed on reduced graphite oxide (RGO) to form a nano-hybrid composite ( $72: 38 \mathrm{wt} \%$ ) by microwave-assisted solvothermal reaction followed by heat treatment at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ [369]. Reversible capacities 154, 142, 128, and $101 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ have been obtained at $1,10,50$, and $100 C$ rates, respectively. The capacity retention after 100 cycles was $95 \%$ at 1 and $10 C$ rates. Carbon nanotubes (CNT)-LTO composites have also been prepared by mechanically mixing LTO with CNTs [370-372]. Better results have been obtained by adding carbon, thus forming a LTO/C/CNT composite, synthesized by solid-state method [373]. For a $6 \mathrm{wt} \%$ of total content of C (pitch) and CNTs, the discharge capacities were 163,148 , and $143 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, for charge-discharge rate of $0.5,5$, and $10 C$, respectively. After 100 cycles at $5 C$, it remained at $146 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. An entanglement structure for an LTO/multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) composite is prepared by a ball-milling-assisted solid-state reaction [370]. At 10 C rate, the corresponding electrode delivered $147 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, with a capacity retention $97 \%$ over 100 cycles. In a different synthesis approach, Nano-LTO has also been anchored on MWCNT by liquid phase deposition, owing to the controlled hydrolysis of tetrabutyl titanate [374]. The predetermined amount of MWCNT in nanoLTO/MVCNTs composite was $10 \mathrm{wt} \%$. Then, the MWCNTs surfaces was functionalized using concentrated nitric acid that introduces functional groups such as carboxyl ( -COOH ), hydroxyl ( -OH ), and carbonyl ( CO ) groups. Then, the $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ nanoparticles were anchored on the surface of MWCNTs by the controlled hydrolysis of tetrabutyl titanate. Finally, the $\mathrm{TiO}_{2} / \mathrm{MWCNTs}$ were converted to LTO/MWCNTs nanocomposite by a short heat-annealing. The LTO particles thus obtained were 50 nm thick. Tested in the voltage window $1-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$, these LTO/MWCNTs delivered a capacity of $171 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ (per gram of LTO) at $1 C$ rate, and still $90 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $30 C$ rate, stable over at least 30 cycles. This performance is close to that of the N-doped carbon coated LTO in [365]. MWNT-LTO core/sheath coaxial nanocables (thickness 25 nm ) obtained by solid-state synthesis with rich hierarchical pores providing a high surface area of $80 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ delivered a capacity of $90 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 40 C over 100 cycles [375]. These different results give evidence of the important improvement of the electrochemical properties by the use of carbon nanotubes that facilitate the penetration of the electrolyte and improve the electronic conductivity.

The main disadvantage of LTO with respect to $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ being its lower capacity, a dual-phase LTO- $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ consisting of irregular nanocrystals and nanorods has been synthesized by hydrothermal process with adding thiourea to add up the properties of both oxides [376]. After 300 cycles at $1 C$ rate, the dual-phase LTO-TiO ${ }_{2}$ nanocomposite maintained a capacity of $116 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The large capacity of $132 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was also observed at the current density of $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ upon cycling. This high rate capacity has been attributed to the pseudocapacitive affect induced by the presence of abundant phase interfaces in this composite. In another work, the elements of the dual-phase LTO- $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ have been carbon-coated [377], delivering $110 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $10 C$ up to 100 cycles.

Another prominent route to overcome the low conductivity has been to dope LTO. The doping introduces $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ ions which contain one conducting electron in the $t_{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ sub-band. In this regard, Shen et al. published a facile template-free route to fabricate LTO nanowire arrays growing directly on Ti foil while creating $\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ sites through hydrogenation [378]. At a low $C$-rate (e.g. $C / 5$ ), this electrode achieved a first discharge capacity as high as $\approx 173 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. As the current rate increased from 1 to 5 and 10C, the discharge capacity slightly decreased from 166 to 157 and $145 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. At the high rate of $30 C\left(5.3 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right)$, the capacity retained $69 \%$ of the value achieved at $0.2 C$ with a discharge voltage plateau at 1.33 V , indicating the excellent rate capability of the material. These electrochemical properties are compared in Fig. 10.9 with the capacities delivered by different LTO anodes: $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$-coated LTO [379], nano-crystalline LTO [354], mesoporous LTO/C [380], LTO/graphene [381], carbon-coated LTO [382], phosphidated LTO [383], Ni-doped LTO [384] (figure extracted from ref. [380]). It shows that the hydrogenated LTO nanowires growing directly on Ti foil without any additive gives the best results, comparable to commonly conductive coating electrodes. This is due to the increase of the electronic conductivity resulting from the doping by hydrogenation, and the large space that facilitate the fast transfer of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and small diameter of NW that provides the short diffusion length. LTO has also been doped with supervalent metal ions with respect to $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}: \mathrm{Nb}^{5+}$ [385, 386], $\mathrm{V}^{5+}$ [387, 388]. More surprising, good results have been obtained by Gu et al. for what the authors called doping with $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$ [389] to obtain carbon-coated $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5-x} \mathrm{Zr}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ ( $x=0,0.05$ ). Since $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$ is isovalent with $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}, \mathrm{Zr}$ is not supposed to act as a dopant if it substitutes for Ti ; nevertheless, the electronic conductivity increased with this substitution [390], for unknown reason. But most of all, the remarkable electrochemical properties was observed by extending the voltage range from 2.5 V down to 0 V instead of 1 V , which recalls the work of the Wohlfahrt-Mehrens group on rutile $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ in the previous section. The $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$-doped $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12} / \mathrm{C}$ delivered a discharge capacity $289 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at 0.2 C rate, when extending the voltage range to $0-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$, and the capacity was still $212.6 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $5 C$. Like in the case of rutile $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$, the reason for this improvement, in particular by extending the voltage range down to 0 V is not understood and more investigations on surface reactions of the titanium oxides are clearly needed. Moreover, we cannot take for granted the possibility to use such low voltages to improve the performance of the anodes. In an analysis made on V-doped LTO [386], it has been noticed that the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-355.jpg?height=1227&width=1164&top_left_y=212&top_left_x=180)
Fig. 10.9 Schematic illustrating the synthetic procedure for making highly loaded $\mathrm{CoO} /$ graphene nanocomposites and their electrochemical properties. (a) The first three CV curves of the $\mathrm{CoO} /$ graphene nanocomposite with a mass ratio of $9: 1$ in the potential range of $0.0-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$ at a scan rate of $0.1 \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. (b) The first three discharge-charge curves of the $\mathrm{CoO} /$ graphene nanocomposite with a mass ratio of $9: 1$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and room temperature. (c) Discharge capacities vs. cycle number for the $\mathrm{CoO} /$ graphene nanocomposites with mass ratios of $9: 1,7: 3$, and 4:6, and pure graphene at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and room temperature. (d) Specific capacities of the $\mathrm{CoO} /$ graphene nanocomposite with a mass ratio of $9: 1$ for different discharge-charge cycles at current rates between 0.25 and $8.0 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. (Black: coulombic efficiency; red: discharge capacity; blue: charge capacity). Reproduced with permission from [494]. Copyright 2013 Royal Chemical Society

electrolyte starts to decompose irreversibly below 1.0 V , and SEI film of LTO is formed at 0.7 V in the first discharge process, thus altering the cycling life. In particular, the cycling life has not been investigated in the Zr-doped LTO discharged at 0 V . Other doping with aliovalent ions have also led to some improvement of the electrochemical properties, but not with the same success:
$\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ [391], $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ [392-394], $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ [384, 395, 396], $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ [395], $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ [396], $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ [394, 395], $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ [396], $\mathrm{Ga}^{3+}$ [394], $\mathrm{La}^{3+}$ [397-400], $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ [401, 402], $\mathrm{Mo}^{6+}$ [403], $\mathrm{Mo}^{4+}$ [404], $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ [405], $\mathrm{Ta}^{5+}$ [406], $\mathrm{Ru}^{4+}$ [407], $\mathrm{F}^{-}$[392] and $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$[408]. Much better results have been obtained recently by choosing a different dopent, namely yttrium [409]. The Y-doped LTO (Y concentration 4 at. \%) delivered $141.3 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 1800 cycles at $10 C$ rate. Owing to these outstanding properties, LTO has been recognized since some time as a viable anode for Li -ion batteries, and the progress that have been reviewed in this section confirm this ability. In particular, due to safety considerations, LTO is well suited to HEV applications and in load-shedding. Therefore, batteries with LTO anode have been tested with all the cathodes that are envisioned for Li-ion batteries.

### 10.6.3 Ti-Nb Oxide

The research on new Ti-derived compounds still goes on. Recently, the Goodenough's group worked on $\mathrm{TiNb}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ (TNO), which crystallizes in a monoclinic layer structure [410]. After carbon-coating, this material a reversible capacity of $285 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was obtained and the voltage-capacity profile showed a plateau at 1.6 V. At $C / 5$ and after ten cycles in the standard voltage range $1-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$, the capacity retention was $98 \%$ and a capacity $270 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was stable up to 20 cycles. The $\mathrm{Nb}^{4+}$-doped carbon-coated TNO ( $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Ti}_{0.9} \mathrm{Nb}_{2.1} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ ) gave the best results, with stable performance upon discharge at $2 C$ and charge from $2 C$ up to $600 C$ rate. The safety and structural stability have not been tested, yet.

### 10.7 Oxides Based on Alloying/De-alloying Reaction

### 10.7.1 Si Oxides

Since silicon suffers from the huge change of volume upon lithiation-delithiation, SiO has been viewed as an alternative. SiO is a mixture of amorphous Si and amorphous silica. Lithium oxide ( $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ) is generated during the first lithiation:

$$
\mathrm{SiO}+2 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Si}
$$

thus creating a microstructure in the active material such that Si nanodomains are embedded in the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ matrix [411]. As a result, the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ layers can act as buffer zones and thus suppress the side effects originating from the volume changes of Si . This reaction during the first lithiation also shows that the cycleability in the subsequent cycles is due to the silicon Si that is the only active species, giving reversible capacity via alloying/de-alloying reaction with Li in a matrix of lithium
silicates formed during the first discharge by the reaction between silica and Li . The study of the electrochemical reduction of $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ in hard carbon has shown that $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ is reduced to Si along with the formation of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and/or $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}$ [412]. The works concerning the electrochemistry of SiO and its composites before 2010 have been published in [25]. Progress has still been done since then. Of course, SiO, just like Si and for the same reason, SiO is usually mixed with a conductive element to improve the electrical conductivity of the material, and this element is usually carbon, either by coating, or under the form of composites. The synthesis parameters of SiO , however, also play a role. In particular, disproportionated- SiO (d- SiO ) powders obtained by heat-treatment of SiO at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ has been found to give the best electrochemical performance among the samples tested in terms of the initial Coulombic efficiency and cycle retention [413]. This material milled with graphite powder to obtain a nano- $\mathrm{Si} / \mathrm{SiO}_{x} /$ graphite composite delivered a constant capacity of $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between the 10th cycle and the 200th cycle at rate of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$.
$\mathrm{SiO}_{x} / \mathrm{Si}$ composed of Si -suboxide and embedded Si nanocrystallites were prepared via a partial reduction reaction between ball-milled silicon monoxide and magnesium by high-energy mechanical milling [414]. After carbon-coating via a chemical vapor deposition process, the composite showed a stable reversible capacity of ca. $1250 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and excellent cycling stability with $90.9 \%$ capacity retention on the 100 th cycle versus the 6th one. Coating SiO with N -doped carbon (NC) improves the capacity at any $C$-rate, since it improves the electronic conductivity of the carbon coat. NC-SiO shows $955 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles when cycled at $1 C$, corresponding to a capacity retention of $92 \%$ [415]. This result was obtained with SiO particles as large as $20 \mu \mathrm{~m}$, giving evidence that the side effects (fracture, pulverization of micron-sized Si particles) upon the change in volume of Si have been avoided with SiO . We have already noticed in the previous section, including the section devoted to Si -anodes, that the porosity improves the electrochemical properties by increasing the effective surface area. This is also true for SiO -anodes. Carbon-coated porous SiO have shown an excellent capacity of $1490 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 50 cycles at rate $0.1 C$, and stable capacities of 1100 and $920 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ have been observed at 3 and $5 C$, respectively [416]. The SiO-carbon composite materials consisting of SiO , graphite, and carbon fiber with carbon coatings showed the smallest irreversible capacity at the first cycle and the best capacity retention among the other silicon-based materials examined, such as carbon-coated silicon, the one-to-one mixture of " SiO " and graphite, or one-to-one mixture of carboncoated "SiO" and graphite [417]. Capacity of more than $85 \%$ compared to capacities observed for initial ten cycles is retained after 100 cycles when the laminate-type cell with a positive electrode is examined in voltage ranging from 2.5 to 4.2 V . In a different work, $\mathrm{Si}-\mathrm{SiO}_{2}-\mathrm{C}$ composites have also been synthesized by ball milling the mixture of SiO , graphite, and coal pitch in the mass ratio mass ratio of $3: 1.5: 1.5$, respectively, and subsequent heat treatment at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in inert atmosphere. This composite delivered a reversible Li-alloying/de-alloying capacity of $700 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and excellent cyclic stability even at about the 90 th cycle at the current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. An even better result was obtained with a ballmilled SiO and carbon nanofiber (CNF) composite anode that delivered the same
capacity of $700 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles [418]. This result comes from the reduction of the irreversible capacity at the first cycle for the SiO/CNF composite electrode was reduced to $2 \%$ by chemically pre-charging with a lithium film attached to the rim of the electrode. Silicon monoxide/graphite/multiwalled carbon nanotubes ( $\mathrm{SiO} / \mathrm{G} / \mathrm{CNTs}$ ) material prepared by ball milling followed by chemical vapor deposition method exhibited an initial specific discharge capacity of $790 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with a columbic efficiency of $65 \%$. After 100 cycles at a constant current density of $230 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, a high reversible capacity of $495 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was still retained [419]. A novel $\mathrm{SiO} /$ graphene composite exhibited a high initial specific capacity of $2285 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, excellent cyclic performance of $890 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 100th cycle and good rate capability, which was ascribed to the three-dimensional architecture of $\mathrm{SiO} /$ graphene nanocomposite [420].

A different approach comes from the consideration that the $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ in disproportionated SiO has been considered as a main matrix phase and a cause of the initial irreversible reaction [421]. Hence the idea to reform this matrix phase to retain less irreversible electrochemical reactivity and more reinforced mechanical properties. This has been done in ref. [422], where a nanostructured $\mathrm{SiAl}_{0.2} \mathrm{O}$ composite material has been developed via mechanochemical synthesis. The compositional structure of the matrix is aluminosilica in which most of Si atoms are neighbored with two Al atoms over oxygen. This composite offered a capacity of $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 100 cycles at a current density of $120 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$.

It should also be noted that attention should be taken to the fact that the performance of SiO -anodes depend importantly on the choice of binder, which makes difficult the quantitative comparison of the electrochemical performance between different works. The study of the electrochemical properties for different binders has been made by Komaba et al. [423] who showed that the electrochemical reversibility of SiO anodes is drastically improved by using poly(acrylic acid) (PAA) as the binder in comparison to the PVdF, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (NaCMC), and poly(vinyl alcohol) PVA binders. Polyimide as binder was also found to give very good results [424]. A concern with SiO is the thermal instability due to the sudden exothermic peak in the region of $350-400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This enthalpy peak, however, is importantly reduced (but not suppressed) by coating SiO with anatase $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ [425]. This has been attributed to the thermal stability of lithiated $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ and its SEI [279, 280].

Columnar-shape $\mathrm{SiO}_{x}$ nanoconifers were directly self-assembled on metallic $\mathrm{NiSi}_{x}(0.9<x<1)$ nanowires by means of chemical vapor deposition technique [426]. The first discharge and charge capacities of these $\mathrm{SiO}_{x}$ nanoconifers were 4058 and $1737 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the current density of $150 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, which produce the irreversible capacity loss of $57 \%$ at the very first cycle. After the first cycle, the $\mathrm{SiO}_{x}$ nanoconifer cell shows very high coulombic efficiency over $97 \%$ for the subsequent cycles. After 20 cycles, the capacity is measured to be $1375 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, which corresponds to $80 \%$ of the initial capacity. Then, its capacity is gradually degraded to about $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ up to the 100 th cycle. Other multicomposites have been recently synthesized. Carbon-coated $\mathrm{SiO}_{x}$ and PVdF binder was uniformly coated with Cr by ion beam sputtering [427]. The Cr coating was
found to improve the electrochemical performance of the electrode. The first charge capacity of the coated anode was $1127 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 10$. The second discharge capacity was $517 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and after 100 cycles at the $C / 10$ rate, the capacity was still $517 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Mechanically alloyed composite anode materials based on SiO$\mathrm{Sn}_{x} \mathrm{Fe}_{y} \mathrm{C}_{z}$ have also been investigated, and the composition $x, y, z$ adjusted to optimize the electrochemical properties [428]. The $50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{SiO}-50 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{Sn}_{30} \mathrm{Fe}_{30} \mathrm{C}_{40}$ composition exhibits high specific capacity ( $900 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 6$ rate) with good cycle life up to 40 cycles, but the capacity decreases at subsequent cycles.

Contrary to the $\mathrm{SiO}, \mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ has not been considered as a competitor to sicon anodes. However, recent efforts have been made to improve the performance of silica. Hollow porous $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ nanocubes prepared via a two-step hard-template process exhibited a reversible capacity of $919 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 30 cycles at the current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ between 3 and 0 V [429]. In the same way, $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ nanotubes fabricated via a facile two step hard-template growth method exhibited a highly stable reversible capacity of $1266 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles with negligible capacity fading [430]. In both cases, the hollow morphology of the $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ nanotubes or nanocubes accommodates the large volume expansion experienced by Si-based anodes during lithiation and promotes preservation of the solid electrolyte interphase layer.

### 10.7.2 $\mathrm{GeO}_{2}$ and Germanates

$\mathrm{GeO}_{2}$ has been studied since 10 years as a potential anode material [431]. During the first discharge reaction, an amorphous $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} \cdot \mathrm{GeO}_{2}$ phase forms at 0.65 V , and in the range $0.55-0.35 \mathrm{~V}$, crystal structure destruction occurs to form the nano composite $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} \cdot \mathrm{Ge}$ and ( LiGe ). This is followed by the formation of the alloy, $\mathrm{Li}_{4.2} \mathrm{Ge}$ in the voltage range $0.35-0.05 \mathrm{~V}$. As a result, a drastic capacity fading is observed due, at least partly, to the large change of volume of the unit cell resulting from the alloying/de-alloying reaction $\mathrm{Ge}+4.2 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+4.2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{4.2} \mathrm{Ge}$. A recent progress, however, has been achieved by using Ge with a conductive carbon coating [432]. $\mathrm{GeO}_{2} / \mathrm{Ge} / \mathrm{C}$ was prepared in a three steps process, starting with the synthesis of $\mathrm{GeO}_{2}$ from hydrolysis of $\mathrm{GeCl}_{4}$ and followed by carbon coating with acetylene gas, resulting in $\mathrm{GeO}_{2} / \mathrm{C}$. The last step was the reduction of $\mathrm{GeO}_{2} / \mathrm{C}$ to $\mathrm{GeO}_{2} / \mathrm{Ge} / \mathrm{C}$ through heating at $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The composite showed high capacities of 1860 and $1680 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, at the current rates of $1 C$ and $10 C$, respectively, with good cycling stability over 50 cycles at 0.5 discharge rate and $1 C\left(2.1 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right)$ charge rate. This improvement in the performance was attributed to the enhancement of the reversibility of the alloying/de-alloying reaction of $\mathrm{GeO}_{2}$ with lithium by the carbon coating and the catalytic effect of Ge . Among the Ge -based compounds [433, 434], $\mathrm{LiGe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ has the most promising electrochemical properties. It adopts the Nasicon-type structure containing $\mathrm{GeO}_{6}$ octahedra and $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ tetrahedra, while Li occupies the 3D channels. A reversible capacity of $460 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was obtained
when LGP was cycled in the voltage range $0.001-1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ at a current rate of $150 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, and $92 \%$ of the capacity was retained after 25 cycles. At a high current of $1500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}, 77 \%$ capacity retention was noted after 1000 cycles [433].

### 10.7.3 Sn Oxides

During the first discharge, SnO and $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ undergo structural destruction (amorphization) and formation of nano Sn -metal dispersed in amorphous $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ according to the reactions:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{SnO}+2 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \\
\mathrm{SnO}_{2}+4 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{Sn}+2 \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} .
\end{gathered}
$$

These two-phase reactions are irreversible and responsible for an irreversible loss of capacity during the first cycle. This is followed by the reversible reactions that contribute to the reversible capacity:

$$
\mathrm{Sn}+4.4 \mathrm{Li} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{4,4} \mathrm{Sn} .
$$

Thus, the theoretical reversible capacities of SnO and $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ are 875 and $782 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. The cycling performance is improved with respect to those of Sn metal, owing to the formation of nano- $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ that acts as a buffer during alloying/de-alloying of Sn (Eq. 10.9), thus maintaining the integrity of the Sn particles. Nevertheless, the problem of capacity fading has never been solved for SnO , despite the numerous efforts that have been reviewed in ref. [22]. The efforts today are focused on $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ that has better electrochemical properties, also linked to the fact that it is the stable form of tin oxide. The optimum voltage range for the alloying-dealloying reaction of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ is $0.005-1 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$. At higher cut-off voltage, $\mathrm{SnO}_{x}$ forms according to the reaction:

$$
\mathrm{Sn}+x \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{SnO}_{x}+2 x \mathrm{Li}^{+}+2 x \mathrm{e}^{-}(x<2),
$$

which invariably gives rise to a capacity fading because of the large change of volume that is associated to this reaction, in addition to the change of volume associated to the alloying-dealloying reaction of Sn in Eq. (10.9). Therefore, until recently, a capacity circa $1494 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ associated to Eq. (10.10) could be observed only in the first cycles, and fades to the value associated to the reversible reaction (Eq. 10.9). That is why the theoretical capacity of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ is usually considered to be $782 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The study of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ upon cycling has been performed by TEM experiments and discussed in detail in some papers [435, 436] and a review [437]. Again, we only refer to the previous reviews (see also [22]) for the studies prior 2009, and focus attention on more recent results. For $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$, like for any anode
nowadays, the material is always prepared at the nanoscale to improve the conductivity and optimize the capacity of the material to suffer the change of volume upon cycling without loss of structural integrity.
$\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanorod arrays of average 60 nm in diameter and 670 nm in length have been prepared on large-area flexible metallic collector substrates via a hydrothermal process [438]. A reversible capacity of $580 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at the $0.1 C$ rate ( $C$ was defined as $4.4 \mathrm{Li}^{+}$per hour; $781 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) and shows good rate capability ( $350 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the $5 C$ rate). The electrode was obtained by thoroughly mixing $75 \mathrm{wt} \%$ active materials, $15 \mathrm{wt} \%$ carbon black, and $10 \mathrm{wt} \%$ polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF) in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidene (NMP) solvent. The addition of a large concentration of carbon is always beneficial to the electronic conductivity and acts as a buffer to facilitate the structural stabilization of the active material, but in a commercial battery the concentration of carbon in the electrolyte does not exceed $10 \mathrm{wt} \%$. This is the amount of carbon that has been mixed with ultra-fine porous $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanopowder ( 5 nm ) synthesized by a simple, easily scaled-up molten salt method [439]. This electrode delivered a reversible capacity of $410 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles in the voltage range of $0.05-1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ at $0.1 C$. The reversible charge capacity at the 5 and $10 C$ rates during the first cycles is about 400 and $300 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. This result is another example of the efficiency of the combination nano and porous, which we have already evidenced in the case of the Si anode. In the same way, mesoporous $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ spheres in the range of $100-300 \mathrm{~nm}$ delivered $761 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ capacity after 50 cycles at the current density of $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Even at $2 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, it retained $480 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles [440]. The self-assembly of tin dioxide ( $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ ) porous microspheres conducted via a surfactant-free onestep hydrothermal reaction delivered a stable capacity about $690 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at a current density of $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. These results are less spectacular than some others reported hereunder, in particular when combined with graphene, but this anode is also more scalable. Other morphologies have been explored. $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanofibers consisting of orderly bonded nanoparticles have been obtained by thermal pyrolysis and oxidization of electrospun tin(II)2-ethylhexanoate/polyacrylonitrile (PAN) polymer nanofibers in air [441]. They delivered a reversible capacity of $446 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at the $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ rate and excellent rate capability of $477.7 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $10 C$ rate.

In the form of thin films, the best results have been obtained with $\mathrm{Sn}-\mathrm{Co}-\mathrm{O}$ and Sn-Mn-O films [442, 443]. In particular, at $C / 2$ rate, the Sn-Co-O films delivered a reversible capacity of $734 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Interestingly, this capacity increases to $845 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles. Such an increase upon cycling has been also observed in other $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$-anodes, and we shall return on this property when discussing the case of the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} / \mathrm{N}$-doped graphene hybrid material.
$\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ hollow structures have been reviewed in ref. [53]. Such structures, however, failed to alliavate entirely the capacity fading associated to the large volume change upon cycling, and good results can be obtained only up to circa $30-50$ cycles. Coating $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ is another route that has been explored to improve the anode performance. Electrodes have been built with carbon-coated $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles ( $6-10 \mathrm{~nm}$-thick) prepared by hydrothermal process [444]. The as-prepared
$\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$-carbon with $8 \mathrm{wt} \%$ carbon could deliver a capacity as high as $631 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ even after 100 charge/discharge cycles at a current drain of $400 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. This result is actually better than nanoparticles of $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ embedded in $12 \mathrm{wt} \%$ carbon matrix [445] despite the porosity of the nanoparticles. More recently, interconnected ultrafine $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}-\mathrm{C}$ core-shell ( $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}-\mathrm{C}$ ) nanospheres have been successfully synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method and subsequent carbonization, giving rise to outstanding electrochemical properties [446]. Discharge capacity reaches as high as $1215 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, giving evidence that the problem of the aging associated to the volume change associated to the reaction in Eq. (10.11) upon cycling has been solved. Even at $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the capacity is still $520 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and can be recovered up to $1232 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ if the current density is turned back to $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. A carbon-coated $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}-\mathrm{NiO}$ nanocomposite electrode exhibited a reversible capacity of about $529 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $800 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, and $265 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, even after 500 cycles [447]. A larger capacity but with larger capacity fading has been obtained with nano- Si -coated nanotube $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ with the composition 40:60 $\mathrm{wt} \%$. At 0.5 C rate in the voltage range $0-1.2 \mathrm{~V}$, this anode delivered an initial reversible capacity of $1800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, slowly degrading to $1600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 90 cycles. This high capacity is due to the fact that both Si and $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ are electrochemically active. However, since both Si and $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ suffer large volume changes upon cycling, some deterioration of the tube wall structure was evidenced by TEM studies after 90 cycles at $2 C$ rate. The best result, however, has been obtained in 2014 by coating $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ with $\mathrm{HfO}_{2}$ by atomic layer deposition [448]. This anode delivered a capacity of $853 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at a current density of $150 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Finally, a novel high performance cathode material for LiBs has been developed by coating $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ on $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanowires, utilizing the better conductivity of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanowires and the short diffusion distance of the thin $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ layer [449]. This material delivers a high power density of about $60 \mathrm{~kW} \mathrm{~kg}{ }^{-1}$, while the energy density remains $282 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$.

Different composites have been investigated, combining $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ with a metallic compound, such as Cu [450, 451]. Good results have also been obtained with a conductive polymer: $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles uniformly decorated polypyrrole (PPy) nanowires could deliver $690 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with $90 \%$ capacity retention between the 2nd and the 80th cycle at current rate $690 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ in the voltage range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$ [452]. However the best results have been obtained with carbon under different forms [453-459]. $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} /$ multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) made of an uniform layer of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanocrystals with crystal size around 5 nm was deposited on the surface of the carbon nanotubes have been synthesized by the solvothermal method [456]. The reversible capacity was $709 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the first cycle, stabilizing to circa $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 100 cycles in the voltage range of $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$ at a constant current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ based on the weight of the composite. A $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} /$ multiwalled carbon nanotubes core-shell structure prepared by wet chemical route delivered an initial discharge capacity and reversible capacity up to 1472.7 and $1020.5 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively [456]. Moreover, the reversible capacity still remained above $720 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 35 cycles in the voltage range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$, at
current density $0.2 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$, and the capacity fading was only $0.8 \%$ per cycle. This performance is attributed to the large surface area of the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ that is deposited on MWCNTs that are only 3 nm-thick, the high conductivity of the MWCNTs, and the fact that the MWCNTS avoid the agglomeration of the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ particles. The integrity of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} /$ carbon nanotubes is best evidenced by the good capacity retention observed in [458] with a reversible capacity of $540 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ almost stable over 200 cycles at 0.5 C rate. A novel ethylene glycol-mediated solvothermal-polyol route for synthesis of highly dispersed $3-5 \mathrm{~nm} \mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanocrystals on the surface of MWCNTs showed high rate capability and superior cycling stability with specific capacity of $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for up to 300 cycles [460]. The capacity of a $\mathrm{Sn} / \mathrm{SnO}_{2} /$ MWCNT composite still delivers a capacity of $624 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at $C / 4$ rate [460].

Carbon-tin oxide ( $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ ) nanofibers have also been synthesized aiming to complement the long cycle life of carbon with the high lithium storage capacity of tin oxide [461]. The composite nanofibrous anodes are binder-free and have first discharge capacities of $788 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ current density. After this pioneering work, this technology has been improved. In particular, $\mathrm{SnO}_{2^{-}}$ electrodeposited porous carbon nanofibers (PCNF-SnO2) composites that can maintain their structural stability during repeated charge-discharge cycling have been recently obtained [462]. After coating with amorphous carbon layers by chemical vapor deposition, this PCNF-SnO2-C composite delivered capacities of 713, 568, 463, and $398 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively, at $100,200,400$, and $800 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ with high capacity retention of $78 \%$ and large coulombic efficiency of $99.8 \%$ at the 100th cycle. The charge capacity value returned to $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ under a reduced current density of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after undergoing these cycles at higher current densities. Remarkable results have been obtained owing to the fabrication of ultra-uniform $\mathrm{SnO}_{x} /$ carbon nanohybrids (denoted as U-SnO ${ }_{x} / \mathrm{C}$ ) by solvent replacement and subsequent electrospinning homogeneous dispersion of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles in polyacrylonitrile (PAN) $/ N, N$ dimethylformamide (DMF) solution [463]. The resulting 1D nanostructure U-SnO ${ }_{x} / \mathrm{C}$ holds strong interaction between $\mathrm{SnO}_{x}$ and nitrogen containing carbon nanofiber matrix that effectively confine the uniformly embedded $\mathrm{SnO}_{x}$. After 200 deep charge/discharge cycles at $0.5 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$ between 0.005 and 3 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$, the U-SnO ${ }_{x} / \mathrm{C}$ electrode still exhibits a reversible capacity of $608 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. As the current densities increase stepwise from 0.5 to $1,2,5$, and $10 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$, the electrode delivers stable capacities at each of these rates, varying from 663 to $518,365,175$, and $80 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. In a different geometry, coaxial $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$-carbon hollow nanospheres (carbon content $32 \mathrm{wt} \%$ ) cycling performance was evaluated between 2 V and 5 mV at a $0.8 C$ rate (where C does not have the usual meaning but is defined as $625 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ in this work) stabilizes around $460 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for more than 100 cycles [464]. at a high rate of $4.8 C$, the hollow spheres can still deliver a stable capacity of about $210 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. We recover here the higher performance when the carbon content is larger.

The extensive and promising study of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} /$ graphene composites started in 2010-2011 [465-473]. More recently, a composite made from graphene nanoribbons and $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles has been synthesized [474]. This composite
exhibits reversible capacities of over 1520 and $1130 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for the first discharge and charge, respectively, which is more than the theoretical capacity of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$. The reversible capacity retains $825 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ with a coulombic efficiency of $98 \%$ after 50 cycles. Further, the composite shows good power performance with a reversible capacity of $\sim 580 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the current density of $2 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. A composite made from graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles has also been tested as an anode [474]. The $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles of diameter 10 nm were synthesized via a simple chemical method, and they were uniformly distributed among the GNR structure layers. The GNRs were made by $\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{K}$ unzipping of MWCNTs. The reversible capacity retained $825 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ with a coulombic efficiency of $98 \%$ after 50 cycles. Further, the composite shows good power performance with a reversible capacity of $580 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the current density of $2 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. The remarkable performance is due to the fact that GNRs are known to enhance lithium storage through edge effects [142,474], and in addition can buffer the change of volume of the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ particles. This property of graphene that also avoids the formation of agglomerates has also been the motivation for using situ hydrazine monohydrate vapor reduction method for binding $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanocrystals in graphene sheets by $\mathrm{Sn}-\mathrm{N}$ bonding to obtain a $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanocrystal/nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide hybrid material [475, 476]. The $4-5-\mathrm{nm}$ thick $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ particles were synthesized by hydrothermal process. After initial conditioning cycles, the coulombic efficiency increases to more than $97 \%$, and a stable capacity of $1021 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ is obtained for the hybrid under a current density of $0.5 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$ in the voltage range of $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$ vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$. In this work, the specific capacity values are calculated on the basis of the total mass of the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} / \mathrm{N}$-doped graphene hybrid material, so that $1021 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ indicates that a capacity of $1352 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanocrystals, which is very close to the theoretical capacity of $1494 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ according to the reaction (Eq. 10.10). Moreover, the capacity increases upon cycling, to reach a reversible charge capacity as high as $1346 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 500 cycles. This increase is attributed to the improvement of lithium ion accessibility in the hybrid during the cycling process, which leads to an increased accommodation behavior for lithium [477]. In any case, this large value of the capacity implies the additional capacity contribution from the conversion reaction of Sn with $\mathrm{LiO}_{2}$ in Eq. (10.10). As the current densities increase from 0.5 to $1,2,5,10$, and $20 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the electrode shows good capacity retention, varying from 1074 to $994,915,782,631$, and $417 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. When the current density returns to $0.5 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$, the charge capacity reverts to $1034 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The decrease of Sn L3-edge peak intensity observed in the hybrid compared with pure $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanocrystals, suggesting a higher electron density at the Sn site in the hybrid, confirming the formation of $\mathrm{Sn}-\mathrm{N}$ bonds in the hybrid, which is probably indispensable for the high and stable electrochemical performance of the hybrid. This is by far the best result obtained with $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ anode, and the performance is remarkable, since the decrease of the size of the particles to $4-5 \mathrm{~nm}$, plus the high flexibility of graphene, plus the fact that the Sn-N bonding has anchored the nanoparticles on the graphen sheets, avoiding the agglomeration of the particles all result in a hybrid that can accommodate the change of volume
associated to the reaction in Eq. (10.10) during long-term cycling (500 cycles). Most recently, a flexible graphene film decorated with spindle-like $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}-\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles was fabricated through vacuum filtration of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}-\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ and GO mixing solution, followed by thermal reduction [478]. The core-shell structured $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}-\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ nanoparticles were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal route and uniformly dispersed between layered graphene nanosheets. This anode showed an excellent cycling performance of $1015 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ even after 200 cycles. Another remarkable result has been obtained with $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ hollow spheres (HS) $140-150 \mathrm{~nm}$ in dimensions embedded in graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and enveloped by a sheath of a conducting polymer, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) or PEDOT [479]. Owing to the synergy between the moderately high intrinsic electronic conductivity of GO nanosheets and the ability of PEDOT to buffer the volume change during repetitive $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$charge-discharge, the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ HS/GO/PEDOT hybrid capacity of $608 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ to the hybrid, retained at the end of 150 cycles, and the latter value was $1248 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ when the mass of only the $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} \mathrm{HS}$ in the hybrid was considered. The $\mathrm{SnO}_{2} \mathrm{HS} / \mathrm{GO} /$ PEDOT hybrid also showed an excellent rate capability as a capacity of $381 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was attained even at a high current density of $2000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$.

These results clearly upgrade $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ as a prospective anode. The breakthrough in 2013 has been to succeed in the possibility to synthesize diverse nanostructures hybrids that avoid the structural degradation and thus the capacity fading associated to the large changes of volume during the reaction (4). However, it is doubtful that these anodes can be prepared at the industrial scale, because of the price: graphene is expensive, so is the synthesis process. Scaling-up the preparation of such nanostructured composites to quantities required for commercial development also remains challenging, and the key issue to insure the future success of this material as an anode.

### 10.8 Anodes Based on Conversion Reaction

The conversion or redox, reaction involves the formation and decomposition of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ according to the so-called conversion-displacement reaction [480-483]:

$$
M \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{Li}^{+} \rightarrow M+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O},
$$

where $M$ is a 3d-metal: $M=\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ni}, \mathrm{Cu}$. Although electrochemically inert, $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ can participate indirectly to the anode performance by catalyzing the reaction. The first discharge reaction with Li metal involves the amorphization of the lattice, followed by the formation of nanoparticles of metal embedded into the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ matrix. During charge, the re-formation of $M O$ is a consequence of the decomposition of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. Therefore the voltage vs. capacity curve of the $M \mathrm{O}$ has the same features. A flat voltage region below 1 V , characteristic of a two-phase reaction due to the coexistence of $M$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ during the amorphization process, followed by a sloping
region down to 0.001 V characteristic of a single phase Li insertion reaction. Except FeO that crystallizes in a cubic defect structure, and CuO that adopts a distorted rock salt structure due to a Jahn-Teller (JT) distortion, the MO compounds adopt the cubic rock salt structure. The properties of these materials have been reviewed in the past [22], and again, attention is focused here on the progress that have been made in the recent years.

### 10.8.1 CoO

The theoretical capacity after Eq. (10.11) is $715 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, large enough to make CoO a promising anode. Indeed, an experimental capacity close to this value can be achieved at low rate. However, cobalt oxides suffer from poor capacity retention due to their low electrical conductivity and large volume swings during the chargedischarge process. Many efforts have been made to resolve the aforementioned problems by minimizing the volume change of cobalt oxide-based anodes, following the same attempts as in the case of the other anodes we have already reviewed.

In addition to the $3-6 \mathrm{~nm}$ thick SEI film that is formed during the first discharge process on the surface of CoO -based anodes, just as in the case of any other anode, a polymeric gel-type film of thickness $50-100 \mathrm{~nm}$ forms on the CoO electrode in the fully discharged state, which becomes thiner upon charging above 1.8 V , and fully disappears upon charging to 3 V . The decomposition of this polymer-gel layer is invoked to explain the observation of capacities much larger than the theoretical value (consumption of more than 2.0 mol of Li per mole of CoO noticed during the first discharge) [482]. In addition, the formation of higher oxides ( $\mathrm{Co}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ ) by the decomposition of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ contributes to this increase of capacity [484]. This feature has also been invoked to explain the increase of capacity upon cycling that is commonly observed in cobalt oxide [484, 485]. However, we have also noted that this effect is more general as it has been also observed in $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ anodes. In any case, the increase of capacity gives evidence of surface modification upon cycling, also the phenomena is not fully understood.

Reddy et al. have synthesized CoO particles with a coral-like structure by carbothermal reduction with $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as the precursor [486]. The 60th discharge cycle capacity is $895 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and the charge cycle capacity is $893 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ (cycles in the voltage range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$, current density $60 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ). Only few experiments used doping to improve the conductivity of CoO , and thus the rate performance. Cu -doped $\mathrm{h}-\mathrm{CoO}$ nanorods have been found to deliver a capacity of circa $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between 30 and 50 cycles at current density of $72 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$; unfortunately, results at a higher current density have not been reported in this case [487]. Good results at higher $C$ rates have been obtained by combining nanoscale and porosity. In particular, the synthesis of CoO porous nanowire arrays with robust mechanical adhesion to a flexible conductive Ti foil exhibit good high-rate capability at a rate of $1 C\left(716 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right), 2 C\left(1432 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right), 4 C\left(2864 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right)$, and $6 C$ ( $4296 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ), respectively [488]. Hierarchically self-assembled mesoporous CoO
nanodisks synthesized by the reaction of cobalt acetate tetrahydrate with hydrazine hydrate in solution followed by heat treatment in an inert atmosphere delivered a capacity of $1118.6 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 discharge-charge cycles at $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and excellent cycling performance: $633.5 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 400 discharge-charge cycles at $800 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [489]. This remarkable result has been attributed to the he unique hierarchical nanoarchitecture with highly beneficial combination of small primary particles with mesoporous, ultra-thin, large area 2D structure, and orderly selfstacked 3D structure. Actually, this performance is sufficient to make this material a promising anode for the next generation of Li-ion batteries, and this synthesis process is reported to be a facile, cheap, and easily scaled-up strategy, which may compensate for the fact that cobalt is expensive. Nevertheless, at higher $C$-rates, the association with a conductive material under the form of a coating or a composite, just as in the case of the other anode materials.

Among the hybrid coated CoO particles that have been studied, $\mathrm{Co}-\mathrm{CoO}$ nanoparticles consisting of an unsealed hollow porous CoO shell with a metal Co core with a void between the core and the shell maintained a reversible capacity over $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at current density $50 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the potential window $0-3 \mathrm{~V}$, with an initial coulombic efficiency of $74.2 \%$ [490]. Almost the same result has been obtained with a carbon-decorated CoO sample, which also delivered $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 70 cycles in the voltage range of $0.01-3.0 \mathrm{~V}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [491]. $\mathrm{CoO} / \mathrm{NiSi}_{x}$ core-shell nanowire arrays synthesized through a facile CVD and subsequent RF-sputtering approach delivered a capacity stabilized at circa $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 1 C rate, and still $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at 44 C rate [492], increasing importantly the rate capability of CoO anodes. The same group obtained a nano-architectured current collector by growing a 3D array of Cu nanorods onto a Cu foil via electrodeposition assisted by a porous alumina membrane that was subsequently dissolved. Then, the deposition of CoO onto Cu nanorods was achieved by controllable rf-sputtering, to form nanostructured hybrid $\mathrm{CoO} / \mathrm{Cu}$ electrodes [493]. At a current density of $215 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}(0.3 C)$, the anode delivered the first discharge capacity of $1362 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and the first charge capacity of $903 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. It corresponds to a coulombic efficiency of $66 \%$, which is a good result for a cobalt oxide where an important irreversible capacity loss is observed during the first cycle, because of the formation of the SEI plus the amorphisation of the particles. A stable reversible capacity of circa $900 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was still retained after 200 cycles. This is a remarkable result showing that the problem of the capacity fading arising from the aggregation of Co [441] has been overcome. At 10 C rate, the capacity was still larger than $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. A self-assembled echinus-like nanostructure consisting of mesoporous CoO nanorod-carbon nanotube core-shell material showed high capacities ( $703-746 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in 200 cycles) and a long cycle life ( $0.029 \%$ capacity loss per cycle) at a high current rate ( $3580 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) [494].

We have already seen in the previous sections that graphene sheets have been widely used as an ideal matrix for anchoring a number of active anode materials to form unique nanocomposites. Such has been also the case with CoO . Until recently, however, these cobalt oxide/graphene nanocomposites have a relatively low
loading of cobalt oxides with an unsatisfactory dispersion on graphene nanosheets. Highly loaded, well-dispersed cobalt oxide/graphene nanocomposites have been synthesized only recently [495], by self-assembly in an oil-phase solution. This synthesis involved the thermal decomposition of cobalt acetylacetonate $\operatorname{Co}(\mathrm{acac})_{3}$ by oleylamine in the presence of graphene (see Fig. 10.9). At a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}, \mathrm{CoO} /$ graphene nanocomposites with mass ratio of $9: 1$ delivered a constant capacity $1400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 60 cycles. Even at a current density as high as $8 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$, the nanocomposite electrode is capable of delivering a stable capacity of about $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Indeed, these performances are better than some prior results on CoO-graphene nanosheets [496]. However, an even better result has been obtained by Peng et al. [497]. These authors designed a facile one-step ultrasonic way to synthesize CoO quantum dots ( $3-8 \mathrm{~nm}$ ) (Qds) on graphene nanosheets (GNs) by using a metal carbonyl $\left(\mathrm{Co}_{4}(\mathrm{CO})_{12}\right)$ cluster as the precursor at ambient temperature. This anode delivered a reversible discharge capacity of $996 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the second cycle at current rate of $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ (always in the voltage range $0-$ 3 V ), increasing with the cycle number to reach $1592 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the 50th cycle. $1008 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was still retained after the 50th cycle at $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Finally, CoO nanoparticles with size 5 nm densely anchored on graphene nanosheets maintained a stable capacity of $1015 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for 520 cycles with $100 \%$ coulombic efficiency [498]. Indeed, transmission electron microscopy analysis has confirmed that the morphology of the CoO particles is preserved along these cycles. The recent improvement of the electrochemical properties give CoO the status of an attractive anode. Nevertheless, CoO suffers form the fact that cobalt is expensive and toxic. In addition, this oxide is not very stable, with a tendency to transform in the more stable spinel phase $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$.

### 10.8.2 NiO

Nickel oxide ( NiO ) is considered to be a promising electrode material for lithium ion batteries and supercapacitors due to its low cost, low toxicity, and superior safety. In addition, the density of NiO is $6.67 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, leading to high volumetric energy density. For these reasons, considerable efforts have been made in the past to synthesize nano-NiO and its composites (for a review, see ref. [22]). Until 2010, however, the reversible capacities obtained on all kinds of NiO -anodes such as $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{NiO}$ core shell particles [499], NiO hollow nanospheres [500], NiO microspheres [501], NiO-carbon nanocomposites [502-504], NiO porous thin films [505-507], NiO/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) composites [508] were confined in the range $250-650 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after $20-50$ cycles at current rate $0.1-1 C$ in the voltage range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$. Similar results have been obtained in 2011 with Co-doped NiO Nanoflake arrays showing a capacity of $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 discharge/charge cycles at low current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, and it retains $471 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ when the current density is increased to $2 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [509]. The doping is important since the electrochemical performance is significantly improved with
respect to undoped ingle-crystalline NiO nanoflake arrays directly on copper substrates by a modified hydrothermal synthesis and post-annealing [510].

Another important parameter is the porosity.
