Mesoporous 8 nm-thick NiO particles with an effective surface area measured by BET equal to $96 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ could deliver a capacity of $680 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $0.1 C$ after 50 cycles [511]. Rapid progress has been obtained more recently. The promising nanoflake geometry has been confirmed, since NiO nanoflakes with mean size about $2 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ and mean thickness about 20 nm showed a charge and discharge capacities of 1015 and $990 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively, after 50 cycles at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. There respective value at the 50th cycle at current rate $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ are still 568 and $578 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [512]. Nanoporous NiO films directly grown on the foam Ni were fabricated via a facile ammonia-induced route, delivering rate capacity of $280 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $10 C$ rate and high reversible capacity of $543 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at $C / 5$ rate [513].

More recently, three-dimensional "curved" hierarchical mesoporous NiO nanomembranes made using a simple fabrication technique followed by a thermal oxidation process delivered a high capacity of $721 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1.5 C$, and a long lifetime of 1400 cycles, making them attractive for high power Li-ion batteries [514]. NiO microspheres with hierarchically porous structures have been synthesized via a facile thermal decomposition of $\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{2} \cdot 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 10 h [515]. This NiO anode retained a reversible capacity of $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at a current density of $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. This is the first report on the mass production of large-surface-area hierarchical architectures for NiO microspheres.

The ensemble of porous structures and one-dimensional shape has also proved to be efficient for NiO-anodes, just as in the other anodes. Mesoporous NiO nanotubes using filter paper as the template presented a reversible capacity of $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at current rate $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [516]. Hierarchically porous NiO microtubes synthesized by a high temperature calcination of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{dmg})_{2}$ ( $\mathrm{dmg}=$ dimethyl-glyoxime) microtubes obtained by a simple precipitation method of PEG 2000 (poly(ethylene glycol)) to hollow the nanostructure delivered a capacity $\sim 640 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles at $1 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. The investigation of the rate capability revealed reversible capacities of $810,780,720,630$, and $520 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $50,200,500,1000$, and $2000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. More importantly, a discharge capacity of $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ can be recovered, while the current density back to $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. It indicates the high stability of NiO microtubes [517].

Coating NiO has not been so successful. To avoid the barrier that a uniform coat of NiO with a metal could rise for the lithium transport, a composite $\mathrm{NiO} / \mathrm{Co}-\mathrm{P}$ has been synthesized with NiO particles 200 nm in size, and 30 nm thick granular plating particles of Co-P [518]. This anode delivered the discharge and charge capacities 560 and $540 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively, after 50 cycles at current density $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. At the higher current densities of 200,500 , and $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the reversible capacities were 560,480 , and $270 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. Among $\mathrm{NiO} / \mathrm{Ni}$ composites [519-521], the best results over 50 cycles have been found on self-supported nickel-coated NiO arrays prepared by chemical bath deposition of NiO flake arrays
followed by magnetron sputtering of nickel nanoparticles [521]. For a sputtering time of 60 s , the capacity delivered after 50 cycles at current density $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was $648 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The capacity delivered at current density of 2,4 , and $7.18 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was 455,316 , and $187 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. Composites of NiO with graphene have been synthesized to increase the conductivity, and thus the rate capability. Some of these composites [521-525] did not achieved better rate capability than the best results obtained for instance in [513] on nanopous films. Recent graphene/ NiO composites, however, reached the goal. NiO-graphene sheet-on-sheet prepared by hydrothermal process delivered a capacity of $1030 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at $0.1 C$ rate, and still retained $492 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $5 C$ [526]. A better rate capability has been obtained with a $\mathrm{NiO} /$ graphene nanosheet hierarchical structure prepared by electrostatic interaction between positively charged NiO nanosheets and negatively charged graphene oxide in aqueous solution with a $\mathrm{pH}=4$ and then sintering in Ar gas atmosphere [527], still able to deliver a discharge capacity of $615 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $4000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ( 5.6 C rate). Reduced graphene oxide and nanosheetbased nickel oxide microsphere composite prepared by homogeneous coprecipitation and subsequent annealing showed a discharge capacity of $1041 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at a current of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, and a good rate capacity with $727 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current of $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, but was not tested at higher rates [528]. Very recently, a powder consisting of core-shell-structured $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{NiO}$ nanocluster-decorated graphene ( $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{NiO}$-graphene) was synthesized by the following method: first, a crumpled graphene powder consisting of uniformly distributed Ni nanoclusters was prepared by one-pot spray pyrolysis. This powder was subsequently transformed into the $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{NiO}$-graphene composite by annealing at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in air [529]. Used as an anode, this composite delivered $863 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 300 cycles at $1500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Even at a high current density of $3000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the discharge capacity of the $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{NiO}$-graphene composite powder was as high as $700 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 40 cycles. This is the best result that gives evidence of the remarkable progress achieved on such anodes since 2011, and also that the performance of the $\mathrm{NiO} /$ graphene composites depends very much on the synthesis and the architecture of the electrode. The remarkable performance of the $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{NiO}-$ graphene composite has been attributed to the role of the highly dispersed Ni nanoclusters resulting in the more complete decomposition of the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ formed during the discharging process, plus the fact that these Ni clusters acted as highly conductive pathways for electron transfer during the conversion reaction of NiO with Li . This explains the benefit obtained by the introduction of the Ni metalin Ni / NiO-graphene composites. However, the results we have mentioned on $\mathrm{NiO} /$ graphene composites without Ni nanoclusters were already very promising, which means that the NiO can be anchored strongly on the graphene. This bonding has been understood by the analysis of by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy measurements, which have given evidence of the formation of oxygen bridges originating from the pinning of hydroxyl/epoxy of a Ni adatom on oxygenated graphite in experiments performed on NiO nanosheets/graphene composites [530].

### 10.8.3 CuO

CuO has also been considered as an attractive material as anode, because it is cheap and environmentally acceptable, in addition of good safety. Its theoretical capacity is only $375 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, but fortunately, the intermediate phase $\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ always formed during charging, with a theoretical capacity of $674 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and it is thus this value that must be kept as the theoretical capacity for CuO -based anodes. However CuO , like any material for anode based on conversion reaction, suffers from a large volume expansion and dispersion of Cu particles in the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ matrix during cycling, which leads to severe mechanical strain and rapid capacity decay. In addition, CuO has a low electronic conductivity, which is damageable to charge transfer. To reduce these limitations, CuO must be prepared under the form of a nanomaterial. Different forms have been investigated. Recently, the dependence of the performance of CuO anodes on the morphology has been studied [531]. As a result, the reversible capacity of the leaf-like CuO decreases during cycling, which confirms prior results found by different groups on CuO particles with this morphology [532, 533], and such is also the case for oatmeal-like CuO . The same holds for hierarchical structures [534]. The morphologies that avoid this capacity fading are the nanowires [535] and the nanoribbon arrays [536]. The nanowires delivered a capacity of $650 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ stable over 100 cycles at $C / 2$ rate in the voltage range $0.02-3 \mathrm{~V}$. The nanoribbon arrays showed an initial reversible capacity of $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ increasing slowly $610 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ upon cycling to 275 cycles at $C / 2$ rate in the same voltage range, and the capacity still retained at current density $800 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was $332 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Many other investigations have been made on different CuO anodes, reviewed for instance in [22], but failed to give better results. For comparison, CuO micrometer/nanoflake walls prepared using molten salt synthesis could at best with the optimum choice of the synthesis temperature ( $750{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) deliver $620 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the end of the 40th cycle [537]. To compete with the performance we have reported, a composite with a conductive material is needed, in particular with graphene that is the most conductive form of carbon. Still, however, the result is not guaranteed, since some of the graphene/ CuO composites have not met this goal [538, 539], so that an optimized nanostructure design is needed even for such composites. Such a design has been proposed by Wang et al. [540], according to a synthesis process they have described in their paper. These authors obtained a basic urchin-like CuO cluster with CuO nanosheet substructure; these CuO flowers were uniformly separated by the surrounding graphene sheets. At current density $65 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, this $\mathrm{CuO} /$ graphene composite delivered a stabilized reversible capacity of $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over the 100 cycles that have been tested. The study of the rate capability has revealed that after ten cycles, this capacity decreased only slowly ten cycles; and this value was slowly lowered to 480,320 , and $150 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the current densities 320,1600 , and $6400 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, respectively. At this highest current density, the capacity was then found to be three times larger than that of graphite, while that of CuO vanishes. Another performing structure has been found by Zhou et al. who synthesized CuO hollow
nanoparticles/graphene-nanosheet composites using the Kirkendall-effect approach [541]. The reversible capacity of the material attains $640 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and the capacity retention is ca. $96 \%$ when the current density is increased ten times. At $1 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$ (ca. $1.7 C$ ), the reversible capacity reaches $485 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and remains at $281 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 500 cycles. Even better results have been obtained with a graphene nanosheet supported shuttle CuO nanostructure prepared by a facile low-temperature solution route [542]. At current density of $700 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, this composite retained a stable capacity of $826 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles. This capacity is even larger than the capacity of $771 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ obtained at a lower current density of $70 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. This higher capacity vs higher current has been also observed in other electrodes, and attributed to the fact that slow Li-ion reactions at a smaller current may lead to more serious electrode pulverization [543]. At least part of this cycling performance is attributable to the fact that the CuO nanoshuttles were distributed uniformly and wrapped well by the graphene nanosheets, another evidence of the importance of the design of the graphene/ CuO composites to obtain a performing anode. To our knowledge, these are the best results obtained with CuO -based anodes. Other $\mathrm{CuO} /$ graphene composites that have been synthesized did not have this rate capability [525, 544].

The progress made these last 5 years on CuO -based anodes, especially when combined with graphene have reached the cyclability and rate capability that make this material challenging. Nevertheless, the best results are still obtained on composites that need proving that they can be prepared at an industrial scale, and at a reasonable price. Indeed, the research on such material might now focus on this aspect. For instance the very recent synthesis of mesoporous NiO microspheres by a simple method that is more scalable than prior synthesis of hollow structures give electrochemical that are less performant than the best CuO -graphene composites, but sufficient to fulfill the requirements in terms of capacity and rate capability to make it a promising anode. This is just an illustration of the fact that further studies on CuO -based anodes will be exciting in the near future.

### 10.8.4 MnO

Manganese oxides are also considered as potential anode materials with its high theoretical capacity of $755 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and indeed, constant progress in the electrochemical properties of MnO -based anodes has been obtained in the recent years. The first results that were encouraging date from 2009, when a mixture of MnO and $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ confined to porous carbon nanofibers delivered an initial capacity of $785 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, stabilized to $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between 10 and 50 cycles at current density of $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ in the range $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$ [545]. $\mathrm{MnO} / \mathrm{C}$ nanocomposites have also been prepared through a simple thermal decomposition of manganese benzoate precursor [546]. Good results have been obtained, but at the price of a large amount of carbon ( $10-18 \mathrm{wt} \%$ ), to obtain a capacity in the range $600-680 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current rate of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. More promising, carbon-coated MnO prepared by
ball-milling with sugar an pyrolysis at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in Ar atmosphere as found to deliver a reversible capacity of $650 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}(0.08 C)$ almost stable up to 150 cycles in the range $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$, and could deliver $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $400 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [547]. Note this synthesis process is the same as the process of fabrication of some of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{LiFePO} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ cathodes presently commercialized. In this case, however, the carbon coating is performed at circa $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to obtain a better electronic conductivity of the carbon. This is then also the temperature that should be better used if it is not damageable to the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ powder. It is thus not clear whether or not the temperature of $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ used in this paper is the best choice. This same sintering temperature ( $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) was used to coat MnO nanotubes with carbon, with $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$ precursor [548], with a result that is comparable, since the capacity was nearly $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $189 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. However the capacity fading was not solved, since $18 \%$ of the capacity was lost after 25 cycles. Other carbon-coated $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ anodes where the coating was also obtained at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ [549] gave a better cyclability, but less than in case the coating has been made at higher temperature. Carbon-coating of porous MnO microspheres obtained through chemical vapor deposition at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using toluene as precursor and argon as gas carrier gave better results, as the product delivered a capacity of $\sim 700 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ almost constant over 50 cycles at a rate of $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}{ }^{-1}$; the capacity retained at $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was $400 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [550]. Another example is provided by 20 nm -thick MnO particles coated with carbon at $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, using glucose as both the source of carbon and the reducing agent [551]. This anode made from $10.7 \mathrm{wt} \%$-carbon-coated $\mathrm{MnO} / \mathrm{C}$ shows very stable cycling performance with a high reversible capacity of $939.3 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 30 cycles at $C / 10$. This electrode also shows favorable rate performance with specific capacities of $726.7,686.8,633.0$, and $587.9 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1,2,5$, and $10 C$, respectively. On another hand, carbon-coated nanorods prepared at a sintering temperature as small as $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using block copolymer F127 as carbon source has a capacity that decreases almost linearly from 800 to $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between the second and the 40th cycle at charge current rate of $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [552].

These different results are consistent with our analysis that the conductivity of the carbon layer increases with the sintering temperature and should be raised to $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ whenever it is possible. However, this is not the only pertinent parameter since the electrochemical properties depend importantly on the porosity of the particles. This is best evidenced by the good results obtained with porous carboncoated MnO nanotubes, despite the fact that the coating has been obtained at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using glucose as the precursor [553]. This porous $\mathrm{MnO} / \mathrm{C}$ nanotubes could deliver a reversible capacity as high as $763.3 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at a charge/discharge current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\left(0.13 C ; 1 C=755.6 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right)$, and $618.3 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles at a rate of $0.66 C$. One explanation might come from the investigation of the structure of the carbon layer coating $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ nanoplates [554]. The ratio of the intensities of D over G bands of the carbon in the Raman spectra was found almost constant when the temperature is raised above $550^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the range $550- 650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ so that the graphitic degree of carbon increases with temperature up to $550^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, but does not change significantly at higher temperature. Indeed, the capacity obtained with the nanoplates with a 8 nm-thick carbon layer, synthesized
at $550{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ during 10 h , was $563 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 30 cycles at a current density of $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Unfortunately, the cycling life was limited to this small number of cycles. Another example is given by the good results obtained without carbon coating on interconnected porous MnO nanoflakes on nickel foam [555]. A high reversible capacity of $568.7 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was obtained at a current density of $246 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ for the second discharge. It retained a capacity of $708.4 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the 200th charge-discharge cycle after cycling with various current densities up to $2460 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and delivered a capacity of $376 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density as high as $2460 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. This good result is due to the combination of two features. One is the beneficial effect of the porosity. Secondly, the architecture of active material grown directly on metals provides an efficient electron transport channel between the active material and the current collector of metals, which compensates for the absence of coating by conducting carbon.

Inspired by the distinctive features of natural microalgae, a biotemplating method has been developed to synthesize hollow microspheres of $\mathrm{MnO} / \mathrm{C}$, which exhibited a specific capacity of $700 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [556]. The high capacity was partially due to the porous carbon matrix. Very recently, hollow nanospheres of MnO have been synthesized, using carbon nanospheres as a template and a reagent [557]. The as-prepared MnO nanospheres were built by aggregated nanoparticles, giving a thin and porous shell. The corresponding anode delivered a reversible capacity of $1515 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 60 cycles at $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Even at $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the capacity retained after 100 cycles was still $1050 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, illustrating the performance that can be obtained when combining hollow structure, surface porosity, and nanoscale size. This also explains the very good results obtained with MnO nanocrystals embedded in carbon nanofibers ( $\mathrm{MnO} /$ CNFs ), obtained with a porous structure through an electrospinning process [558]. The as-formed $\mathrm{MnO} /$ CNFs have diameters of $100-$ 200 nm and lengths up to several millimeters. Until 100 cycles at $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the discharge capacity of these $\mathrm{MnO} /$ CNFs was as high as $1082 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with a coulombic efficiency of $99 \%$, indicating an excellent cyclic performance. Even at a high current density of $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the specific capacity is up to $575 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles. In addition, the $\mathrm{MnO} /$ CNFs electrode cycled at a high current density of $2000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ presents similar electrochemical performances, which is better than that of previously reported $\mathrm{MnO}_{2} / \mathrm{CNTs}$.

As stated before, graphene uses to be the best form of carbon to associate with an active anode element. This is confirmed also in the case of MnO . A hybrid material consisting of MnO nanocrystals grown on conductive graphene nanosheets exhibits a reversible capacity as high as $2014.1 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 150 discharge/charge cycles at $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, excellent rate capability ( $625.8 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $3000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ), and superior cyclability ( $843.3 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ even after 400 discharge/charge cycles at $2000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ with only $0.01 \%$ capacity loss per cycle) [559]. This is to our knowledge, the best performance in terms of rate capacity and cyclability obtained with MnO , which illustrates the constant progress in the synthesis of MnO -graphene composites since 2011 [560-564].

### 10.8.5 Oxides with Spinel Structure

Many of these oxides have been studied and share common features [22] illustrated in Fig. 10.10. The first discharge reaction may involve Li intercalation into the lattice (single-phase reaction) (Fig. 10.10), followed by crystal structure destruction (amorphization), and finally formation of the respective nanosized metal particles via a two-phase reaction. These three steps correspond to the regions labeled a, b, c, respectively in Fig. 10.10a.

### 10.8.5.1 $\quad \mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$

The theoretical capacity of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ according to the conversion reaction is $890 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and has thus attracted attention. In addition, this is the most stable

Fig. 10.10 Galvanostatic discharge-charge cycling curves of $A B_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\left(M M^{\prime}{ }_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right) (A=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ni}$, or Mn , $B=\mathrm{Co}$ or Fe ). (a) First cycle and (b) second cycle cycled in the voltage range $0.005-3.0 \mathrm{~V}$ at current $60 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} . A \mathrm{Co}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ with $A=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Mn}$ prepared by molten salt method at $280^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; particle size is on the order of submicrometer; $A=\mathrm{Ni}$ or Fe (urea combustion method) and $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ carbothermal reduction method. For comparison, binary spinel $\left(\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$ discharge-charge cycling curves are also shown
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_54c0b8cfb09b0b0ba3dfg-375.jpg?height=1183&width=767&top_left_y=889&top_left_x=580)

Table 10.1 Comparison of electrochemical performance of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ as anode material for LiB (from ref. [5])
| Materials | Current density ( $\mathrm{mA} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) | Initial charge capacity ( $\mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) | Initial coulombic efficiency (\%) | References |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanowires | 100 | 892 | 80 | [572] |
| Needle-like $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | 50 | 950 | 58.7 | [573] |
| $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanocages | 50 | 741 | 73.5 | [574] |
| $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanosheets | 150 | 1031 | 61 | [571] |
| Network-like $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | 100 | 1214 | 60 | [575] |


cobalt oxide, so that it is much easier to prepare it than CoO or $\mathrm{Co}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, and we can thus expect a stronger structural stability favoring the cycling life. Nevertheless, the large change of volume during the redox reaction implies the necessity of using nano-sized particles like in the case of CoO , so that many efforts have been made to prepare $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ under different nano-morphologies: nanowires [565, 566], nanotubes [567, 568], nanobelts [569], nanocapsules [570], nanosheets [571]. A comparison of the first charge and discharge capacities between different morphologies [572-575] has been discussed in ref. [575] and are summarized in Table 10.1. Among the simplest nanostructures, $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticles synthesized by the thermal decomposition of nanoparticles of cobalt-based Prussian blue analogues at $550{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ could deliver a capacity of $970 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 30 cycles at a current density of $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [574]. This capacity is larger than theoretical, a phenomenon often observed in nano-sized anode materials, owing to the additional contribution of lithium available on the surface sites, a general feature that applies to any anode material, or also the contribution of the polymeric layer that is more specific to Co -based anodes, already discussed for CoO -anodes.

The $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-graphene nanosheets have not reached the best electrochemical activities we have reviewed for CoO -graphene. The results include a capacity sweeping from $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ (second cycle) to $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 70 cycles at current rate $74 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [576], a capacity of $935 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 30 cycles at current density $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [143], a capacity of $740 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 60 cycles [577], $640 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles [578], or ca. $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at current density $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [579]. The synthesis of free-standing one-dimensional anode elements grown directly on conducting substrates in a controlled manner is an important issue to support the large changes of volume without affecting the integrity of the anode. In the particular case of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ the efforts include the synthesis of well-aligned rhombus-shaped $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanorod arrays grown directly on nickel foil via fluorine-mediated synthesis though a hydrothermal method [580]. These nanorods exhibit the combined properties of meso-porosity and quasi-single-crystalline structure, and meanwhile, exhibit robust mechanical
adhesion to the nickel foil. This anode delivered a reversible capacity of circa $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between the second and the 20th cycle at $1 C$ rate in the voltage range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$. The combined effects of porosity and crystalline structure are also evidenced by mesoporous and single-crystal $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoplates ( 30 nm in thickness, $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in width) with large surface area ( $118.6 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) and small average pore size ( 4.7 nm ) [581]. This anode delivered $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 30 cycles at $0.2 C$, corresponding to a current density of $178 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The rate capability however is limited, as this anode delivered only $750 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1 C$ rate instead of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in ref. [580]. The higher rate capability obtained in ref. [580] may be attributable to the fact that one-dimensional structure (nanorods) can better accommodate the volume change upon cycling than the geometry used in ref. [580] (plate-like particles). Coating nano- $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ has also been tried to improve the electrochemical properties. Network-like Ppy-coated $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ particles delivered a reversible capacity of circa $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ almost constant between the second and the 50 th cycle at current density $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [575].

Porous hollow architectures are well performing to accommodate the large change of volume upon cycling. The most popular method used for $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ has been the templating approach [582, 583]. However, these methods usually need strict experimental conditions or sophisticated post-treatment (such as removal of the template, selective etching in an appropriate solvent and cumbersome retreatment process), which not only introduce impurities but also increase the cost. More importantly, those methods are not suitable for large-scale production. Recently, however, a simple and scalable coordination-derived method for the synthesis of porous $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ hollow nanospheres has been found [584]. At a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the capacity of the anode prepared with these porous hollow nanospheres was maintained almost constant at circa $1100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between the 10th and the 60th cycles. Unfortunately, the capacity decreases at larger cycles: nearly $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the 80th cycle, $820 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the 100th cycle, which is still a good results, but shows the difficulty to overcome the aging of the anode. The rate performance has been investigated by measuring the capacity over ten cycles at successive current densities $100,200,400,600$, and $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. At this highest current density, the capacity was $543 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. When returning to a current density of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, however, the capacity recovered was $802 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, smaller than the 1100 mA h recorded before, so that the anode has aged significantly along the process. This fading of the capacity has been solved recently with anode built with porous $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ hexagonal nanodisks ( 20 nm in thickness, surface area $60 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) prepared with a template-free hydrothermal method [585]. After 100 cycles at current density $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ in the usual voltage range $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$, the capacity reached $1180 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, while the initial capacity was $1417 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The rate capability was investigated by measuring the capacity over 50 cycles at successive current densities $500,1000,2000,4000$, and $8000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, followed by a final return to $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. At this highest current density $8000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the capacity was still close to $300 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}{ }^{-1}$. Most of all, after returning to the current to $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, i.e. after a total of 250 cycles at these different rates, a capacity of $1086 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was restored with almost $100 \%$ coulombic efficiency.

Hierarchical urchin-like $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spheres ( $5-8 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in diameter) consisting of many nanowires and nanoparticles ( $10-50 \mathrm{~nm}$ ) also possess very good cycling stability ( $1190 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at $C / 10$ ) and excellent rate capability ( $796 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $5 C$ and $433 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $10 C$, i.e. at current density $8900 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) [586]. Moreover, the preparation is also template-free, which makes the synthesis process more scalable. Some other shapes are also promising, such as porous hollow multishelled $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ microspheres, which delivered $1616 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the 30th cycle [587], but unfortunately, the cyclability has not been explored at more cycles. We also regret that a $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanobelt array ( $20-50 \mathrm{~nm}$ in width with needle-like tips) prepared with excellent rate capability ( 530 and $320 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 30 cycles at 15 and $30 C$, respectively) has not been tested beyond 30 cycles [588].

These outstanding recent results, however, should not mask show how difficult it is to improve the cyclability of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. Capacity retention larger than $90 \%$ between the second and the 100th cycle usually does not reach $90 \%$. Apart the results we have reported above, this goal has been achieved with a graphene-encapsuled $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (amount of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ : $91.5 \mathrm{wt} \%$ ) composite obtained by co-assembly between negatively charged graphene oxide and positively charged oxide nanoparticles. The process is driven by the mutual electrostatic interactions of the two species, and is followed by chemical reduction. The resulting GE-MO possesses flexible and ultrathin graphene shells that effectively enwrap the oxide nanoparticles. This GE-MO delivered a reversible capacity of $1100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with a capacity retention of $91 \%$ between the 2nd and the 130th cycles at current density of $74 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, a result that turns out to be significantly better than the results obtained with the standard $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} /$ graphene composites where metal oxides are distributed onto the surface of graphene or between the graphene layers. We can also note that porous polyhedral and fusiform $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ powders synthesized through the hydrothermal method have shown good capacity rate and good capacity retention at the scale of 70 cycles [589]. They delivered circa $1350 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ initial discharge capacity, with capacity retention $92 \%$ at $0.1 C$ after 70 cycles. The rate capability was better with fusiform particles, in which case the spindles ( $2.0-5.0 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in length, $0.5-2.0 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in width) are composed of irregular nanoparticles ( $20-200 \mathrm{~nm}$ in diameter, $20-40 \mathrm{~nm}$ in thickness), which delivered 93.8, 90.1, and $98.9 \%$ of the second discharge capacities after 70 cycles at $0.5,1$, and $2 C$, respectively. Hierarchical heterostructures composed of anatase $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ nanofibers and secondary $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanosheets with porous surface have been synthesized [590]. As an anode, this composite delivered a high reversible capacity of $632.5 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and $95.3 \%$ capacity retention over 480 cycles. In addition, a good rate capability has been observed owing to the $\mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ component, with capacities of 475.8 and $449.5 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at current densities of 400 and $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, respectively.

To conclude, the difficulty to build a $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ with a good cycling life and good rate capability explains that this problem has been solved only recently. The major improvements the last 2 or 3 years now makes possible the fabrication of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}$ based anodes that deliver capacities the order of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $100-200$ cycles even at high rates, and most of all, some of the synthesis processes are scalable. Therefore, $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ remains a promising material as a future anode for Li -ion
batteries, while most of the reviews published on the subject few years ago were not optimistic on its future. Nevertheless, the fact that cobalt is expensive and not environmentally friendly remains a limitation.

### 10.8.5.2 $\quad \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$

$\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ has some advantages that make it a promising anode. The theoretical reversible capacity is high ( $928 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ), it is abundant as it occurs in nature as the mineral magnetite, it is nontoxic; its low cost and high corrosion resistance are other characteristics. The limits come, as usual with all the anode elements based on conversion reactions, the degradation of the battery coming from the large change of volume ( $200 \%$ ) upon cycling. In addition, it is not so simple to prepare the material free from impurity, due to the presence of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ (in addition to $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ), while iron likes to be in the III valence state, so that there is a tendency with $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ to contain $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ impurity. This is the same problem we met in the preparation of the $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$ cathode element (see Chap. 8). In the present case, however, the presence of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is not dramatic, since $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is itself a high capacity electro-active materials and undergoes a similar kind of metallic reduction i.e. conversion reaction, as we shall see in a subsequent section.

A lot of efforts have been made on the synthesis of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ composites under different forms (see ref. [591] for a review), in parallel with the same efforts made on the synthesis of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$. Without carbon coating, nanoparticles usually show important capacity fading [592] and carbon coating, or formation of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} /$ carbon composites has been considered as necessary to facilitate the charge transfer. Without carbon coat, particles 200 nm in size could still deliver $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, but the test has been carried out on 40 cycles only [593]. An intermediate situation is provided by recent experiments on clusters of mesoporous $11-12 \mathrm{~nm}$ sized nanoparticles, with carbon-coating through bottom-up self assembly approach [594]. The anode could deliver a capacity of $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 100 cycles, but still an important decrease of capacity is observed when increasing the rate. In addition, the carbon must be conducting, so that the carbon must be coated at a high temperature (larger than $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, as already discussed in the case of the chromium spinel in the previous section, or in the case of $\mathrm{LiFePO}_{4}$, as discussed in Chap. 8). For instance, carbon deposited at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ non intentionally owing to the presence of carbon in the precursors during the synthesis of monodisperse mesoporous particles was of little help [595]. Due to their high specific surface area ( $122.3 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) the initial discharge capacity is vey large ( $1307 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ), but the capacity fading remained important (capacity reduced to $450 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 110 cycles at $0.2 C$ rate).

The combination of hollow structures that accommodate more easily the changes of volume upon cycling, porosity that increases the surface contact with the electrolyte, and graphene that facilitates the electron conduction and thus the charge transfer has also been experimented on $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. Flexible free-standing (binder-less) hollow $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} /$ graphene ( $39.6 \mathrm{wt} \%$ graphene) hybrid films, fabricated
through vacuum filtration and thermal reduction processes, in which graphene formed a three-dimensional conductive network, with hollow and porous $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spindles being captured and distributed homogeneously has been fabricated. The anode exhibited a high specific capacity of 1400,940 , and $660 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles in the voltage range $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$, at current densities 100,200 , and $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, respectively [596]. Yet, no capacity loss was observed at the 50th cycle where the experiments have been stopped, which suggests excellent capacity retention over a larger number of cycles. The synthesis of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticles grafted on graphene usually requires the use of surfactants and solvents to avoid the aggregation of the particles. Their introduction inevitably makes the solid-liquid separation more complex. A new method to synthesize a $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-graphene has been proposed recently [597]. First, the precursor was synthesized through the decomposition of ferric nitrate in the presence of graphene oxide in the mixed solvent of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$-expanded ethanol. Then, the precursor was converted to the $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-graphene composite via thermal treatment in $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ atmosphere. With the help of the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}-$ expanded ethanol, $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticles were coated on the surface of GN completely and uniformly with high loading. The resulting $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-graphene composite with $25 \mathrm{wt} \%$ graphene delivered a capacity of $826 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at current density of $1 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$, almost constant after the two first cycles. At a high current density of $5 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the composite was still able to deliver a capacity of $460 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. This rate capability is better than another composite graphene nanoscroll (GNS)- $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticles, where GNS is a spirally wrapped two-dimensional (2D) graphene sheet (GS) with a 1D tubular structure wrapping $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticle [598]. Although this composite showed a very large capacity at 0.1 rate (ca. 1100 mA hafter 50 cycles), it only retained $300 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ at $5 \mathrm{Ag}^{-1}$. A peculiar nanoarchitecture obtained by coating copper nanoribbons with $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ has given very good anode properties [599]. Here, a CuO nanoribbons array (NRA) was prepared by using a one-step oxidation of Cu sheet in alkaline solution, and a Cu NRA on copper substrate was fabricated by electrochemical reduction of CuO NRA. Then, 3D nanostructured $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ was fabricated by electrodeposition of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticles on the Cu NRA. Used as an anode, this 3D-architecture delivered a reversible capacity of $870 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 280 cycles at a current density of $385 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}(0.42 C)$. The capacity retained at high rate of $9 C\left(8000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\right)$ was still $231 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$.

### 10.8.5.3 $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$

The energy density of $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ is expected to be larger than those of $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and the $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ spinels because of the lower operational voltage of $\mathrm{Mn}(1.2 \mathrm{~V})$, and the high theoretical capacity of $936 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Until recently, however, the results obtained with $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ were not competitive with the performance of the other spinel compounds, unless it entered the formation of a composite. Among the best results, spongelike nanosized $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ exhibited a stabilized reversible capacity of about $800 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 40 charge-discharge cycles at a current rate of $C / 4$ [600].

Recently, however, the interest in $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ has been renewed, following the fabrication of porous nanorods fabricated through the decomposition of MnOOH nanorods with a high BET surface area of $27.6 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and a narrow pore size distribution of 3.9 nm [601]. A specific capacity of $901.5 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was retained with long cycling stability (coulombic efficiency of $99.3 \%$ after 150 cycles) and high rate capability ( $387.5 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $2000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ). This performance is better than the $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-carbon composites, such as $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanocrystals anchored on multiwalled carbon nanotubes ( $592 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at a current density of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) [602] and $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$-ordered mesoporous carbon ( $802 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at a current density of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) [603], or $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} /$ reduced graphene oxide composite and $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4} /$ graphene-platelets composite ( 675 and $725 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles at $75 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively) [604]. The improved performance of the porous nanorods in ref. [601] illustrates the fact that the composite architecture either with graphene or with carbon nanotubes or with any other material is not the only parameter that needs to be taken into consideration, and the porosity of the active $\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanoparticles is a key parameter.

### 10.8.6 Oxides with the Corundum Structure: $\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O}_{\mathbf{3}}(\mathbf{M}=\mathbf{F e}, \mathbf{C r}, \mathbf{M n})$

### 10.8.6.1 $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

This compound is cheap material, since it naturally occurs as the mineral hematite. In addition, it is stable, since iron is trivalent, and its theoretical capacity is large as $1005 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. These features explain that a huge amount of works has been done to optimize the preparation of $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ as a potential anode for LiBs , which have been reviewed for instance in ref. [22]. We just recall here that the conversion reaction has been demonstrated by the first charge-discharge profile in the range $0-3 \mathrm{~V}$ (see for instance [605]), which indicates Li intercalation to give first $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\left(\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$ at 1.2 V vs. $\mathrm{Li}^{0} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}$, followed by amorphisation at 0.75 V by an additional consumption 4 mol of Li to yield $\mathrm{Fe}^{0}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ via the two-phase reaction. Therefore, the safety of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is much better than carbon materials due to the higher voltage plateau for Li ion lithiation, avoiding appearing of lithium dendrite. The first Li extraction profile up to 3 V indicates the conversion reaction to give FeO or $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, as shown by the voltage plateau at 2.1 V . The best results have been obtained with mesoporous particles, a general trend with materials for anodes based on conversion reactions as already shown in previous sections. As soon as in 2007, porous nanoparticles 25 nm in size were found to deliver a reversible capacity of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 100 cycles at $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ with $99 \%$ capacity retention [606]. Then, the efforts have been focused on the improvement of the rate capacity. Again focusing on the progress that has been achieved in the recent years we find that many $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ materials can now accommodate the large variation of volume upon cycling.

An effective, inexpensive, and large-scale production approach to the synthesis of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanoparticles with a favorable configuration that 5 nm iron oxide domains in diameter assembled into a mesoporous network delivered a reversible capacity of up to $1009 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ up to 230 cycles [607]. A porous $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ xerogel assembled from nanocrystalline particles ( $\sim 5 \mathrm{~nm}$ ) with abundant mesopores ( 3 nm ) via a simple hydrothermal method delivered capacities 1000,600 , and $200 \mu \mathrm{Ah} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 1000 cycles at $0.1,5$, and $10 C$ rates, respectively [608]. The capacity retained after 230 cycles at $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was still $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, showing that mesoporous $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanoparticles without a carbon matrix as a buffer still have an excellent structural stability. Electrospun $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanorods (surface area and average pore radius are $27.6 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and 15 nm , respectively) delivered a high reversible capacity of $1095 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, with $100 \%$ capacity retention after 50 cycles when cycled in the range of $0.005-3.0 \mathrm{~V}$ at a current rate of $0.05 C$, and a capacity of $765 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ is still obtainable at $2.5 C\left(1 C=1007 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\right)$ [609]. It confirms that electrospinning has emerged as a versatile and low cost method for producing long continuous porous fibers with diameters ranging from several micrometers down to a few nanometers by applying a high voltage on a polymer solution or melt. Remarkable results have also been obtained with $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanotubes showing a $102.1 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ surface area and $0.46 \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ total pore volume, although the electrochemical tests have been performed only over 30 cycles [610]. A simple synthesis method for hierarchical spheres with hollow interiors composed of ultrathin nanosheets of has also been reported [611]. These ultrathin nanosheet subunits possess a thickness of around 3.5 nm and the specific surface area of the porous hierarchical $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ spheres was $139.5 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. As a result, a reversible discharge capacity of $815 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ during the 200th cycle has been delivered at current density of $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The best results have been obtained very recently with $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ triple-shelled porous hollow microspheres synthesized using carbonaceous microsphere sacrificial templates [612]. The hollow microspheres had uniform diameters of about $1.2 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. Control over the shell thickness, porosity, and number of internal multi-shells was achieved by tuning the $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ concentration in the carbonaceous microsphere templates. The best results were obtained with average shell thickness of 35 nm containing irregularly shaped 40 nm pores. The hollow shells had a hierarchical structure and are composed of interconnected $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ grains approximately $25-30 \mathrm{~nm}$ in diameter. Under such conditions a capacity of $102 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ has been obtained, constant over the 50 cycles at current density $50 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ that have been tested. The rate capability was also very good, as the anode achieved a stable capacity of $1100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ar current density of $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. For comparison, let us report the best results obtained with $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3^{-}}$ carbon composites. Hierarchical nanostructures composed of carbon-coated $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ hollow nanohorns on carbon nanotube (CNT) backbones delivered a capacity that increases gradually from 660 to $820 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ within 100 cycles at current density of $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with a high Coulombic efficiency of around 97$98 \%$, and the reversible capacity retained at $3000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ was still $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [613]. These results are better than the results obtained with $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanoparticleloaded carbon nanofiber composites [614] and other $\gamma-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$-carbon composites
cited therein. It is obvious from the results obtained these recent years that porous $\alpha-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanoparticles, coupled with carbon or not, with a capacity in the range $400-1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over hundreds of cycles and excellent rate capability up to $5 C$ rate are very promising anode materials, especially as the material is low-cost en environmentally friendly.

### 10.8.6.2 $\quad \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

Although $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is the most stable oxide of chromium and adopts the corundum structure, the capacity fading could never be solved entirely. Even mesoporous $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ sheet consisting of 20 nm -sized particles could only deliver a capacity that decreases to ca. $400 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at current density of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, despite its high surface area of $162 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [615]. Things have improved with the help of graphene. Graphene- $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanosheets have recently been synthesized without foreign templates. $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ and graphene oxide were chosen as the oxidant and reductant templates, respectively, in a hydrothermal reaction in order to synthesize porous $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_{3}$ nanosheet precursors. The best results were obtained for the initial mass ratios of graphene oxide and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$ equal to $1: 3$. Subsequently, graphene- $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ composites with the mass ratio of graphene to $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ of 0.135 could be obtained by calcinating this precursor [616]. These graphene- $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanosheets exhibited a reversible capacity of $850 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at a current density of $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. At higher current densities of $800 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and $1.6 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the reversible capacities still remain at 630 and $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. $\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanoparticles, ranging from 10 to 20 nm , well dispersed in the matrix of mesoporous carbon composite delivered a capacity that remained at circa $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 80 cycles at current density of $50 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [617]. However, this result was obtained owing to a large concentration of porous carbon since the material contained $42 \%$ carbon and $58 \% \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.

### 10.8.6.3 $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

Its theoretical capacity ( $1018 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) is associated to the following reactions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+3 \mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \\
& 2 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+\mathrm{Mn}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{MnO}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \\
& 2 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+\mathrm{MnO}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mn}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \\
& \mathrm{Mn}+x \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} \leftrightarrow 2 x \mathrm{Li}^{+}+\mathrm{MnO}_{x}+2 x \mathrm{e}^{-}(1.0<x<1.5) .
\end{aligned}
$$

The successful synthesis of porous $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is recent. In 2011, two kinds of precursor morphologies, oval-shaped and straw-sheaf-shaped, have been selectively prepared by hydrothermal treatment of different functional polyol molecules (oval-shape
with fructose and straw-sheaf-shape with $\alpha$-cyclodextrin) and potassium permanganate; the morphologies were conserved after decomposition of the precursors [618]. Among them, straw-sheaf-shaped $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ gave the best results, delivering a specific capacity of $550 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}, 180 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. After 36 cycles, however, the capacity recovered at $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ had decreased to $430 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The next year, porous microspheres obtained by decomposition of $\mathrm{MnCO}_{3}$ precursor delivered $796 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles [619]. An improved control of the porosity of the microsphere by adjusting the temperature at which the $\mathrm{MnCO}_{3}$ precursor has been sintered led to much better results. The best result was obtained at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, in which case the BET surface area was $28.3 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, with the pore diameter 23.6 nm [620]. At the fourth cycle at current density at $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ the discharge capacity was $529 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, remaining at $524 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 200 cycles. At high current density of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, the capacity was still $125 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, stable over 1000 cycles. Porous $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ microspheres were also mixed with carbon in $\mathrm{wt} \%$ ratios $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ /carbon/VDF of 60:20:20 to improve the rate capability [621]. This anode delivered $470 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 70 cycles at current density $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. Very recently, porous $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ nanoplates with a surface area of $21.6 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ have been prepared by a polyol solution method and post-annealing treatment [622]. As an anode, this material retained a capacity of $814 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at current density of $100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. At a current density of $2000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, the retained capacity was $38 \%$ of the theoretical value, i.e. $387 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Despite some progress since 2 years, the $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is not competitive with the other Mn oxides as a cathode element. Another obstacle experienced with Mn -based anodes (including $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ we did not consider here) is some lack of reproducibility in the results that have been obtained even when the same synthetic methods were used to prepare the Pn-based anode materials, pointed out recently in a review devoted to them [623].

### 10.8.7 Dioxides

Many other oxides have been explored, will less success so far. This is the case with dioxides $M \mathrm{O}_{2}(M=\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Mo}, \mathrm{Ru})$, reviewed in ref. [22]. Among them, the best results have been obtained with $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$. Indeed, this compound crystallizes in a distorted rutile structure, with a resulting ban structure that makes it a good electrical conductor (the resistivity is $8.8 \times 10^{-5} \Omega \mathrm{~cm}$ for bulk $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ at room temperature), at contrast will all the other anode materials (except carbon) that we have envisioned so far. We can also mention its good chemical stability, its theoretical capacity ( $838 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ), and its affordable cost. However, the problem of low rate capability and rather poor capacity retention has never been solved, unless the $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ particles are nano-sized, and are mixed with carbon. For instance, $\mathrm{MnO}_{2} /$ carbon nanowires delivered an almost constant capacity of $350 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ up to 20 cycles at a current density of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [624]. Carbon-coated $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ nanospheres ( $60-80 \mathrm{~nm}$ ) delivered an initial capacity of $670 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1 C$ rate in
the voltage range $0.1-3 \mathrm{~V}$, but most of all retained $90 \%$ of it after 30 cycles [625]. In these hybrids, and more complex ones like carbon decorated $\mathrm{WO}_{x}-\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ nanorods [626], the good capacity retention and rate capability is attributable to the carbon providing an elastic matrix for absorbing the change of volume during cycling, and preventing the agglomeration of the particles. $\mathrm{MoO}_{2} /$ graphene showed a capacity of $550 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 1000 cycles at current density of $540 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ in the voltage range $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$ [627]. More recently, $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$-ordered mesoporous carbon ( $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$-OMC) has been prepared through a two-step low-temperature solvothermal chemical reaction route, using SBA-15 as a hard template and sucrose as the carbon source [628]. For a content of $45 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{MoO}$, which is the optimized case, a reversible capacity as high as $1049 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ even after 50 cycles at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ has been reached, much larger than the theoretical capacity of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}\left(838 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\right) \cdot$ At a high current density of $1600 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}, \mathrm{MoO}_{2}-\mathrm{OMC}$ still retains an excellent cyclic performance of $600 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles. This is one order of magnitude larger than that of OMC ( $54 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ). The outstanding performance of the $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$-OMC composite has been attributed to the OMC that acts as branches to connect the $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ nanoparticules and build up a network to ensure the good electrical contact. Meanwhile, the open channels of the ordered mesoporous OMC with $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ nanoparticles modified on the surface allow sufficient infiltration of electrolyte and provide fast diffusion channels of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$the $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ nanoparticles. These results are comparable with other conversion reaction-based anodes.

### 10.9 Ternary Metal Oxides with Spinel Structure

Since $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ adopt the inverse spinel structure, the substitution of Co or Fe has also been explored. Among them, $\mathrm{NiFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nano fibers synthesized by an electrospinning approach exhibit a higher charge-storage capacity of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ even after 100 cycles with high Coulombic efficiency of $100 \%$ between 10 and 100 cycles at the current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ in the voltage range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$ [629]. This is by far the best result obtained with this compound. Unfortunately, the electrochemical properties of this anode have not been explored at higher current densities. For comparison, $\mathrm{NiFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} /$ single-wall carbon nanotube (SWNT) composites with $70 \mathrm{wt} \%$ loading ratio exhibited a reversible capacity of $776 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ over 55 cycles under the same conditions [630]. Porous $\mathrm{CoFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}+20 \%$ reduced graphene oxide, synthesized by a solvothermal process, exhibited a capacity of $1040 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $C / 10\left(91 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\right)$, very stable along the 30 cycles that have been tested [631]. Even at the very high rate of $20 C$, the discharge capacity was still $380 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. Even without graphene support, very good results have been obtained with porous $\mathrm{CoFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanosheets, having the thickness of $30-60 \mathrm{~nm}$ and lateral size of several microns with numerous penetrating pores, synthesized via thermal decomposition of $\left(\mathrm{CoFe}_{2}\right)_{1 / 3} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ nanosheets [632]. At current densities 1000 and $2000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the anode delivered capacities of 806 and
$648 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively, each for 200 cycles. These results show the superior behavior of $\mathrm{CoFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ with respect to $\mathrm{NiFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, and renew the interest in this spinel compound as a promising anode material. In addition, these nanosheets have been synthesized at a sintering temperature that does not exceed $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, while the obtention of a good capacity retention for $\mathrm{CoFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\left(740 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}\right.$ after 75 cycles at 1 C rate) required a sintering temperature as high as $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in prior works [633]. This gain in the sintering temperature makes the synthesis more scalable for industrial processes.

### 10.9.1 Molybdenum Compounds

Among molybdenum-containing mixed oxides that adopt the Scheelite structure, the best results have been obtained with $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$. Interconnected networks of $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$ submicrometer particles prepared by thermolysis of polymer matrix based metal precursor solution exhibited a high reversible capacity of $990 \pm 10 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $100 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, with $100 \%$ capacity retention between 5 and 50 cycles [634]. Hierarchically porous 3D electrode of $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$ prepared by the hydrothermal method resulted in the formation of a $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$ network composed of interconnected porous nanosheets with a lateral length of $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ and a thickness of $8-10 \mathrm{~nm}$, each nanosheet consisting of many interconnected nanocrystallites with grain size around 5 nm and voids around $2-$ 4 nm [635]. This structure achieved a discharge capacity of $1063 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and a coulombic efficiency of $100 \%$ at the second and second cycles at a current density of $0.1 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}\left(C / 10\right.$; where $\left.1 C=980 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}{ }^{-1}\right)$ between 0.005 and 3.0 V , and remained steady over 100 cycles. Even at high rates of 2 and $3 C$, the $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$ electrode can still achieve high capacities of 460 and $327 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, respectively. After 100 cycles at $0.3 C$, the reversible discharge capacity was $894 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, which corresponds to a capacity retention of $87.6 \%$ of the initial capacity. When the current density was increased to $0.5 \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$ electrode delivered a capacity of $758 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at the end of 100 cycles, corresponding to $75.7 \%$ retention. These results show that $\mathrm{CoMoO}_{4}$ submicrometer particles with interconnected network like morphology makes it promising as a high capacity anode material for LiBs , as Co and Mo are mutually beneficial elements to buffer the volume change upon Li cycling.

### 10.9.2 Oxide Bronzes

Molybdenum trioxide $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ is also considered as a promising anode material, due to its layer structure and its high theoretical capacity of $1117 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ by the conversion reaction, assuming 6Li can participate. The interest in this material has been raised by the good performance of nanospheroids of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}(5-20 \mathrm{~nm}$ in size),
prepared by the hotwire chemical vapor deposition [636]. The electrodes were prepared by electrophoretic deposition of these nanospheres onto stainless steel substrates to give $2-\mu \mathrm{m}$ thick films followed by annealing at $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in air to obtain porous crystalline $\alpha-\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$. The electrode was then coated with a solid polymer electrolyte PEO- $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$. The investigation of the electrochemical properties upon cycling at $C / 10$ in the range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$ revealed that the capacity was stabilized to $630 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between the 30th and the 150th cycles. Since this compound has a poor electronic conductivity, many efforts have been made to fabricate nanoparticles under different forms: wire arrays [637], hollow nanospheres [638], nanobelts [639], and/or by coating the nanoparticles with a conductive layer. Coating the $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ electrode with four monolayers of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ raises the capacity to $900 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ up to the 50th cycle at the rate $C / 2$ [640]. More recently, carbon coated $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ nanobelts maintained its capacity of $1064 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 50 cycles at a rate of $C / 10[641]$. The nanobelts, having a diameter of 150 nm and a length of $5-8 \mu \mathrm{~m}$, ware prepared by a simple hydrothermal route. It is interesting to note that the carbon coating was done using malic acid as the carbon precursor, dispersed in toluene. The malic acid is decomposed at a temperature of $265^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is sufficient to coat the material with amorphous carbon. The comparison of the electrochemical properties before and after carbon coating gives evidence of major improvements associated with the carbon-coating, which can be considered as the proof that the carbon layer is a good electronic conductor despite the very low sintering temperature, while higher synthesis temperatures are needed with other precursors, as we have already discussed in previous sections. Like in the case of all the anodes, porosity is important to increase the performance of $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$. Recently, a porous $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ film was prepared by using a facile hydrothermal route and subsequent calcinations in air. This material had a high capacity ( $750 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at $1 C$ ), long cycle life ( 120 cycles with $80 \%$ capacity retention) [642]. Recently, a $\mathrm{MoO}_{3} /$ graphene composite electrode ( $1: 1$ in weight ratio) delivered discharge capacities of 1437, 967,688 , and $574 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for the 1st, 2nd, 50th, and 100th cycles at a current density of $500 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, respectively, with a coulombic efficiency of $97 \%$ from the second cycle. The electrode still delivered the capacity of $513 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ even at a high current density of $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ [642]. Taking into account that $1 C=1117 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the rate capability is still smaller than that of the porous $\mathrm{MoO}_{3}$ film in [643] without the help of graphite, again pointing to the important effect of the porosity in the performance of the electrodes.

### 10.9.3 $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{Mo}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8}$

Numerous well-defined Mo triangular cluster oxide compounds exist and have been considered as possible anode materials (see ref. [22] for a review). However, the only one that has a rate capability and capacity competitive with the other materials we have considered above is $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{Mo}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8}$. Hierarchically nanostructured $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{Mo}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8}$-graphene composites ( $10.3 \mathrm{wt} \%$ carbon) consisting of graphene-wrapped
secondary microspheres ( $3-5 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ ) synthesized by a two-step reduction method delivered a capacity that increases from 650 to $921 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ between the second and the 20th cycles at a current density of $200 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}{ }^{-1}$. The capacity reached $950 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after the 40 th cycle. The rate capability was also very good, as the reversible capacity at was $671 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ at a current density of $1500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ [644]. However, this work dates from 2011, and no further study has been reported on this compound to our knowledge. This is at contrast with the situation met with the other compounds mentioned in this paper, where important progress has been achieved owing to the intensive efforts made in the years 2011-2014.

### 10.10 Anodes Based on Both Alloying and Conversion Reaction

Different materials in which a Li alloying-dealloying element is added to a conversion of transition metal oxide have been investigated, hoping that the addition of the two effects would improve the electrochemical properties. The most promising materials entering this category are oxides with spinel structure.

### 10.10.1 $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$

These are not the only spinel that belongs to this family, which include $\mathrm{CdFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$, but Cd is toxic. Since the electrochemical properties of $\mathrm{CdFe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ are not better than those of $\mathrm{Zn} M_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(M=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Fe})$ that are more friendly for the environment, only the Zn -based spinels will be considered here. The reactions that govern the electrochemical properties are written hereunder for $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and will also apply with Co replaced by Fe :

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}+x \mathrm{Li}^{+}+x \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{x}\left(\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right), \\
\mathrm{Li}_{x}\left(\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)+(8-x) \mathrm{Li}^{+}+(8-x) \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{Co}+4 \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \\
\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{Li}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{ZnLi}(\text { alloying } / \text { de-alloying reaction }), \\
\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{Co}+3 \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 / 3 \mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}+4 / 3 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+4 / 3 \mathrm{e}^{-} .
\end{gathered}
$$

A reversible capacity corresponding to 8.3 mol of Li per mole of $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (ca. $900 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ ) is expected, assuming $x \leq 0.5$ for Li intercalation into the spinel lattice (Eq. 10.16), crystal structure destruction, followed by metal particle formation, and alloy formation with Zn (Eqs. 10.17 and 10.18). The conversion reactions occur during charging (Li extraction, Eq. 10.19). Part of the CoO can also form $\mathrm{Co}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ during the charging process (Eq. 10.19). The theoretical capacity has been
achieved with nanosized $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(20 \mathrm{~nm})$ prepared by the urea combustion technique, stable up to at least 60 cycles at a current density of $60 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1} (0.07 \mathrm{C})$ in the range $0.005-3 \mathrm{~V}$ [645]. Starting with $\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4} \cdot 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$ precursors, this stable capacity was raised to $957 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ for $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ prepared by the molten salt method at $280^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in air [646]. However, neither these works nor other nano/microstructures including nanoparticles [647], nanowires [648], nanotubes [649] could meet the rate capability and cyclability needed to be competitive. Again, one had to wait for the synthesis of porous nanoparticles to obtain significant improvements, with the results of uniform mesoporous $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ microspheres with surrounding nanoparticles (surface area of $26.8 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ and a pore volume of $0.12 \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, pore size in the range $2-10 \mathrm{~nm}$ ) and microspheres [650]. The capacity maintained at $721 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 80 discharge-charge cycles. Even as current density reached to $1000 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$, the initial specific capacity still showed $937 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ and the discharge capacity of $432 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ was retained after 40 cycles.

A binder-free flexible anode has been fabricated with hierarchical 3D- $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ nanowire arrays grown on a carbon cloth by using a hydrothermal route [651]. This anode delivered charge-discharge capacities in the range of about 1200$1340 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with $99 \%$ capacity retention from 3 to 160 cycles at current density of $200 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. The capacity decreased from 1200, 920, 890, 710, and $605 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ with increasing $C$-rate ranging from $0.2,0.5,1,2$, to $5 C$ ( $1 C=900 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ). The capacity was then reversibly back to $105 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ once the charging/discharging rate was set back to $C / 5$ again, revealing that almost 92 \% of the initial capacity at $C / 5$ was recovered. This anode can be used in many applications such as stretchable/bendable electronic devices, portable energy storage devices, flexible powering sustainable vehicles, photovoltaic devices, and control of commercial available LED and displays.

A facile two-step strategy involving a polyol method and subsequent thermal annealing treatment has been successfully developed for the large-scale preparation of $\mathrm{ZnCo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ various hierarchical micro/nanostructures (twin microspheres and microcubes) without surfactant assistance [652]. For the twin microspheres, the surface area was $7.33 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ with a relatively narrow pore size distribution ranging from 30 to 80 nm . The pore volume is determined to be $0.0449 \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. The relatively low pore volume is mainly contributed form the small mesopores between the homogeneous nanometer-sized building blocks. After 50 and 100 cycles at current density of $500 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ in the voltage range $0.01-3 \mathrm{~V}$, the discharge capacities were the same at $1100 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$. At a current of $1000 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, the capacity was $1145 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$, and still $831.7 \mathrm{mAh} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ after 100 cycles with a coulombic efficiency of $99 \%$ after the second cycle.
